title: 4DEquine: Disentangling Motion and Appearance for 4D Equine Reconstruction from Monocular Video
summary: 4D reconstruction of equine family (e.g. horses) from monocular video is important for animal welfare. Previous mainstream 4D animal reconstruction methods require joint optimization of motion and appearance over a whole video, which is time-consuming and sensitive to incomplete observation. In this work, we propose a novel framework called 4DEquine by disentangling the 4D reconstruction problem into two sub-problems: dynamic motion reconstruction and static appearance reconstruction. For motion, we introduce a simple yet effective spatio-temporal transformer with a post-optimization stage to regress smooth and pixel-aligned pose and shape sequences from video. For appearance, we design a novel feed-forward network that reconstructs a high-fidelity, animatable 3D Gaussian avatar from as few as a single image. To assist training, we create a large-scale synthetic motion dataset, VarenPoser, which features high-quality surface motions and diverse camera trajectories, as well as a synthetic appearance dataset, VarenTex, comprising realistic multi-view images generated through multi-view diffusion. While training only on synthetic datasets, 4DEquine achieves state-of-the-art performance on real-world APT36K and AiM datasets, demonstrating the superiority of 4DEquine and our new datasets for both geometry and appearance reconstruction. Comprehensive ablation studies validate the effectiveness of both the motion and appearance reconstruction network. Project page: https://luoxue-star.github.io/4DEquine_Project_Page/.

AI.tldr: 4DEquine disentangles motion and appearance to reconstruct 4D equine subjects from monocular video, using a spatio-temporal transformer for smooth, pixel-aligned motion and a feed-forward network to build an animatable 3D Gaussian avatar from as little as one image; trained only on new synthetic datasets (VarenPoser, VarenTex), it achieves state-of-the-art on real APT36K and AiM.
AI.motivation: Conventional 4D animal reconstruction jointly optimizes motion and appearance over entire videos, which is slow and fragile under incomplete observations. For animal welfare applications, a faster, more robust approach is needed.
AI.method: Disentangle 4D reconstruction into two sub-problems: (1) Motion: a simple spatio-temporal transformer regresses pose/shape sequences with a post-optimization step for smooth, pixel-aligned results. (2) Appearance: a feed-forward network builds a high-fidelity, animatable 3D Gaussian avatar from as few as a single image. Training relies on two synthetic datasets: VarenPoser (high-quality surface motions, diverse camera paths) and VarenTex (realistic multi-view images via multi-view diffusion).
AI.result: Without real-data training, the system reaches state-of-the-art performance on real-world APT36K and AiM benchmarks for geometry and appearance; ablations verify the contributions of both motion and appearance modules.
AI.conclusion: Separating motion and appearance, combined with high-quality synthetic supervision, yields efficient and robust 4D equine reconstruction. The newly proposed datasets and pipeline generalize well to real data and offer a practical path for animal-focused 4D capture.

title: HG-Lane: High-Fidelity Generation of Lane Scenes under Adverse Weather and Lighting Conditions without Re-annotation
summary: Lane detection is a crucial task in autonomous driving, as it helps ensure the safe operation of vehicles. However, existing datasets such as CULane and TuSimple contain relatively limited data under extreme weather conditions, including rain, snow, and fog. As a result, detection models trained on these datasets often become unreliable in such environments, which may lead to serious safety-critical failures on the road. To address this issue, we propose HG-Lane, a High-fidelity Generation framework for Lane Scenes under adverse weather and lighting conditions without requiring re-annotation. Based on this framework, we further construct a benchmark that includes adverse weather and lighting scenarios, containing 30,000 images. Experimental results demonstrate that our method consistently and significantly improves the performance of existing lane detection networks. For example, using the state-of-the-art CLRNet, the overall mF1 score on our benchmark increases by 20.87 percent. The F1@50 score for the overall, normal, snow, rain, fog, night, and dusk categories increases by 19.75 percent, 8.63 percent, 38.8 percent, 14.96 percent, 26.84 percent, 21.5 percent, and 12.04 percent, respectively. The code and dataset are available at: https://github.com/zdc233/HG-Lane.

AI.tldr: HG-Lane proposes a high-fidelity adverse-weather and low-light scene generation framework that augments lane-detection data without re-annotation, builds a 30k-image benchmark, and markedly boosts state-of-the-art detectors (e.g., +20.87 mF1 on CLRNet) across rain/snow/fog/night/dusk.
AI.motivation: Current lane-detection datasets (e.g., CULane, TuSimple) lack sufficient extreme-weather and challenging lighting samples. Models trained on them degrade in rain, snow, fog, or night, creating safety-critical risks. There is a need for scalable robustness improvements without the cost of collecting and re-annotating large adverse-condition datasets.
AI.method: Introduce a High-fidelity Generation framework (HG-Lane) that transforms existing lane images into realistic adverse weather and lighting conditions while preserving lane annotations, avoiding re-labeling. Using this pipeline, construct a 30,000-image benchmark covering multiple conditions (snow, rain, fog, night, dusk, normal). Train existing lane-detection networks on the augmented data to evaluate robustness gains.
AI.result: Consistent and significant performance gains across models; with CLRNet, overall mF1 improves by 20.87%. F1@50 increases: overall +19.75%, normal +8.63%, snow +38.8%, rain +14.96%, fog +26.84%, night +21.5%, dusk +12.04%.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic, high-fidelity adverse-condition augmentation can substantially enhance lane-detection robustness without re-annotation. HG-Lane and its 30k-image benchmark offer a practical path to safer deployment; code and dataset are publicly available (https://github.com/zdc233/HG-Lane).

title: Unbalanced Optimal Transport Dictionary Learning for Unsupervised Hyperspectral Image Clustering
summary: Hyperspectral images capture vast amounts of high-dimensional spectral information about a scene, making labeling an intensive task that is resistant to out-of-the-box statistical methods. Unsupervised learning of clusters allows for automated segmentation of the scene, enabling a more rapid understanding of the image. Partitioning the spectral information contained within the data via dictionary learning in Wasserstein space has proven an effective method for unsupervised clustering. However, this approach requires balancing the spectral profiles of the data, blurring the classes, and sacrificing robustness to outliers and noise. In this paper, we suggest improving this approach by utilizing unbalanced Wasserstein barycenters to learn a lower-dimensional representation of the underlying data. The deployment of spectral clustering on the learned representation results in an effective approach for the unsupervised learning of labels.

AI.tldr: They propose an unsupervised HSI clustering pipeline that learns a low‑dimensional representation via unbalanced Wasserstein barycenters and then applies spectral clustering, avoiding class‑blurring and improving robustness compared to balanced Wasserstein dictionary learning.
AI.motivation: Labeling hyperspectral images is costly and difficult due to high spectral dimensionality. Prior unsupervised approaches based on dictionary learning in Wasserstein space require balancing spectral profiles, which blurs class distinctions and reduces robustness to outliers/noise.
AI.method: Replace balanced optimal‑transport dictionary learning with unbalanced Wasserstein barycenters to learn a compact spectral representation; perform spectral clustering on this representation to obtain labels/segments.
AI.result: The approach is reported to be effective for unsupervised labeling/segmentation of HSI, addressing class blurring and improving robustness to outliers and noise relative to balanced Wasserstein methods (no quantitative results provided in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: Unbalanced Wasserstein barycenters provide a better basis for learning low‑dimensional HSI representations, and when coupled with spectral clustering yield a practical, robust, and automated unsupervised segmentation method that mitigates the need for manual labels.

title: Video-Based Reward Modeling for Computer-Use Agents
summary: Computer-using agents (CUAs) are becoming increasingly capable; however, it remains difficult to scale evaluation of whether a trajectory truly fulfills a user instruction. In this work, we study reward modeling from execution video: a sequence of keyframes from an agent trajectory that is independent of the agent's internal reasoning or actions. Although video-execution modeling is method-agnostic, it presents key challenges, including highly redundant layouts and subtle, localized cues that determine success. We introduce Execution Video Reward 53k (ExeVR-53k), a dataset of 53k high-quality video--task--reward triplets. We further propose adversarial instruction translation to synthesize negative samples with step-level annotations. To enable learning from long, high-resolution execution videos, we design spatiotemporal token pruning, which removes homogeneous regions and persistent tokens while preserving decisive UI changes. Building on these components, we fine-tune an Execution Video Reward Model (ExeVRM) that takes only a user instruction and a video-execution sequence to predict task success. Our ExeVRM 8B achieves 84.7% accuracy and 87.7% recall on video-execution assessment, outperforming strong proprietary models such as GPT-5.2 and Gemini-3 Pro across Ubuntu, macOS, Windows, and Android, while providing more precise temporal attribution. These results show that video-execution reward modeling can serve as a scalable, model-agnostic evaluator for CUAs.

AI.tldr: They introduce a model-agnostic way to judge whether computer-using agents completed tasks by reading only execution videos (keyframes) plus the instruction, enabled by a new 53k-sample dataset and a pruning mechanism, and show strong cross-OS accuracy exceeding leading baselines.
AI.motivation: Evaluating if an agent’s on-computer trajectory truly satisfies a user instruction is hard to scale and often entangled with the agent’s internal reasoning or logs. A method-agnostic, scalable evaluator that only looks at what happened on screen would make benchmarking, training, and safety supervision more robust and comparable across agents and platforms.
AI.method: 1) Curate ExeVR-53k: video–task–reward triplets of execution keyframes. 2) Generate hard negatives via adversarial instruction translation with step-level annotations. 3) Introduce spatiotemporal token pruning to discard redundant visual regions/persistent tokens and preserve decisive UI changes. 4) Fine-tune an Execution Video Reward Model (ExeVRM) that takes the user instruction and execution video to predict success and provide temporal attribution.
AI.result: ExeVRM 8B attains 84.7% accuracy and 87.7% recall on video-execution assessment and reportedly outperforms proprietary baselines (e.g., GPT-5.2, Gemini-3 Pro) across Ubuntu, macOS, Windows, and Android, while offering finer temporal attribution.
AI.conclusion: Video-execution reward modeling is a practical, scalable, and model-agnostic evaluator for computer-using agents. With a large dataset, adversarial negative synthesis, and spatiotemporal pruning, ExeVRM can reliably judge task success across operating systems and may serve as a standard evaluation component for CUAs.

title: GhazalBench: Usage-Grounded Evaluation of LLMs on Persian Ghazals
summary: Persian poetry plays an active role in Iranian cultural practice, where verses by canonical poets such as Hafez are frequently quoted, paraphrased, or completed from partial cues. Supporting such interactions requires language models to engage not only with poetic meaning but also with culturally entrenched surface form. We introduce GhazalBench, a benchmark for evaluating how large language models (LLMs) interact with Persian ghazals under usage-grounded conditions. GhazalBench assesses two complementary abilities: producing faithful prose paraphrases of couplets and accessing canonical verses under varying semantic and formal cues. Across several proprietary and open-weight multilingual LLMs, we observe a consistent dissociation: models generally capture poetic meaning but struggle with exact verse recall in completion-based settings, while recognition-based tasks substantially reduce this gap. A parallel evaluation on English sonnets shows markedly higher recall performance, suggesting that these limitations are tied to differences in training exposure rather than inherent architectural constraints. Our findings highlight the need for evaluation frameworks that jointly assess meaning, form, and cue-dependent access to culturally significant texts. GhazalBench is available at https://github.com/kalhorghazal/GhazalBench.

AI.tldr: GhazalBench evaluates how LLMs handle Persian ghazals via paraphrasing and verse-access tasks, finding they capture meaning but struggle with exact verse recall—especially in completion—while recognition cues help; English sonnet results suggest exposure, not architecture, drives the gap.
AI.motivation: Persian poetry is central to Iranian cultural practice, where people routinely quote or complete canonical verses. LLMs must therefore handle both poetic meaning and culturally entrenched surface form, but existing evaluations underrepresent this need.
AI.method: Introduce GhazalBench with two task families: (1) faithful prose paraphrasing of ghazal couplets (meaning) and (2) access to canonical verses under varying semantic and formal cues (form/recall). Evaluate multiple proprietary and open-weight multilingual LLMs; run a parallel evaluation on English sonnets for comparison.
AI.result: Models generally paraphrase meaning well but perform poorly at exact verse recall in completion-based settings; recognition-based tasks reduce the deficit. On English sonnets, recall is markedly better, indicating the shortfall likely stems from differences in training exposure rather than architectural limits.
AI.conclusion: Effective cultural-text evaluation must jointly assess meaning, form, and cue-dependent retrieval. GhazalBench provides such a framework and reveals current LLMs’ recall limitations on Persian ghazals; the benchmark is publicly available for further research.

title: Agentic Control Center for Data Product Optimization
summary: Data products enable end users to gain greater insights about their data by providing supporting assets, such as example question-SQL pairs which can be answered using the data or views over the database tables. However, producing useful data products is challenging, and typically requires domain experts to hand-craft supporting assets. We propose a system that automates data product improvement through specialized AI agents operating in a continuous optimization loop. By surfacing questions, monitoring multi-dimensional quality metrics, and supporting human-in-the-loop controls, it transforms data into observable and refinable assets that balance automation with trust and oversight.

AI.tldr: Proposes an AI-agent–driven, continuous optimization system that auto-generates and refines data product assets (e.g., question–SQL pairs, views) using multi-dimensional quality metrics with human-in-the-loop oversight to make data more observable and improvable.
AI.motivation: Creating useful data products requires labor-intensive, expert-crafted supporting assets; teams need scalable ways to enhance usability and insight while maintaining trust and governance.
AI.method: Orchestrated specialized AI agents in a continuous improvement loop that: surface questions, generate/curate supporting assets (e.g., SQL, views), monitor multi-dimensional quality metrics, and incorporate human feedback/controls for iterative refinement.
AI.result: Introduces a system that balances automation with oversight, turning datasets into observable, refinable assets; claims improved quality and coverage through metrics-driven iteration, though no quantitative benchmarks are provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Agentic, metrics-aware, human-in-the-loop automation can sustainably scale and improve data products while preserving trust and governance, offering a framework for ongoing optimization of data assets.

title: Delta-K: Boosting Multi-Instance Generation via Cross-Attention Augmentation
summary: While Diffusion Models excel in text-to-image synthesis, they often suffer from concept omission when synthesizing complex multi-instance scenes. Existing training-free methods attempt to resolve this by rescaling attention maps, which merely exacerbates unstructured noise without establishing coherent semantic representations. To address this, we propose Delta-K, a backbone-agnostic and plug-and-play inference framework that tackles omission by operating directly in the shared cross-attention Key space. Specifically, with Vision-language model, we extract a differential key $ΔK$ that encodes the semantic signature of missing concepts. This signal is then injected during the early semantic planning stage of the diffusion process. Governed by a dynamically optimized scheduling mechanism, Delta-K grounds diffuse noise into stable structural anchors while preserving existing concepts. Extensive experiments demonstrate the generality of our approach: Delta-K consistently improves compositional alignment across both modern DiT models and classical U-Net architectures, without requiring spatial masks, additional training, or architectural modifications.

AI.tldr: Delta-K is a training-free, plug-and-play inference method that injects a differential cross-attention key signal to fix missing concepts in text-to-image diffusion, improving multi-object compositionality across architectures without masks or retraining.
AI.motivation: Text-to-image diffusion models often omit requested concepts in complex multi-instance prompts. Prior training-free fixes rescale attention maps, which increases unstructured noise and fails to form coherent, grounded semantics.
AI.method: Operate directly in the shared cross-attention Key space. Use a vision-language model to extract a differential key ΔK that encodes the semantic signature of missing concepts. Inject ΔK during early diffusion steps (semantic planning) under a dynamically optimized schedule to ground noise into stable structural anchors while preserving already-present concepts. The approach is backbone-agnostic and plug-and-play at inference.
AI.result: Across extensive experiments, Delta-K consistently improves compositional alignment and reduces concept omission for both modern DiT backbones and classical U-Nets. It requires no spatial masks, no additional training, and no architectural changes.
AI.conclusion: Manipulating cross-attention keys with a VLM-derived differential signal is an effective, general, and practical remedy for concept omission in diffusion-based T2I. Early, scheduled ΔK injection stabilizes structure and preserves existing content, yielding better compositional fidelity across architectures.

title: Large Language Models and Book Summarization: Reading or Remembering, Which Is Better?
summary: Summarization is a core task in Natural Language Processing (NLP). Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) and the introduction of large context windows reaching millions of tokens make it possible to process entire books in a single prompt. At the same time, for well-known books, LLMs can generate summaries based only on internal knowledge acquired during training. This raises several important questions: How do summaries generated from internal memory compare to those derived from the full text? Does prior knowledge influence summaries even when the model is given the book as input? In this work, we conduct an experimental evaluation of book summarization with state-of-the-art LLMs. We compare summaries of well-known books produced using (i) only the internal knowledge of the model and (ii) the full text of the book. The results show that having the full text provides more detailed summaries in general, but some books have better scores for the internal knowledge summaries. This puts into question the capabilities of models to perform summarization of long texts, as information learned during training can outperform summarization of the full text in some cases.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates whether LLMs summarize books more effectively from their internal knowledge or from the books’ full text; surprisingly, internal-memory summaries sometimes score better, challenging assumptions about long-context understanding.
AI.motivation: Large context windows make whole-book summarization feasible, but widely known books may be memorized by LLMs. The authors seek to quantify how prior knowledge vs. provided text affects summary quality and whether models genuinely leverage long inputs.
AI.method: Experimental comparison on well-known books across two settings: (i) summaries generated purely from the model’s internal knowledge and (ii) summaries generated when the entire book is supplied in-context. Quality is compared using evaluation scores (details unspecified in the abstract).
AI.result: Access to the full text generally yields more detailed summaries, yet for some titles, summaries based solely on internal knowledge obtain higher scores than those derived from the full text.
AI.conclusion: LLMs’ training-time knowledge can overshadow or even outperform long-context summarization in certain cases, questioning the reliability of long-input processing and motivating methods to disentangle and evaluate grounding vs. memorization.

title: Hybrid Self-evolving Structured Memory for GUI Agents
summary: The remarkable progress of vision-language models (VLMs) has enabled GUI agents to interact with computers in a human-like manner. Yet real-world computer-use tasks remain difficult due to long-horizon workflows, diverse interfaces, and frequent intermediate errors. Prior work equips agents with external memory built from large collections of trajectories, but relies on flat retrieval over discrete summaries or continuous embeddings, falling short of the structured organization and self-evolving characteristics of human memory. Inspired by the brain, we propose Hybrid Self-evolving Structured Memory (HyMEM), a graph-based memory that couples discrete high-level symbolic nodes with continuous trajectory embeddings. HyMEM maintains a graph structure to support multi-hop retrieval, self-evolution via node update operations, and on-the-fly working-memory refreshing during inference. Extensive experiments show that HyMEM consistently improves open-source GUI agents, enabling 7B/8B backbones to match or surpass strong closed-source models; notably, it boosts Qwen2.5-VL-7B by +22.5% and outperforms Gemini2.5-Pro-Vision and GPT-4o.

AI.tldr: HyMEM is a brain-inspired hybrid structured memory for GUI agents that combines a graph of symbolic nodes with continuous trajectory embeddings, enabling multi-hop retrieval, self-evolving updates, and working-memory refreshing; it substantially boosts open-source VLM agents (e.g., +22.5% on Qwen2.5-VL-7B) and can surpass closed-source systems like Gemini2.5-Pro-Vision and GPT-4o.
AI.motivation: Real-world computer-use tasks are long-horizon, interface-diverse, and error-prone. Existing external memories rely on flat retrieval over discrete summaries or continuous embeddings, lacking structured organization and adaptive, self-evolving properties akin to human memory, limiting robustness and generalization for GUI agents.
AI.method: Introduce Hybrid Self-evolving Structured Memory (HyMEM): a graph-based memory coupling discrete high-level symbolic nodes with continuous trajectory embeddings. The graph supports multi-hop retrieval to compose knowledge, self-evolution via node update operations, and on-the-fly working-memory refreshing during inference, integrated into VLM-based GUI agents.
AI.result: Across extensive experiments, HyMEM consistently improves open-source GUI agents, enabling 7B/8B models to match or outperform strong closed-source baselines; specifically, it raises Qwen2.5-VL-7B performance by +22.5% and achieves better results than Gemini2.5-Pro-Vision and GPT-4o on reported benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid, structured, and self-evolving memory meaningfully enhances GUI agent reliability and efficiency on complex computer-use tasks, narrowing or surpassing the gap with closed-source systems; memory organization and dynamic updating are key to scaling VLM agents in real-world interfaces.

title: FusionNet: a frame interpolation network for 4D heart models
comment: This is the authors' version. The final authenticated version is available online at https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-031-47425-5_4. Published in Medical Image Computing and Computer Assisted Intervention - MICCAI 2023 Workshops
summary: Cardiac magnetic resonance (CMR) imaging is widely used to visualise cardiac motion and diagnose heart disease. However, standard CMR imaging requires patients to lie still in a confined space inside a loud machine for 40-60 min, which increases patient discomfort. In addition, shorter scan times decrease either or both the temporal and spatial resolutions of cardiac motion, and thus, the diagnostic accuracy of the procedure. Of these, we focus on reduced temporal resolution and propose a neural network called FusionNet to obtain four-dimensional (4D) cardiac motion with high temporal resolution from CMR images captured in a short period of time. The model estimates intermediate 3D heart shapes based on adjacent shapes. The results of an experimental evaluation of the proposed FusionNet model showed that it achieved a performance of over 0.897 in terms of the Dice coefficient, confirming that it can recover shapes more precisely than existing methods. This code is available at: https://github.com/smiyauchi199/FusionNet.git

AI.tldr: FusionNet is a neural network that reconstructs high–temporal-resolution 4D cardiac motion from shorter CMR scans by estimating intermediate 3D heart shapes between observed frames, outperforming prior methods with Dice > 0.897.
AI.motivation: Standard CMR requires long, uncomfortable scans; shortening scans typically sacrifices temporal (and/or spatial) resolution, which can reduce diagnostic accuracy. The work targets the loss of temporal resolution specifically.
AI.method: A temporal-shape interpolation approach: FusionNet takes adjacent 3D cardiac shapes from short-duration CMR and predicts intermediate 3D shapes to form a high-temporal-resolution 4D sequence.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation shows Dice coefficient > 0.897 and better shape recovery than existing methods.
AI.conclusion: FusionNet can recover accurate 4D cardiac motion from shorter scans, potentially maintaining diagnostic fidelity while improving patient comfort; code is released for reproducibility.

title: AraModernBERT: Transtokenized Initialization and Long-Context Encoder Modeling for Arabic
comment: 9 pages, 1 figure. Accepted at AbjadNLP Workshop, EACL 2026
summary: Encoder-only transformer models remain widely used for discriminative NLP tasks, yet recent architectural advances have largely focused on English. In this work, we present AraModernBERT, an adaptation of the ModernBERT encoder architecture to Arabic, and study the impact of transtokenized embedding initialization and native long-context modeling up to 8,192 tokens. We show that transtokenization is essential for Arabic language modeling, yielding dramatic improvements in masked language modeling performance compared to non-transtokenized initialization. We further demonstrate that AraModernBERT supports stable and effective long-context modeling, achieving improved intrinsic language modeling performance at extended sequence lengths. Downstream evaluations on Arabic natural language understanding tasks, including inference, offensive language detection, question-question similarity, and named entity recognition, confirm strong transfer to discriminative and sequence labeling settings. Our results highlight practical considerations for adapting modern encoder architectures to Arabic and other languages written in Arabic-derived scripts.

AI.tldr: AraModernBERT adapts the ModernBERT encoder to Arabic, showing that transtokenized embedding initialization is crucial and that native 8k-token context yields stable training and better masked language modeling, with gains transferring to multiple Arabic NLU tasks.
AI.motivation: Encoder-only transformers remain central for discriminative NLP, but most architectural advances target English. Arabic (and Arabic-derived scripts) pose tokenization and morphology challenges, and many real-world tasks benefit from longer context. The work investigates practical choices needed to port a modern English-centric encoder to Arabic effectively.
AI.method: Adapt the ModernBERT encoder architecture to Arabic; compare embedding initialization strategies with and without transtokenization when changing tokenization; equip the model with native long-context capacity up to 8,192 tokens; evaluate intrinsic masked language modeling and extrinsic performance on Arabic NLU tasks (inference, offensive language detection, question-question similarity) and sequence labeling (NER).
AI.result: Transtokenized embedding initialization yields large improvements in masked language modeling over non-transtokenized initialization. The model trains stably with extended context and improves intrinsic language modeling at long sequence lengths. These benefits transfer to downstream Arabic NLU and NER benchmarks, indicating strong discriminative and sequence-labeling performance.
AI.conclusion: Transtokenization is a key ingredient for effective Arabic adaptation of modern encoders, and native long-context modeling up to 8k tokens is stable and beneficial. The findings provide practical guidance for adapting contemporary encoder architectures to Arabic and other Arabic-script languages.

title: HEAL: Hindsight Entropy-Assisted Learning for Reasoning Distillation
summary: Distilling reasoning capabilities from Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) into smaller models is typically constrained by the limitation of rejection sampling. Standard methods treat the teacher as a static filter, discarding complex "corner-case" problems where the teacher fails to explore valid solutions independently, thereby creating an artificial "Teacher Ceiling" for the student. In this work, we propose Hindsight Entropy-Assisted Learning (HEAL), an RL-free framework designed to bridge this reasoning gap. Drawing on the educational theory of the Zone of Proximal Development(ZPD), HEAL synergizes three core modules: (1) Guided Entropy-Assisted Repair (GEAR), an active intervention mechanism that detects critical reasoning breakpoints via entropy dynamics and injects targeted hindsight hints to repair broken trajectories; (2) Perplexity-Uncertainty Ratio Estimator (PURE), a rigorous filtering protocol that decouples genuine cognitive breakthroughs from spurious shortcuts; and (3) Progressive Answer-guided Curriculum Evolution (PACE), a three-stage distillation strategy that organizes training from foundational alignment to frontier breakthrough. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that HEAL significantly outperforms traditional SFT distillation and other baselines.

AI.tldr: HEAL is an RL-free distillation framework that repairs failing reasoning traces with entropy-guided hindsight hints, filters out spurious solutions via a perplexity–uncertainty ratio, and trains with a progressive curriculum—surpassing standard SFT distillation and other baselines while mitigating the “teacher ceiling.”
AI.motivation: Distilling strong reasoning from large reasoning models into smaller models is limited by rejection-sampling style supervision: teachers act as static filters, discard hard corner cases, and impose a “teacher ceiling” when they themselves cannot explore valid solutions. The goal is to let students learn from difficult problems beyond what the teacher can solve unaided.
AI.method: HEAL combines three modules, inspired by the Zone of Proximal Development: (1) GEAR detects high-entropy breakpoints in a reasoning trajectory and injects targeted hindsight hints to repair and continue the path; (2) PURE filters training signals using a perplexity–uncertainty ratio to separate genuine reasoning breakthroughs from shortcutting; (3) PACE applies a three-stage curriculum that moves from basic alignment to repaired reasoning and then to frontier problems, enabling progressive capability growth. The approach is RL-free.
AI.result: Across multiple reasoning benchmarks, HEAL yields significant gains over standard SFT-based distillation and other baselines (no specific numbers reported in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: Targeted, entropy-driven repair plus rigorous filtering and a progressive curriculum can overcome the teacher ceiling without RL, enabling smaller models to acquire stronger reasoning from complex examples the teacher cannot solve unaided.

title: An Automated Radiomics Framework for Postoperative Survival Prediction in Colorectal Liver Metastases using Preoperative MRI
summary: While colorectal liver metastasis (CRLM) is potentially curable via hepatectomy, patient outcomes remain highly heterogeneous. Postoperative survival prediction is necessary to avoid non-beneficial surgeries and guide personalized therapy. In this study, we present an automated AI-based framework for postoperative CRLM survival prediction using pre- and post-contrast MRI. We performed a retrospective study of 227 CRLM patients who had gadoxetate-enhanced MRI prior to curative-intent hepatectomy between 2013 and 2020. We developed a survival prediction framework comprising an anatomy-aware segmentation pipeline followed by a radiomics pipeline. The segmentation pipeline learns liver, CRLMs, and spleen segmentation from partially-annotated data, leveraging promptable foundation models to generate pseudo-labels. To support this pipeline, we propose SAMONAI, a prompt propagation algorithm that extends Segment Anything Model to 3D point-based segmentation. Predicted pre- and post-contrast segmentations are then fed into our radiomics pipeline, which extracts per-tumor features and predicts survival using SurvAMINN, an autoencoder-based multiple instance neural network for time-to-event survival prediction. SurvAMINN jointly learns dimensionality reduction and survival prediction from right-censored data, emphasizing high-risk metastases. We compared our framework against established methods and biomarkers using univariate and multivariate Cox regression. Our segmentation pipeline achieves median Dice scores of 0.96 (liver) and 0.93 (spleen), driving a CRLM segmentation Dice score of 0.78 and a detection F1-score of 0.79. Accurate segmentation enables our radiomics pipeline to achieve a survival prediction C-index of 0.69. Our results show the potential of integrating segmentation algorithms with radiomics-based survival analysis to deliver accurate and automated CRLM outcome prediction.

AI.tldr: Retrospective study (n=227) introduces a fully automated MRI-based pipeline that segments liver, spleen, and colorectal liver metastases using a SAM-derived 3D prompt propagation method (SAMONAI), then performs per-lesion radiomics with a multiple-instance, autoencoder survival model (SurvAMINN). Achieves strong organ segmentation (Dice 0.96 liver, 0.93 spleen), moderate CRLM performance (Dice 0.78; F1 0.79), and a postoperative survival C-index of 0.69, outperforming conventional baselines, suggesting clinical utility for risk stratification before hepatectomy.
AI.motivation: Post-hepatectomy outcomes in CRLM are heterogeneous; accurate preoperative survival prediction could avoid non-beneficial surgery and tailor therapy. Existing biomarkers and manual workflows are limited; there is a need for scalable, automated models that leverage modern foundation models and can learn from partially annotated data.
AI.method: Retrospective cohort of 227 CRLM patients with gadoxetate-enhanced preoperative MRI (2013–2020). A two-stage framework: (1) Anatomy-aware segmentation leveraging promptable foundation models with pseudo-labels; proposes SAMONAI to extend Segment Anything to 3D point-based segmentation for liver, metastases, and spleen across pre/post-contrast phases. (2) Radiomics survival analysis using SurvAMINN, an autoencoder-based multiple-instance neural network trained on right-censored data to learn per-tumor representations and emphasize high-risk lesions. Evaluation via univariate/multivariate Cox comparisons.
AI.result: Segmentation median Dice: liver 0.96, spleen 0.93; CRLM Dice 0.78 with detection F1 0.79. Using predicted segmentations as input, the radiomics survival model attained a C-index of 0.69 for postoperative survival and compared favorably to established methods/biomarkers in Cox analyses.
AI.conclusion: Automated integration of foundation-model-aided segmentation with lesion-level radiomics and MIL-based survival modeling is feasible and yields moderate accuracy for CRLM postoperative survival prediction. The pipeline shows promise for preoperative risk stratification and personalized management, warranting external and prospective validation.

title: An Efficient Hybrid Deep Learning Approach for Detecting Online Abusive Language
summary: The digital age has expanded social media and online forums, allowing free expression for nearly 45% of the global population. Yet, it has also fueled online harassment, bullying, and harmful behaviors like hate speech and toxic comments across social networks, messaging apps, and gaming communities. Studies show 65% of parents notice hostile online behavior, and one-third of adolescents in mobile games experience bullying. A substantial volume of abusive content is generated and shared daily, not only on the surface web but also within dark web forums. Creators of abusive comments often employ specific words or coded phrases to evade detection and conceal their intentions. To address these challenges, we propose a hybrid deep learning model that integrates BERT, CNN, and LSTM architectures with a ReLU activation function to detect abusive language across multiple online platforms, including YouTube comments, online forum discussions, and dark web posts. The model demonstrates strong performance on a diverse and imbalanced dataset containing 77,620 abusive and 272,214 non-abusive text samples (ratio 1:3.5), achieving approximately 99% across evaluation metrics such as Precision, Recall, Accuracy, F1-score, and AUC. This approach effectively captures semantic, contextual, and sequential patterns in text, enabling robust detection of abusive content even in highly skewed datasets, as encountered in real-world scenarios.

AI.tldr: Proposes a hybrid BERT+CNN+LSTM model with ReLU to detect abusive language across platforms (YouTube, forums, dark web), reporting ~99% on Precision, Recall, Accuracy, F1, and AUC on an imbalanced dataset (1:3.5 abusive:non-abusive).
AI.motivation: Online platforms have seen a surge in harassment, bullying, hate speech, and coded abusive expressions; existing detectors struggle with evasive phrasing and skewed real-world class distributions.
AI.method: Integrates BERT for semantic/contextual encoding with CNN and LSTM layers to capture local n-grams and long-range sequential patterns; trained on a large, heterogeneous corpus spanning social media, forums, and dark web posts; uses ReLU activation.
AI.result: On 349,834 texts (77,620 abusive; 272,214 non-abusive), the model achieves approximately 99% across Precision, Recall, Accuracy, F1, and AUC, indicating strong performance despite class imbalance.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid architecture effectively models semantic and sequential cues, enabling robust abusive-language detection on highly skewed, multi-platform data; suitable for real-world moderation scenarios.

title: Beyond Scalars: Evaluating and Understanding LLM Reasoning via Geometric Progress and Stability
summary: Evaluating LLM reliability via scalar probabilities often fails to capture the structural dynamics of reasoning. We introduce TRACED, a framework that assesses reasoning quality through theoretically grounded geometric kinematics. By decomposing reasoning traces into Progress (displacement) and Stability (curvature), we reveal a distinct topological divergence: correct reasoning manifests as high-progress, stable trajectories, whereas hallucinations are characterized by low-progress, unstable patterns (stalled displacement with high curvature fluctuations). Leveraging these signatures, our probabilistic framework achieves competitive performance and superior robustness across diverse benchmarks. Crucially, TRACED bridges geometry and cognition by mapping high curvature to ''Hesitation Loops'' and displacement to ''Certainty Accumulation'', offering a physical lens to decode the internal dynamics of machine thought.

AI.tldr: TRACED evaluates LLM reasoning using geometric kinematics of traces, decomposing them into Progress (displacement) and Stability (curvature) to distinguish correct, stable reasoning from hallucination-like, unstable stalling; it yields competitive, robust reliability assessment and offers interpretable cognitive analogies.
AI.motivation: Scalar probabilities miss the structural, temporal dynamics of chain-of-thought reasoning, leading to unreliable detection of correctness or hallucinations; the authors seek a principled alternative that captures trajectory-level behavior.
AI.method: Model each reasoning trace as a geometric trajectory and compute two kinematic features: Progress (net displacement) as certainty accumulation and Stability (curvature) as local directional changes/hesitation. Identify topological signatures (high-progress/low-curvature vs low-progress/high-curvature) and build a probabilistic classifier using these features to assess reasoning quality.
AI.result: The two-feature geometry reveals clear divergence between correct and hallucinated traces. The resulting probabilistic framework is reported to be competitive with state-of-the-art and more robust across diverse benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Geometric analysis provides an interpretable, physically motivated lens on machine reasoning, mapping curvature to hesitation loops and displacement to certainty buildup; TRACED offers a robust, theoretically grounded alternative to scalar probability-based reliability measures.

title: Robotic Ultrasound Makes CBCT Alive
summary: Intraoperative Cone Beam Computed Tomography (CBCT) provides a reliable 3D anatomical context essential for interventional planning. However, its static nature fails to provide continuous monitoring of soft-tissue deformations induced by respiration, probe pressure, and surgical manipulation, leading to navigation discrepancies. We propose a deformation-aware CBCT updating framework that leverages robotic ultrasound as a dynamic proxy to infer tissue motion and update static CBCT slices in real time. Starting from calibration-initialized alignment with linear correlation of linear combination (LC2)-based rigid refinement, our method establishes accurate multimodal correspondence. To capture intraoperative dynamics, we introduce the ultrasound correlation UNet (USCorUNet), a lightweight network trained with optical flow-guided supervision to learn deformation-aware correlation representations, enabling accurate, real-time dense deformation field estimation from ultrasound streams. The inferred deformation is spatially regularized and transferred to the CBCT reference to produce deformation-consistent visualizations without repeated radiation exposure. We validate the proposed approach through deformation estimation and ultrasound-guided CBCT updating experiments. Results demonstrate real-time end-to-end CBCT slice updating and physically plausible deformation estimation, enabling dynamic refinement of static CBCT guidance during robotic ultrasound-assisted interventions. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/anonymous-codebase/us-cbct-demo.

AI.tldr: Real-time, deformation-aware updating of static CBCT guidance using robotic ultrasound: a lightweight correlation U-Net estimates dense tissue motion from US streams, rigidly aligned to CBCT with LC2, and warps CBCT slices without extra radiation.
AI.motivation: Static intraoperative CBCT cannot track soft-tissue motion from respiration, probe pressure, and manipulation, causing navigation errors; repeatedly reacquiring CBCT adds radiation and delays. A dynamic, low-radiation way to maintain CBCT-to-patient alignment is needed.
AI.method: 1) Initialize US–CBCT alignment via calibration and refine with LC2-based rigid registration. 2) Introduce USCorUNet, a lightweight network trained with optical flow–guided supervision to learn deformation-aware correlation features and infer dense deformation fields from live US. 3) Spatially regularize the deformation and transfer it to the CBCT reference to update CBCT slices in real time, avoiding repeat scans.
AI.result: Experiments on deformation estimation and US-guided CBCT updating show real-time end-to-end slice updates with physically plausible, accurate deformation fields.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables dynamic, radiation-free refinement of static CBCT guidance during robotic ultrasound-assisted interventions, improving intraoperative navigation; source code is publicly available.

title: The Dunning-Kruger Effect in Large Language Models: An Empirical Study of Confidence Calibration
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities across diverse tasks, yet their ability to accurately assess their own confidence remains poorly understood. We present an empirical study investigating whether LLMs exhibit patterns reminiscent of the Dunning-Kruger effect -- a cognitive bias where individuals with limited competence tend to overestimate their abilities. We evaluate four state-of-the-art models (Claude Haiku 4.5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Kimi K2) across four benchmark datasets totaling 24,000 experimental trials. Our results reveal striking calibration differences: Kimi K2 exhibits severe overconfidence with an Expected Calibration Error (ECE) of 0.726 despite only 23.3% accuracy, while Claude Haiku 4.5 achieves the best calibration (ECE = 0.122) with 75.4% accuracy. These findings demonstrate that poorly performing models display markedly higher overconfidence -- a pattern analogous to the Dunning-Kruger effect in human cognition. We discuss implications for safe deployment of LLMs in high-stakes applications.

AI.tldr: Empirical study of LLM confidence calibration across four models and four datasets (24k trials) shows large differences: a low-accuracy model (Kimi K2) is severely overconfident (ECE 0.726, 23.3% accuracy) while Claude Haiku 4.5 is best calibrated (ECE 0.122, 75.4% accuracy), suggesting a Dunning–Kruger-like pattern.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used in settings where reliable uncertainty estimates matter, yet their self-confidence may be miscalibrated. The work probes whether LLMs systematically overestimate their correctness—especially at low competence—analogous to the Dunning–Kruger effect, and what this means for safe deployment.
AI.method: Evaluate four recent LLMs (Claude Haiku 4.5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Kimi K2) on four benchmarks totaling 24,000 trials; elicit model answers and confidences; compute accuracy and Expected Calibration Error (ECE); compare calibration patterns against performance.
AI.result: Marked calibration disparities: Kimi K2 shows severe overconfidence with ECE 0.726 despite 23.3% accuracy; Claude Haiku 4.5 has the best calibration with ECE 0.122 and 75.4% accuracy. Generally, poorer-performing models are more overconfident.
AI.conclusion: LLM calibration varies widely; lower-performing models tend to be more overconfident, echoing a Dunning–Kruger-like effect. Measuring and improving calibration is critical for safe, high-stakes deployment.

title: Verbalizing LLM's Higher-order Uncertainty via Imprecise Probabilities
summary: Despite the growing demand for eliciting uncertainty from large language models (LLMs), empirical evidence suggests that LLM behavior is not always adequately captured by the elicitation techniques developed under the classical probabilistic uncertainty framework. This mismatch leads to systematic failure modes, particularly in settings that involve ambiguous question-answering, in-context learning, and self-reflection. To address this, we propose novel prompt-based uncertainty elicitation techniques grounded in \emph{imprecise probabilities}, a principled framework for repesenting and eliciting higher-order uncertainty. Here, first-order uncertainty captures uncertainty over possible responses to a prompt, while second-order uncertainty (uncertainty about uncertainty) quantifies indeterminacy in the underlying probability model itself. We introduce general-purpose prompting and post-processing procedures to directly elicit and quantify both orders of uncertainty, and demonstrate their effectiveness across diverse settings. Our approach enables more faithful uncertainty reporting from LLMs, improving credibility and supporting downstream decision-making.

AI.tldr: They propose prompt-based procedures, grounded in imprecise probabilities, to elicit both first-order (over answers) and second-order (over models of uncertainty) uncertainty from LLMs, showing more faithful and useful uncertainty reporting across ambiguous QA, in-context learning, and self-reflection tasks.
AI.motivation: Classical, single-probability (precise) uncertainty elicitation often mischaracterizes LLM behavior, producing systematic failures—especially under ambiguity, in-context learning dynamics, and self-reflection—so a richer framework is needed.
AI.method: Introduce general-purpose prompting templates and post-processing that directly elicit imprecise probabilities (e.g., ranges or sets) to quantify first-order and second-order uncertainty. The approach operationalizes higher-order uncertainty by having LLMs expose indeterminacy in their probability models rather than forcing precise point estimates.
AI.result: Across diverse settings, the techniques successfully elicit both orders of uncertainty and produce uncertainty reports that align better with model behavior, improving faithfulness and robustness compared to classical elicitation methods.
AI.conclusion: Imprecise-probability-based prompting enables more credible and decision-useful uncertainty from LLMs; capturing second-order uncertainty is key to mitigating failure modes seen with traditional, precise probabilistic elicitation.

title: OilSAM2: Memory-Augmented SAM2 for Scalable SAR Oil Spill Detection
summary: Segmenting oil spills from Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) imagery remains challenging due to severe appearance variability, scale heterogeneity, and the absence of temporal continuity in real world monitoring scenarios. While foundation models such as Segment Anything (SAM) enable prompt driven segmentation, existing SAM based approaches operate on single images and cannot effectively reuse information across scenes. Memory augmented variants (e.g., SAM2) further assume temporal coherence, making them prone to semantic drift when applied to unordered SAR image collections. We propose OilSAM2, a memory augmented segmentation framework tailored for unordered SAR oil spill monitoring. OilSAM2 introduces a hierarchical feature aware multi scale memory bank that explicitly models texture, structure, and semantic level representations, enabling robust cross image information reuse. To mitigate memory drift, we further propose a structure semantic consistent memory update strategy that selectively refreshes memory based on semantic discrepancy and structural variation.Experiments on two public SAR oil spill datasets demonstrate that OilSAM2 achieves state of the art segmentation performance, delivering stable and accurate results under noisy SAR monitoring scenarios. The source code is available at https://github.com/Chenshuaiyu1120/OILSAM2.

AI.tldr: OilSAM2 is a memory-augmented, SAM-based framework that segments oil spills in unordered SAR image collections using a hierarchical multi-scale, feature-aware memory and a drift-resistant update strategy, achieving state-of-the-art performance on two public datasets.
AI.motivation: Oil spill segmentation in SAR is difficult due to large appearance variability, scale heterogeneity, noise, and lack of temporal continuity. Existing SAM methods are single-image and cannot reuse cross-scene information; memory-augmented variants like SAM2 assume temporal order and drift semantically on unordered collections. A method is needed to reuse information across unordered scenes without drift.
AI.method: Introduce OilSAM2: (1) a hierarchical, feature-aware, multi-scale memory bank that explicitly encodes texture-, structure-, and semantic-level representations to enable robust cross-image information reuse; (2) a structure–semantic consistent memory update that selectively refreshes memory based on semantic discrepancy and structural variation to curb memory drift. Built on prompt-driven SAM-style segmentation but tailored for unordered SAR monitoring.
AI.result: On two public SAR oil spill datasets, OilSAM2 attains state-of-the-art segmentation accuracy and delivers stable results under noisy monitoring conditions.
AI.conclusion: A specialized memory design and drift-aware update allow SAM-style segmentation to generalize across unordered SAR scenes, improving robustness and accuracy for oil spill monitoring. Code is publicly available at the provided GitHub link.

title: Quantifying Hallucinations in Language Language Models on Medical Textbooks
summary: Hallucinations, the tendency for large language models to provide responses with factually incorrect and unsupported claims, is a serious problem within natural language processing for which we do not yet have an effective solution to mitigate against. Existing benchmarks for medical QA rarely evaluate this behavior against a fixed evidence source. We ask how often hallucinations occur on textbook-grounded QA and how responses to medical QA prompts vary across models. We conduct two experiments: the first experiment to determine the prevalence of hallucinations for a prominent open source large language model (LLaMA-70B-Instruct) in medical QA given novel prompts, and the second experiment to determine the prevalence of hallucinations and clinician preference to model responses. We observed, in experiment one, with the passages provided, LLaMA-70B-Instruct hallucinated in 19.7\% of answers (95\% CI 18.6 to 20.7) even though 98.8\% of prompt responses received maximal plausibility, and observed in experiment two, across models, lower hallucination rates aligned with higher usefulness scores ($ρ=-0.71$, $p=0.058$). Clinicians produced high agreement (quadratic weighted $κ=0.92$) and ($τ_b=0.06$ to $0.18$, $κ=0.57$ to $0.61$) for experiments 1 and ,2 respectively

AI.tldr: They evaluate hallucinations in textbook-grounded medical QA, finding LLaMA‑70B‑Instruct hallucinates ~20% of the time despite highly plausible answers; across models, fewer hallucinations track with higher clinician-perceived usefulness, with strong annotator agreement.
AI.motivation: Medical QA systems can sound plausible yet be unfaithful to evidence, posing safety risks. Existing benchmarks rarely test hallucinations against a fixed, authoritative evidence source (e.g., textbooks). The paper asks how often hallucinations occur in evidence-grounded settings and how this varies across models and relates to clinical usefulness.
AI.method: Textbook-grounded QA with clinician adjudication. Two experiments: (1) Measure hallucination prevalence for LLaMA‑70B‑Instruct on novel prompts with provided passages; also rate plausibility. (2) Compare multiple models’ hallucination rates and clinician preferences/usefulness; assess correlation between hallucination and usefulness; compute inter-rater agreement (quadratic weighted κ, Kendall’s τ_b).
AI.result: Experiment 1: LLaMA‑70B‑Instruct hallucinated in 19.7% of answers (95% CI 18.6–20.7) while 98.8% of responses were rated maximally plausible. Experiment 2: Across models, lower hallucination rates aligned with higher usefulness (ρ = −0.71, p = 0.058). Clinician agreement was high: κ = 0.92 (exp. 1); and τ_b = 0.06–0.18, κ = 0.57–0.61 (exp. 2).
AI.conclusion: Even when grounded in textbook passages, LLMs can produce plausible but unfaithful medical answers. Faithfulness (low hallucination) tends to improve perceived usefulness. Reliable clinician adjudication is feasible. Results underscore the need for evidence-grounded evaluation and mitigation methods to reduce hallucinations in medical QA.

title: Resource-constrained Amazons chess decision framework integrating large language models and graph attention
comment: 20 pages, 15 figures. Supported by the National Key Research and Development Project of China (No. 2020YFA0714300), NSFC (No. 61833005, 12061088), the Open Project of Key Laboratory of Transport Industry of Comprehensive Transportation Theory (Nanjing Modern Multimodal Transportation Laboratory) (MTF2023004), and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2024T170129, GZC20240261)
summary: Artificial intelligence has advanced significantly through the development of intelligent game-playing systems, providing rigorous testbeds for decision-making, strategic planning, and adaptive learning. However, resource-constrained environments pose critical challenges, as conventional deep learning methods heavily rely on extensive datasets and computational resources. In this paper, we propose a lightweight hybrid framework for the Game of the Amazons, which explores the paradigm of weak-to-strong generalization by integrating the structural reasoning of graph-based learning with the generative capabilities of large language models. Specifically, we leverage a Graph Attention Autoencoder to inform a multi-step Monte Carlo Tree Search, utilize a Stochastic Graph Genetic Algorithm to optimize evaluation signals, and harness GPT-4o-mini to generate synthetic training data. Unlike traditional approaches that rely on expert demonstrations, our framework learns from noisy and imperfect supervision. We demonstrate that the Graph Attention mechanism effectively functions as a structural filter, denoising the LLM's outputs. Experiments on a 10$\times$10 Amazons board show that our hybrid approach not only achieves a 15\%--56\% improvement in decision accuracy over baselines but also significantly outperforms its teacher model (GPT-4o-mini), achieving a competitive win rate of 45.0\% at N=30 nodes and a decisive 66.5\% at only N=50 nodes. These results verify the feasibility of evolving specialized, high-performance game AI from general-purpose foundation models under stringent computational constraints.

AI.tldr: Lightweight hybrid game AI for Amazons that fuses graph attention, MCTS, and LLM-generated data to learn from noisy supervision, denoise it structurally, and achieve sizable accuracy and win-rate gains under tight compute.
AI.motivation: Conventional deep learning for games needs large data and compute, which is impractical in resource-constrained settings. The authors seek a method that can bootstrap strong play from weak, noisy supervision (LLM outputs) and limited search, testing weak-to-strong generalization for a complex combinatorial board game.
AI.method: Combine (1) GPT-4o-mini to synthesize imperfect training data, (2) a Graph Attention Autoencoder to encode board states and act as a structural denoiser and evaluator, (3) a multi-step Monte Carlo Tree Search guided by the learned representations, and (4) a Stochastic Graph Genetic Algorithm to optimize evaluation signals. Training eschews expert demonstrations and learns from noisy labels; evaluation is on 10×10 Amazons with varying MCTS node budgets.
AI.result: Graph attention serves as an effective structural filter, improving decision accuracy by 15–56% over baselines and surpassing the teacher (GPT-4o-mini). Reported win rates: 45.0% at N=30 nodes and 66.5% at N=50 nodes, indicating strong performance under low search budgets.
AI.conclusion: A graph+LLM hybrid can produce specialized, high-performance agents from general-purpose models in compute-limited regimes, validating weak-to-strong generalization with noisy supervision for the Game of the Amazons.

title: Why Does It Look There? Structured Explanations for Image Classification
summary: Deep learning models achieve remarkable predictive performance, yet their black-box nature limits transparency and trustworthiness. Although numerous explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) methods have been proposed, they primarily provide saliency maps or concepts (i.e., unstructured interpretability). Existing approaches often rely on auxiliary models (\eg, GPT, CLIP) to describe model behavior, thereby compromising faithfulness to the original models. We propose Interpretability to Explainability (I2X), a framework that builds structured explanations directly from unstructured interpretability by quantifying progress at selected checkpoints during training using prototypes extracted from post-hoc XAI methods (e.g., GradCAM). I2X answers the question of "why does it look there" by providing a structured view of both intra- and inter-class decision making during training. Experiments on MNIST and CIFAR10 demonstrate effectiveness of I2X to reveal prototype-based inference process of various image classification models. Moreover, we demonstrate that I2X can be used to improve predictions across different model architectures and datasets: we can identify uncertain prototypes recognized by I2X and then use targeted perturbation of samples that allows fine-tuning to ultimately improve accuracy. Thus, I2X not only faithfully explains model behavior but also provides a practical approach to guide optimization toward desired targets.

AI.tldr: I2X converts unstructured saliency signals into structured, prototype-based explanations tracked over training, yielding faithful insights into intra- and inter-class decisions and enabling accuracy gains via targeted perturbation and fine-tuning (shown on MNIST and CIFAR10).
AI.motivation: Deep models are accurate but opaque; most XAI gives unstructured saliency/concepts and sometimes depends on auxiliary models (e.g., GPT/CLIP), undermining faithfulness. A structured, model-faithful, and actionable explanation is needed to both understand and improve models.
AI.method: Extract class/region prototypes from post-hoc XAI (e.g., Grad-CAM) at multiple training checkpoints; quantify their evolution to form a structured view of intra- and inter-class decision-making. Use the identified uncertain prototypes to guide targeted sample perturbations, then fine-tune models to improve predictions. Validated on various image classifiers for MNIST and CIFAR10.
AI.result: I2X reveals a prototype-based inference process and answers “why does it look there,” improves transparency across architectures, and, by focusing on uncertain prototypes and applying targeted perturbations plus fine-tuning, improves accuracy across models and datasets (no exact numbers provided).
AI.conclusion: I2X bridges interpretability and explainability by producing faithful, structured explanations without relying on auxiliary models, and turns these explanations into practical guidance for optimizing model performance across datasets and architectures.

title: Evolving Demonstration Optimization for Chain-of-Thought Feature Transformation
summary: Feature Transformation (FT) is a core data-centric AI task that improves feature space quality to advance downstream predictive performance. However, discovering effective transformations remains challenging due to the large space of feature-operator combinations. Existing solutions rely on discrete search or latent generation, but they are frequently limited by sample inefficiency, invalid candidates, and redundant generations with limited coverage. Large Language Models (LLMs) offer strong priors for producing valid transformations, but current LLM-based FT methods typically rely on static demonstrations, resulting in limited diversity, redundant outputs, and weak alignment with downstream objectives. We propose a framework that optimizes context data for LLM-driven FT by evolving trajectory-level experiences in a closed loop. Starting from high-performing feature transportation sequences explored by reinforcement learning, we construct and continuously update an experience library of downstream task-verified transformation trajectories, and use a diversity-aware selector to form contexts along with a chain-of-thought and guide transformed feature generation toward higher performance. Experiments on diverse tabular benchmarks show that our method outperforms classical and LLM-based baselines and is more stable than one-shot generation. The framework generalizes across API-based and open-source LLMs and remains robust across downstream evaluators.

AI.tldr: Closed-loop, experience-evolving contexts steer LLMs to generate higher-quality feature transformations for tabular prediction, outperforming classical and prior LLM-based baselines with better stability and cross-model robustness.
AI.motivation: Feature transformation is crucial for tabular ML but hard due to a vast, combinatorial search over features and operators. Prior discrete/latent search is sample-inefficient and yields invalid/redundant candidates. LLMs can propose valid transformations, yet static-prompt approaches lack diversity and alignment with downstream objectives.
AI.method: Initialize with high-performing transformation trajectories found via reinforcement learning; build a continually updated library of downstream-validated trajectories. Use a diversity-aware selector to assemble contextual examples plus chain-of-thought to condition LLMs. Close the loop by evaluating generated transformations on downstream tasks, adding successful trajectories back to the library to improve future contexts.
AI.result: Across diverse tabular benchmarks, the approach surpasses classical and LLM-based baselines, shows greater stability than one-shot generation, generalizes across API and open-source LLMs, and remains robust under different downstream evaluators.
AI.conclusion: Evolving, diversity-aware context optimization aligns LLM-driven feature transformation with task objectives, reducing redundancy/invalidity and improving performance and stability. The framework is model-agnostic and robust, suggesting practical gains for tabular ML feature engineering.

title: IH-Challenge: A Training Dataset to Improve Instruction Hierarchy on Frontier LLMs
summary: Instruction hierarchy (IH) defines how LLMs prioritize system, developer, user, and tool instructions under conflict, providing a concrete, trust-ordered policy for resolving instruction conflicts. IH is key to defending against jailbreaks, system prompt extractions, and agentic prompt injections. However, robust IH behavior is difficult to train: IH failures can be confounded with instruction-following failures, conflicts can be nuanced, and models can learn shortcuts such as overrefusing. We introduce IH-Challenge, a reinforcement learning training dataset, to address these difficulties. Fine-tuning GPT-5-Mini on IH-Challenge with online adversarial example generation improves IH robustness by +10.0% on average across 16 in-distribution, out-of-distribution, and human red-teaming benchmarks (84.1% to 94.1%), reduces unsafe behavior from 6.6% to 0.7% while improving helpfulness on general safety evaluations, and saturates an internal static agentic prompt injection evaluation, with minimal capability regression. We release the IH-Challenge dataset (https://huggingface.co/datasets/openai/ih-challenge) to support future research on robust instruction hierarchy.

AI.tldr: Proposes an instruction hierarchy (IH) training/evaluation regime and releases IH-Challenge; fine-tuning GPT-5-Mini with adversarial RL substantially boosts IH robustness and safety with minimal capability cost.
AI.motivation: LLMs need a consistent, trust-ordered policy to resolve conflicts among system, developer, user, and tool instructions to resist jailbreaks, prompt extraction, and agentic prompt injections. Training this behavior is hard because IH failures are entangled with general instruction following, conflicts are subtle, and models can adopt shortcuts like overrefusal.
AI.method: Create IH-Challenge, a reinforcement-learning training dataset paired with online adversarial example generation. Fine-tune GPT-5-Mini on IH-Challenge and evaluate across 16 in- and out-of-distribution IH benchmarks plus human red-teaming, general safety helpfulness tests, and an internal static agentic prompt injection evaluation.
AI.result: IH robustness improves by +10.0% on average (84.1%→94.1%), unsafe behavior drops from 6.6%→0.7%, helpfulness on general safety evaluations improves, internal static agentic prompt-injection benchmark is saturated, and capability regression is minimal.
AI.conclusion: Targeted RL with adversarial data focused on instruction hierarchy meaningfully strengthens models against jailbreaks and prompt injections while maintaining capabilities; the released IH-Challenge dataset enables further research on robust IH.

title: One Adapter for All: Towards Unified Representation in Step-Imbalanced Class-Incremental Learning
summary: Class-incremental learning (CIL) aims to acquire new classes over time while retaining prior knowledge, yet most setups and methods assume balanced task streams. In practice, the number of classes per task often varies significantly. We refer to this as step imbalance, where large tasks that contain more classes dominate learning and small tasks inject unstable updates. Existing CIL methods assume balanced tasks and therefore treat all tasks uniformly, producing imbalanced updates that degrade overall learning performance. To address this challenge, we propose One-A, a unified and imbalance-aware framework that incrementally merges task updates into a single adapter, maintaining constant inference cost. One-A performs asymmetric subspace alignment to preserve dominant subspaces learned from large tasks while constraining low-information updates within them. An information-adaptive weighting balances the contribution between base and new adapters, and a directional gating mechanism selectively fuses updates along each singular direction, maintaining stability in head directions and plasticity in tail ones. Across multiple benchmarks and step-imbalanced streams, One-A achieves competitive accuracy with significantly low inference overhead, showing that a single, asymmetrically fused adapter can remain both adaptive to dynamic task sizes and efficient at deployment.

AI.tldr: Addresses step imbalance in class-incremental learning by fusing all task updates into a single, asymmetry-aware adapter that preserves dominant subspaces from large tasks while cautiously integrating small-task updates, achieving competitive accuracy with constant inference cost.
AI.motivation: Real-world CIL streams have highly variable numbers of classes per task. Treating all tasks uniformly causes large tasks to dominate learning and small tasks to introduce noisy, unstable updates, degrading retention and adaptation. A method is needed to balance stability vs. plasticity under step-imbalanced task streams while keeping inference efficient.
AI.method: One-A incrementally merges task updates into a single adapter. It: (1) performs asymmetric subspace alignment to preserve dominant subspaces from large tasks and constrain low-information updates; (2) uses information-adaptive weighting to balance contributions of base vs. new adapters; and (3) applies directional gating along singular directions to keep stability in head directions and allow plasticity in tail directions, yielding constant inference cost.
AI.result: On multiple benchmarks and step-imbalanced streams, One-A attains competitive accuracy while significantly reducing inference overhead compared to methods that maintain multiple adapters or heads.
AI.conclusion: Imbalance-aware, asymmetric fusion into a single adapter effectively mitigates step imbalance in CIL, preserving knowledge from large tasks, stabilizing small-task updates, and enabling efficient deployment without growing inference cost.

title: Causally Grounded Mechanistic Interpretability for LLMs with Faithful Natural-Language Explanations
comment: 8 pages, 7 figures, 4 tables. MSc thesis work conducted at Hochschule Trier (2026). Code will be released upon publication
summary: Mechanistic interpretability identifies internal circuits responsible for model behaviors, yet translating these findings into human-understandable explanations remains an open problem. We present a pipeline that bridges circuit-level analysis and natural language explanations by (i) identifying causally important attention heads via activation patching, (ii) generating explanations using both template-based and LLM-based methods, and (iii) evaluating faithfulness using ERASER-style metrics adapted for circuit-level attribution. We evaluate on the Indirect Object Identification (IOI) task in GPT-2 Small (124M parameters), identifying six attention heads accounting for 61.4% of the logit difference. Our circuit-based explanations achieve 100% sufficiency but only 22% comprehensiveness, revealing distributed backup mechanisms. LLM-generated explanations outperform template baselines by 64% on quality metrics. We find no correlation (r = 0.009) between model confidence and explanation faithfulness, and identify three failure categories explaining when explanations diverge from mechanisms.

AI.tldr: Pipeline linking mechanistic circuits to natural-language explanations; on GPT-2 Small IOI, six heads explain 61.4% of the signal. Explanations are sufficient but not comprehensive; LLM-written explanations beat templates, and model confidence does not track faithfulness.
AI.motivation: Mechanistic interpretability can find causal circuits, but people still lack faithful, human-readable explanations of how models produce behaviors. The work aims to bridge circuit discovery with natural-language rationales and to quantify their faithfulness.
AI.method: (i) Use activation patching to causally identify important attention heads; (ii) generate explanations with templates and with an LLM conditioned on circuit findings; (iii) evaluate faithfulness via ERASER-style sufficiency and comprehensiveness adapted to circuit-level attributions.
AI.result: On the IOI task in GPT-2 Small, six attention heads account for 61.4% of the logit difference. Circuit-based explanations achieve 100% sufficiency but only 22% comprehensiveness, indicating distributed backups. LLM-generated explanations outperform templates by 64% on quality metrics. Model confidence and explanation faithfulness are uncorrelated (r = 0.009). Three failure categories are identified where explanations diverge from mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: Natural-language explanations grounded in circuit analysis are possible and can be high-quality, but they often omit distributed mechanisms, limiting comprehensiveness. Confidence should not be used as a proxy for faithfulness. Improving coverage of distributed circuits and refining faithfulness metrics is necessary; LLMs help readability but do not guarantee faithfulness.

title: Adaptive RAN Slicing Control via Reward-Free Self-Finetuning Agents
summary: The integration of Generative AI models into AI-native network systems offers a transformative path toward achieving autonomous and adaptive control. However, the application of such models to continuous control tasks is impeded by intrinsic architectural limitations, including finite context windows, the lack of explicit reward signals, and the degradation of the long context. This paper posits that the key to unlocking robust continuous control is enabling agents to internalize experience by distilling it into their parameters, rather than relying on prompt-based memory. To this end, we propose a novel self-finetuning framework that enables agentic systems to learn continuously through direct interaction with the environment, bypassing the need for handcrafted rewards. Our framework implements a bi-perspective reflection mechanism that generates autonomous linguistic feedback to construct preference datasets from interaction history. A subsequent preference-based fine-tuning process distills long-horizon experiences into the model's parameters. We evaluate our approach on a dynamic Radio Access Network (RAN) slicing task, a challenging multi-objective control problem that requires the resolution of acute trade-offs between spectrum efficiency, service quality, and reconfiguration stability under volatile network conditions. Experimental results show that our framework outperforms standard Reinforcement Learning (RL) baselines and existing Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents in sample efficiency, stability, and multi-metric optimization. These findings demonstrate the potential of self-improving generative agents for continuous control tasks, paving the way for future AI-native network infrastructure.

AI.tldr: They introduce a self-finetuning generative agent for continuous control that converts interaction histories into preference data via autonomous reflections, then fine-tunes itself to internalize long-horizon experience—achieving superior sample efficiency, stability, and multi-metric performance on dynamic RAN slicing versus RL and LLM baselines.
AI.motivation: LLMs struggle with continuous control due to finite context windows, lack of explicit rewards, and long-context degradation. To enable autonomous, adaptive network control, the authors argue agents should internalize experience in parameters instead of relying on prompt-based memory and handcrafted rewards.
AI.method: A self-finetuning loop where an agent interacts with the environment and uses a bi-perspective reflection mechanism to generate linguistic feedback. This produces preference datasets from the interaction history, which are then used in preference-based fine-tuning to distill long-horizon experience into the model’s parameters. The framework is assessed on a multi-objective, dynamic RAN slicing task with trade-offs across spectrum efficiency, service quality, and reconfiguration stability.
AI.result: On the dynamic RAN slicing benchmark, the proposed method outperforms standard RL baselines and existing LLM-based agents in sample efficiency, stability, and joint optimization across multiple metrics under volatile conditions.
