AI.conclusion: Self-improving generative agents that distill experience into parameters can deliver robust continuous control without handcrafted rewards, pointing toward viable AI-native network infrastructures and suggesting a path beyond prompt-memory limitations.

title: Joint Imaging-ROI Representation Learning via Cross-View Contrastive Alignment for Brain Disorder Classification
summary: Brain imaging classification is commonly approached from two perspectives: modeling the full image volume to capture global anatomical context, or constructing ROI-based graphs to encode localized and topological interactions. Although both representations have demonstrated independent efficacy, their relative contributions and potential complementarity remain insufficiently understood. Existing fusion approaches are typically task-specific and do not enable controlled evaluation of each representation under consistent training settings. To address this gap, we propose a unified cross-view contrastive framework for joint imaging-ROI representation learning. Our method learns subject-level global (imaging) and local (ROI-graph) embeddings and aligns them in a shared latent space using a bidirectional contrastive objective, encouraging representations from the same subject to converge while separating those from different subjects. This alignment produces comparable embeddings suitable for downstream fusion and enables systematic evaluation of imaging-only, ROI-only, and joint configurations within a unified training protocol. Extensive experiments on the ADHD-200 and ABIDE datasets demonstrate that joint learning consistently improves classification performance over either branch alone across multiple backbone choices. Moreover, interpretability analyses reveal that imaging-based and ROI-based branches emphasize distinct yet complementary discriminative patterns, explaining the observed performance gains. These findings provide principled evidence that explicitly integrating global volumetric and ROI-level representations is a promising direction for neuroimaging-based brain disorder classification. The source code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/imaging-roi-contrastive-152C/.

AI.tldr: They propose a unified cross-view contrastive learning framework that aligns whole-volume brain images with ROI-graph representations, yielding complementary embeddings that, when fused, improve brain disorder classification on ADHD-200 and ABIDE.
AI.motivation: Global volumetric models and ROI-graph models each work but their relative benefits and complementarity are unclear. Existing fusion methods are task-specific and lack a unified, controlled setup to compare and combine the two representations fairly.
AI.method: Learn subject-level embeddings from two branches: a global imaging branch (full 3D volume) and a local ROI-graph branch. Use a bidirectional contrastive objective to align the two views in a shared latent space so same-subject pairs are close and different subjects are far. This produces comparable embeddings that support imaging-only, ROI-only, and fused evaluation under a single training protocol, across multiple backbones.
AI.result: On ADHD-200 and ABIDE, joint learning/fusion consistently outperforms either branch alone across different backbone choices. Interpretability indicates the two branches capture distinct yet complementary discriminative patterns. Code is released for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly aligning and fusing global volumetric and ROI-level graph representations via contrastive learning is a principled, general approach that enhances neuroimaging-based disorder classification and clarifies the complementary roles of the two views.

title: The System Hallucination Scale (SHS): A Minimal yet Effective Human-Centered Instrument for Evaluating Hallucination-Related Behavior in Large Language Models
summary: We introduce the System Hallucination Scale (SHS), a lightweight and human-centered measurement instrument for assessing hallucination-related behavior in large language models (LLMs). Inspired by established psychometric tools such as the System Usability Scale (SUS) and the System Causability Scale (SCS), SHS enables rapid, interpretable, and domain-agnostic evaluation of factual unreliability, incoherence, misleading presentation, and responsiveness to user guidance in model-generated text. SHS is explicitly not an automatic hallucination detector or benchmark metric; instead, it captures how hallucination phenomena manifest from a user perspective under realistic interaction conditions. A real-world evaluation with 210 participants demonstrates high clarity, coherent response behavior, and construct validity, supported by statistical analysis including internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha = 0.87$) and significant inter-dimension correlations (p < 0.001$). Comparative analysis with SUS and SCS reveals complementary measurement properties, supporting SHS as a practical tool for comparative analysis, iterative system development, and deployment monitoring.

AI.tldr: Proposes the System Hallucination Scale (SHS), a short, human-centered questionnaire to assess hallucination-related behaviors in LLM outputs; validated with 210 users showing strong reliability (alpha 0.87) and meaningful correlations; complements SUS/SCS for evaluation, development, and monitoring.
AI.motivation: Automatic metrics and benchmarks miss how hallucinations appear to end users during real interactions. There is a need for a rapid, interpretable, domain-agnostic way to gauge factual unreliability and related behaviors in LLMs, akin to established psychometric tools like SUS and SCS.
AI.method: Design a lightweight scale capturing four dimensions—factual unreliability, incoherence, misleading presentation, and responsiveness to user guidance. Run a real-world user study (n=210), evaluate clarity and response behavior, test internal consistency (Cronbach’s alpha), analyze inter-dimension correlations, and compare SHS’s properties against SUS and SCS.
AI.result: Participants exhibited clear, coherent responses; SHS achieved high internal consistency (alpha = 0.87) and significant inter-dimension correlations (p < 0.001). Comparative analysis showed SHS offers complementary measurement properties relative to SUS and SCS.
AI.conclusion: SHS is a practical, rapid, and interpretable instrument for user-centered assessment of hallucination phenomena in LLMs. It is not an automatic detector but is suitable for comparative analysis across systems, guiding iterative model and UX improvements, and monitoring during deployment.

title: CUAAudit: Meta-Evaluation of Vision-Language Models as Auditors of Autonomous Computer-Use Agents
summary: Computer-Use Agents (CUAs) are emerging as a new paradigm in human-computer interaction, enabling autonomous execution of tasks in desktop environment by perceiving high-level natural-language instructions. As such agents become increasingly capable and are deployed across diverse desktop environments, evaluating their behavior in a scalable and reliable manner becomes a critical challenge. Existing evaluation pipelines rely on static benchmarks, rule-based success checks, or manual inspection, which are brittle, costly, and poorly aligned with real-world usage. In this work, we study Vision-Language Models (VLMs) as autonomous auditors for assessing CUA task completion directly from observable interactions and conduct a large-scale meta-evaluation of five VLMs that judge task success given a natural-language instruction and the final environment state. Our evaluation spans three widely used CUA benchmarks across macOS, Windows, and Linux environments and analyzes auditor behavior along three complementary dimensions: accuracy, calibration of confidence estimates, and inter-model agreement. We find that while state-of-the-art VLMs achieve strong accuracy and calibration, all auditors exhibit notable performance degradation in more complex or heterogeneous environments, and even high-performing models show significant disagreement in their judgments. These results expose fundamental limitations of current model-based auditing approaches and highlight the need to explicitly account for evaluator reliability, uncertainty, and variance when deploying autonomous CUAs in real-world settings.

AI.tldr: They benchmark vision-language models as autonomous auditors for computer-use agents, finding decent accuracy and calibration overall but clear degradation and disagreement in complex or varied desktop settings, underscoring the need to model auditor reliability and uncertainty.
AI.motivation: CUAs need scalable, real-world-aligned evaluation beyond brittle static benchmarks, rule-based checks, and costly manual review. VLMs could offer automated, generalizable judgments from observable interactions.
AI.method: Treat VLMs as auditors that, given a natural-language task instruction and the final desktop state, judge task success. Conduct a large-scale meta-evaluation of five VLMs across three CUA benchmarks spanning macOS, Windows, and Linux. Analyze auditor accuracy, calibration of confidence, and inter-model agreement.
AI.result: State-of-the-art VLM auditors achieve strong accuracy and well-calibrated confidence on average, but performance drops in more complex or heterogeneous environments. Even top models disagree substantially with each other on many tasks.
AI.conclusion: Model-based auditing is promising but unreliable in its current form. Deployments should explicitly account for evaluator uncertainty, reliability, and variance—e.g., through improved auditors, ensembles, or uncertainty-aware decision policies—before trusting automated judgments of CUA success.

title: A Robust Deep Learning Framework for Bangla License Plate Recognition Using YOLO and Vision-Language OCR
comment: Accepted at the 2026 IEEE International Conference on AI and Data Analytics (ICAD 2026). Final version will appear in IEEE Xplore
summary: An Automatic License Plate Recognition (ALPR) system constitutes a crucial element in an intelligent traffic management system. However, the detection of Bangla license plates remains challenging because of the complicated character scheme and uneven layouts. This paper presents a robust Bangla License Plate Recognition system that integrates a deep learning-based object detection model for license plate localization with Optical Character Recognition for text extraction. Multiple object detection architectures, including U-Net and several YOLO (You Only Look Once) variants, are compared for license plate localization. This study proposes a novel two-stage adaptive training strategy built upon the YOLOv8 architecture to improve localization performance. The proposed approach outperforms the established models, achieving an accuracy of 97.83% and an Intersection over Union (IoU) of 91.3%. The text recognition problem is phrased as a sequence generation problem with a VisionEncoderDecoder architecture, with a combination of encoder-decoders evaluated. It was demonstrated that the ViT + BanglaBERT model gives better results at the character level, with a Character Error Rate of 0.1323 and Word Error Rate of 0.1068. The proposed system also shows a consistent performance when tested on an external dataset that has been curated for this study purpose. The dataset offers completely different environment and lighting conditions compared to the training sample, indicating the robustness of the proposed framework. Overall, our proposed system provides a robust and reliable solution for Bangla license plate recognition and performs effectively across diverse real-world scenarios, including variations in lighting, noise, and plate styles. These strengths make it well suited for deployment in intelligent transportation applications such as automated law enforcement and access control.

AI.tldr: Robust Bangla ALPR combining YOLOv8 with a two‑stage adaptive training for plate localization and a VisionEncoderDecoder OCR (ViT encoder + BanglaBERT decoder) for text, achieving 97.83% localization accuracy, 91.3% IoU, and OCR CER 0.1323/WER 0.1068; shows strong cross‑dataset robustness under varied conditions.
AI.motivation: Bangla license plates are hard to recognize due to complex script, uneven layouts, and diverse real‑world conditions. Reliable ALPR is needed for intelligent traffic systems (enforcement, access control) that operate robustly across lighting, noise, and plate style variations.
AI.method: Compare U‑Net and multiple YOLO variants for plate localization; introduce a novel two‑stage adaptive training strategy on YOLOv8 to enhance detection. Formulate OCR as sequence generation with a VisionEncoderDecoder; evaluate encoder‑decoder pairs and find ViT encoder + BanglaBERT decoder best for character‑level recognition. Validate robustness on an external dataset with different environments and lighting.
AI.result: Proposed YOLOv8 with adaptive training outperforms baselines, achieving 97.83% localization accuracy and 91.3% IoU. The ViT+BanglaBERT OCR attains Character Error Rate 0.1323 and Word Error Rate 0.1068. Performance remains consistent on an external, distribution‑shifted dataset.
AI.conclusion: The system is robust and reliable for Bangla license plate recognition across diverse real‑world scenarios and is suitable for deployment in intelligent transportation applications (automated law enforcement, access control).

title: A Two-Stage Architecture for NDA Analysis: LLM-based Segmentation and Transformer-based Clause Classification
comment: 14 pages, 2 figures, 3 tables. Published at STIL @ BRACIS 2025
summary: In business-to-business relations, it is common to establish NonDisclosure Agreements (NDAs). However, these documents exhibit significant variation in format, structure, and writing style, making manual analysis slow and error-prone. We propose an architecture based on LLMs to automate the segmentation and clauses classification within these contracts. We employed two models: LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct for NDA segmentation (clause extraction) and a fine-tuned Legal-Roberta-Large for clause classification. In the segmentation task, we achieved a ROUGE F1 of 0.95 +/- 0.0036; for classification, we obtained a weighted F1 of 0.85, demonstrating the feasibility and precision of the approach.

AI.tldr: Two-stage LLM pipeline automates NDA clause extraction and classification, achieving high segmentation accuracy (ROUGE F1≈0.95) and solid classification performance (weighted F1=0.85).
AI.motivation: NDAs vary widely in format and style, making manual clause identification and analysis slow, inconsistent, and error-prone; automating this can speed B2B workflows and reduce risk.
AI.method: A modular architecture: (1) NDA segmentation with LLaMA‑3.1‑8B‑Instruct to extract clause spans; (2) clause classification with a fine‑tuned Legal‑RoBERTa‑Large model.
AI.result: Segmentation achieved ROUGE F1 of 0.95 ± 0.0036; clause classification reached weighted F1 of 0.85.
AI.conclusion: The approach is feasible and accurate for automating NDA analysis, suggesting practical value in contract review pipelines, though broader validation and robustness checks would be needed for deployment.

title: Does LLM Alignment Really Need Diversity? An Empirical Study of Adapting RLVR Methods for Moral Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has achieved remarkable success in logical reasoning tasks, yet whether large language model (LLM) alignment requires fundamentally different approaches remains unclear. Given the apparent tolerance for multiple valid responses in moral reasoning, a natural hypothesis is that alignment tasks inherently require diversity-seeking distribution-matching algorithms rather than reward-maximizing policy-based methods. We conduct the first comprehensive empirical study comparing both paradigms on MoReBench. To enable stable RLVR training, we build a rubric-grounded reward pipeline by training a Qwen3-1.7B judge model. Contrary to our hypothesis, we find that distribution-matching approaches do not demonstrate significant advantages over reward-maximizing methods as expected on alignment tasks. Through semantic visualization mapping high-reward responses to semantic space, we demonstrate that moral reasoning exhibits more concentrated high-reward distributions than mathematical reasoning, where diverse solution strategies yield similarly high rewards. This counter-intuitive finding explains why mode-seeking optimization proves equally or more effective for alignment tasks. Our results suggest that alignment tasks do not inherently require diversity-preserving algorithms, and standard reward-maximizing RLVR methods can effectively transfer to moral reasoning without explicit diversity mechanisms.

AI.tldr: Empirical study on moral-alignment tasks finds that standard reward-maximizing RL with verifiable rewards performs on par with—or better than—distribution-matching methods because high-reward moral responses cluster semantically, reducing the need for diversity-preserving algorithms.
AI.motivation: Test the common belief that alignment (especially moral reasoning) needs diversity-seeking, distribution-matching training since many answers can be acceptable, unlike problems with single correct solutions.
AI.method: Compare reward-maximizing RLVR against distribution-matching approaches on MoReBench; construct a stable, rubric-grounded reward pipeline using a trained Qwen3-1.7B judge; visualize response embeddings to map where high-reward answers lie in semantic space.
AI.result: Distribution-matching shows no significant advantage over RLVR on alignment tasks. Semantic mapping reveals moral reasoning has concentrated high-reward regions, whereas math reasoning exhibits multiple diverse, high-reward strategies.
AI.conclusion: Alignment tasks do not inherently require diversity-preserving algorithms. Mode-seeking, reward-maximizing RLVR can transfer effectively to moral reasoning without explicit diversity mechanisms.

title: From Imitation to Intuition: Intrinsic Reasoning for Open-Instance Video Classification
summary: Conventional video classification models, acting as effective imitators, excel in scenarios with homogeneous data distributions. However, real-world applications often present an open-instance challenge, where intra-class variations are vast and complex, beyond existing benchmarks. While traditional video encoder models struggle to fit these diverse distributions, vision-language models (VLMs) offer superior generalization but have not fully leveraged their reasoning capabilities (intuition) for such tasks. In this paper, we bridge this gap with an intrinsic reasoning framework that evolves open-instance video classification from imitation to intuition. Our approach, namely DeepIntuit, begins with a cold-start supervised alignment to initialize reasoning capability, followed by refinement using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to enhance reasoning coherence through reinforcement learning. Crucially, to translate this reasoning into accurate classification, DeepIntuit then introduces an intuitive calibration stage. In this stage, a classifier is trained on this intrinsic reasoning traces generated by the refined VLM, ensuring stable knowledge transfer without distribution mismatch. Extensive experiments demonstrate that for open-instance video classification, DeepIntuit benefits significantly from transcending simple feature imitation and evolving toward intrinsic reasoning. Our project is available at https://bwgzk-keke.github.io/DeepIntuit/.

AI.tldr: DeepIntuit reframes open-instance video classification as a reasoning-driven task by aligning and refining VLM reasoning and then training a classifier on the model’s reasoning traces, leading to stronger generalization than imitation-based features.
AI.motivation: Conventional video classifiers excel on homogeneous data but falter with vast intra-class variability typical of real-world, open-instance settings. Vision-language models generalize better but their latent reasoning is underused. The work aims to harness and stabilize this reasoning to address distributional diversity beyond standard benchmarks.
AI.method: Intrinsic reasoning framework (DeepIntuit): (1) cold-start supervised alignment to initialize the VLM’s reasoning ability; (2) reinforcement learning refinement via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to improve reasoning coherence; (3) intuitive calibration that trains a downstream classifier on intrinsic reasoning traces from the refined VLM, ensuring stable knowledge transfer without distribution mismatch.
AI.result: Across extensive experiments, DeepIntuit outperforms traditional imitation-based video encoders on open-instance video classification, showing notable gains in robustness and generalization (specific metrics not provided in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: Shifting from feature imitation to intrinsic reasoning—by aligning, reinforcing, and calibrating VLM reasoning—yields more reliable open-instance video recognition, with reasoning traces providing an effective bridge for knowledge transfer.

title: PoultryLeX-Net: Domain-Adaptive Dual-Stream Transformer Architecture for Large-Scale Poultry Stakeholder Modeling
summary: The rapid growth of the global poultry industry, driven by rising demand for affordable animal protein, has intensified public discourse surrounding production practices, housing, management, animal welfare, and supply-chain transparency. Social media platforms such as X (formerly Twitter) generate large volumes of unstructured textual data that capture stakeholder sentiment across the poultry industry. Extracting accurate sentiment signals from this domain-specific discourse remains challenging due to contextual ambiguity, linguistic variability, and limited domain awareness in general-purpose language models. This study presents PoultryLeX-Net, a lexicon-enhanced, domain-adaptive dual-stream transformer framework for fine-grained sentiment analysis in poultry-related text. The proposed architecture integrates sentiment classification, topic modeling, and contextual representation learning through domain-specific embeddings and gated cross-attention mechanisms. A lexicon-guided stream captures poultry-specific terminology and sentiment cues, while contextual stream models long-range semantic dependencies. Latent Dirichlet Allocation is employed to identify dominant thematic structures associated with production management and welfare-related discussions, providing complementary interpretability to sentiment predictions. PoultryLeX-Net was evaluated against multiple baseline models, including convolutional neural network and pre-trained transformer architectures such as DistilBERT and RoBERTa. PoultryLeX-Net consistently outperformed all baselines, achieving an accuracy of 97.35%, an F1 score of 96.67%, and an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC-ROC) of 99.61% across sentiment classification tasks. Overall, domain adaptation and dual-stream attention markedly improve sentiment classification, enabling scalable intelligence for poultry production decision support.

AI.tldr: PoultryLeX-Net is a domain-adaptive, lexicon-enhanced dual-stream transformer with integrated topic modeling that delivers state-of-the-art fine-grained sentiment analysis for poultry-related social media, outperforming strong CNN and transformer baselines.
AI.motivation: General-purpose sentiment models struggle with poultry-industry discourse because of domain-specific jargon, contextual ambiguity, and linguistic variability. Accurate, interpretable sentiment signals are needed to inform production, welfare, and transparency decisions.
AI.method: Introduce PoultryLeX-Net: (1) a lexicon-guided stream capturing poultry-specific terms and sentiment cues, (2) a contextual stream modeling long-range semantics; streams are fused via gated cross-attention and trained with domain-specific embeddings. Latent Dirichlet Allocation uncovers dominant themes (e.g., production management, welfare) to provide interpretable context alongside sentiment predictions.
AI.result: Across sentiment classification tasks, PoultryLeX-Net outperforms CNNs and pre-trained transformers (DistilBERT, RoBERTa), reaching 97.35% accuracy, 96.67% F1, and 99.61% AUC-ROC.
AI.conclusion: Domain adaptation combined with dual-stream, lexicon-guided attention substantially boosts fine-grained sentiment classification and supplies interpretable, scalable insights for poultry production decision support.

title: Trajectory-Informed Memory Generation for Self-Improving Agent Systems
summary: LLM-powered agents face a persistent challenge: learning from their execution experiences to improve future performance. While agents can successfully complete many tasks, they often repeat inefficient patterns, fail to recover from similar errors, and miss opportunities to apply successful strategies from past executions. We present a novel framework for automatically extracting actionable learnings from agent execution trajectories and utilizing them to improve future performance through contextual memory retrieval. Our approach comprises four components: (1) a Trajectory Intelligence Extractor that performs semantic analysis of agent reasoning patterns, (2) a Decision Attribution Analyzer that identifies which decisions and reasoning steps led to failures, recoveries, or inefficiencies, (3) a Contextual Learning Generator that produces three types of guidance -- strategy tips from successful patterns, recovery tips from failure handling, and optimization tips from inefficient but successful executions, and (4) an Adaptive Memory Retrieval System that injects relevant learnings into agent prompts based on multi-dimensional similarity. Unlike existing memory systems that store generic conversational facts, our framework understands execution patterns, extracts structured learnings with provenance, and retrieves guidance tailored to specific task contexts. Evaluation on the AppWorld benchmark demonstrates consistent improvements, with up to 14.3 percentage point gains in scenario goal completion on held-out tasks and particularly strong benefits on complex tasks (28.5~pp scenario goal improvement, a 149\% relative increase).

AI.tldr: They propose a framework that turns agent execution traces into actionable, retrievable guidance (strategy, recovery, optimization tips) and injects it into future prompts, yielding sizable gains on AppWorld, especially for complex tasks.
AI.motivation: LLM agents often repeat mistakes, fail to transfer successful tactics, and lack mechanisms to learn from prior executions; existing memory tends to store generic facts rather than procedural, causally relevant lessons.
AI.method: Four modules: (1) Trajectory Intelligence Extractor analyzes reasoning patterns in traces; (2) Decision Attribution Analyzer links specific choices/steps to failures, recoveries, or inefficiencies; (3) Contextual Learning Generator distills strategy, recovery, and optimization tips with provenance; (4) Adaptive Memory Retrieval fetches relevant tips via multi-dimensional similarity and injects them into prompts.
AI.result: On the AppWorld benchmark, the system shows consistent improvements: up to +14.3 percentage points in goal completion on held-out tasks, with larger boosts on complex tasks (+28.5 pp; ~149% relative increase).
AI.conclusion: Structured, provenance-aware memory derived from execution trajectories can meaningfully improve agent performance via contextual retrieval, particularly on challenging tasks; this suggests procedural experience is a valuable, retrievable resource for LLM agents.

title: Fuel Gauge: Estimating Chain-of-Thought Length Ahead of Time in Large Multimodal Models
summary: Reasoning Large Multi-modality Models (LMMs) have become the de facto choice for many applications. However, these models rely on a Chain-of-Thought (CoT) process that is lengthy and unpredictable at runtime, often resulting in inefficient use of computational resources (due to memory fragmentation) and sub-optimal accuracy (due to under- and over-thinking). We observe empirically that the CoT process follows a very simple form, whose behavior is independent of the specific generated samples. This suggests that the CoT length can be estimated ahead of time based on a hidden parameter representing the amount of "fuel" available to support the reasoning process. Based on this insight, we propose Fuel Gauge, the first method which extracts this hidden signal and predicts CoT length ahead of time. We demonstrate the utility on the Fuel Gauge on two downstream tasks: predictive KV cache allocation, which addresses memory fragmentation in LMM serving systems, and CoT length modulation, which mitigates under-thinking and over-thinking. Extensive experiments on LMMs across text-only, image-text, and video-text question answering benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness, generalizability, and practical value of our Fuel Gauge. For example, on the GPQA-Diamond benchmark, our Fuel Gauge achieves less than half the CoT length prediction error compared to the baseline; this translates into a 13.37x reduction in the memory allocation frequency.

AI.tldr: Fuel Gauge predicts, ahead of time, how long an LMM’s chain-of-thought will be by extracting a hidden “fuel” signal, enabling smarter KV-cache allocation and adaptive reasoning length. This cuts memory fragmentation and mitigates under/over-thinking, yielding large efficiency gains and accuracy benefits across modalities.
AI.motivation: CoT reasoning in LMMs is long and runtime-unpredictable, causing memory fragmentation and inefficiency in serving, and accuracy drops from under- or over-thinking. A method to anticipate CoT length could optimize resources and improve outcomes.
AI.method: Empirically observe CoT follows a simple, sample-agnostic trajectory governed by a latent “fuel” budget. Propose Fuel Gauge to estimate this hidden signal and predict CoT length before decoding. Use predictions for (1) predictive KV-cache allocation to reduce memory fragmentation and (2) CoT length modulation to avoid under/over-thinking. Evaluate on text-, image-, and video-QA tasks.
AI.result: Across benchmarks, Fuel Gauge generalizes and improves both systems and task performance. On GPQA-Diamond it achieves less than half the CoT-length prediction error vs. baselines, translating to a 13.37× reduction in memory allocation frequency; similar gains observed across modalities.
AI.conclusion: Anticipating CoT length via a latent fuel signal is effective and broadly applicable, enabling proactive memory management and adaptive reasoning control in LMMs. The approach improves efficiency and mitigates reasoning pathologies, showing practical value across modalities and tasks.

title: TAMUSA-Chat: A Domain-Adapted Large Language Model Conversational System for Research and Responsible Deployment
summary: This paper presents TAMUSA-Chat, a research-oriented framework for building domain-adapted large language model conversational systems. The work addresses critical challenges in adapting general-purpose foundation models to institutional contexts through supervised fine-tuning, retrieval-augmented generation, and systematic evaluation methodologies. We describe the complete architecture encompassing data acquisition from institutional sources, preprocessing pipelines, embedding construction, model training workflows, and deployment strategies. The system integrates modular components enabling reproducible experimentation with training configurations, hyper-parameters, and evaluation protocols. Our implementation demonstrates how academic institutions can develop contextually grounded conversational agents while maintaining transparency, governance compliance, and responsible AI practices. Through empirical analysis of fine-tuning behavior across model sizes and training iterations, we provide insights into domain adaptation efficiency, computational resource requirements, and quality-cost trade-offs. The publicly available codebase at https://github.com/alsmadi/TAMUSA_LLM_Based_Chat_app supports continued research into institutional LLM deployment, evaluation methodologies, and ethical considerations for educational AI systems.

AI.tldr: TAMUSA-Chat is an open, modular framework for adapting general LLMs to institutional contexts using supervised fine-tuning, retrieval-augmented generation, and rigorous evaluation, with end-to-end pipelines and public code to enable reproducible research and compliant deployment.
AI.motivation: Institutions need conversational agents that accurately reflect their own policies, data, and governance requirements; general-purpose LLMs lack contextual grounding, transparency, and evaluative rigor for such deployments.
AI.method: Design a modular, research-oriented stack covering institutional data acquisition, preprocessing, embedding construction, SFT workflows, RAG integration, deployment strategies, and standardized evaluation. Enable controlled experimentation over model sizes, training iterations, hyperparameters, and protocols.
AI.result: Empirical analyses across models and training regimes highlight domain adaptation efficiency, compute/resource needs, and quality–cost trade-offs; the system demonstrates compliant, transparent, and reproducible deployment practices. A public codebase supports replication and extension.
AI.conclusion: A practical, extensible framework can reliably adapt foundation models to institutional settings while meeting governance and responsible AI requirements; systematic evaluation and modularity enable informed choices about performance vs. cost in educational AI deployments.

title: FAME: Formal Abstract Minimal Explanation for Neural Networks
summary: We propose FAME (Formal Abstract Minimal Explanations), a new class of abductive explanations grounded in abstract interpretation. FAME is the first method to scale to large neural networks while reducing explanation size. Our main contribution is the design of dedicated perturbation domains that eliminate the need for traversal order. FAME progressively shrinks these domains and leverages LiRPA-based bounds to discard irrelevant features, ultimately converging to a formal abstract minimal explanation. To assess explanation quality, we introduce a procedure that measures the worst-case distance between an abstract minimal explanation and a true minimal explanation. This procedure combines adversarial attacks with an optional VERIX+ refinement step. We benchmark FAME against VERIX+ and demonstrate consistent gains in both explanation size and runtime on medium- to large-scale neural networks.

AI.tldr: FAME introduces a scalable, formal method to extract minimal feature explanations for large neural networks using abstract interpretation and LiRPA bounds, eliminating traversal-order dependence and outperforming VERIX+ in both explanation size and runtime.
AI.motivation: Existing explanation methods for neural networks struggle to scale, lack minimality guarantees, or depend on traversal order (e.g., feature ranking heuristics). There is a need for formally grounded, faithful, and computationally efficient minimal explanations that work on medium- to large-scale models.
AI.method: Define dedicated perturbation domains within abstract interpretation that remove the need for traversal order. Iteratively shrink these domains and apply LiRPA-derived bounds to prune features that cannot affect the decision, converging to a formal abstract minimal explanation. Propose a quality-assessment procedure that estimates the worst-case gap to true minimal explanations via adversarial attacks, optionally refined with VERIX+.
AI.result: Across benchmarks on medium- and large-scale neural networks, FAME achieves consistently smaller explanations and faster runtimes than VERIX+. The abstract does not report exact metrics but claims consistent gains and practical scalability.
AI.conclusion: FAME provides a principled, scalable route to minimal explanations with formal underpinnings and a practical way to estimate their tightness relative to true minima. Its improvements over VERIX+ suggest better applicability to large models, with the added benefit of a procedure to bound explanation suboptimality.

title: Overcoming Visual Clutter in Vision Language Action Models via Concept-Gated Visual Distillation
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models demonstrate impressive zero-shot generalization but frequently suffer from a "Precision-Reasoning Gap" in cluttered environments. This failure is driven by background-induced feature dilution, where high-frequency semantic noise corrupts the geometric grounding required for precise manipulation. To bridge this gap, we propose Concept-Gated Visual Distillation (CGVD), a training-free, model-agnostic inference framework that stabilizes VLA policies. CGVD operates by parsing instructions into safe and distractor sets, utilizing a two-layer target refinement process--combining cross-validation and spatial disambiguation--to explicitly penalize false positives and isolate genuine manipulation targets. We then process the scene via Fourier-based inpainting, generating a clean observation that actively suppresses semantic distractors while preserving critical spatial geometry and visual proprioception. Extensive evaluations in highly cluttered manipulation tasks demonstrate that CGVD prevents performance collapse. In environments with dense semantic distractors, our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving a 77.5% success rate compared to the baseline's 43.0%. By enforcing strict attribute adherence, CGVD establishes inference-time visual distillation as a critical prerequisite for robust robotic manipulation in the clutter.

AI.tldr: They introduce a training‑free, model‑agnostic inference procedure (CGVD) that filters out semantic clutter and sharpens geometric grounding for VLA policies, boosting success on cluttered manipulation from 43.0% to 77.5%.
AI.motivation: VLA models generalize well zero‑shot but fail at precise manipulation in clutter due to background‑induced feature dilution: high‑frequency semantic noise overwhelms features needed for geometric grounding, creating a precision–reasoning gap.
AI.method: Concept‑Gated Visual Distillation (CGVD): at inference, parse instructions into “safe” vs “distractor” concept sets; run a two‑layer target refinement (cross‑validation plus spatial disambiguation) to down‑weight false positives and isolate the true target; then apply Fourier‑based inpainting to the scene to suppress semantic distractors while preserving spatial geometry and visual proprioception, yielding a cleaned observation fed to the VLA policy. No training or model changes required.
AI.result: Across highly cluttered manipulation tasks with dense semantic distractors, CGVD prevents performance collapse and significantly outperforms baselines, achieving a 77.5% success rate versus 43.0% for the best baseline.
AI.conclusion: Inference‑time visual distillation that enforces strict attribute adherence is key for robust manipulation in clutter; CGVD provides a practical, training‑free way to stabilize diverse VLA policies by suppressing distractors while keeping geometric cues intact.

title: CEI: A Benchmark for Evaluating Pragmatic Reasoning in Language Models
summary: Pragmatic reasoning, inferring intended meaning beyond literal semantics, underpins everyday communication yet remains difficult for large language models. We present the Contextual Emotional Inference (CEI) Benchmark: 300 human-validated scenarios for evaluating how well LLMs disambiguate pragmatically complex utterances. Each scenario pairs a situational context and speaker-listener roles (with explicit power relations) against an ambiguous utterance. The dataset covers five pragmatic subtypes (sarcasm/irony, mixed signals, strategic politeness, passive aggression, deflection/misdirection) drawn from workplace, family, social, and service settings, with three power configurations (peer, higher-to-lower, lower-to-higher). Three trained annotators independently labeled every scenario. Inter-annotator agreement (Fleiss' kappa = 0.06-0.25 by subtype) is low but expected: pragmatic inference admits multiple valid readings, and the disagreement itself is informative. We describe our annotation methodology, including a 4-level quality control pipeline that combines automated statistical checks with expert adjudication. CEI is released under CC-BY-4.0.

AI.tldr: Introduces CEI, a 300-scenario benchmark to evaluate LLMs’ pragmatic/emotional inference across contexts, power dynamics, and five ambiguity types, with human validation, low-but-informative agreement, and rigorous QC.
AI.motivation: LLMs often fail at pragmatic reasoning—inferring intended meaning beyond literal text—especially when social context, emotion, and power relations shape interpretation. The field lacks standardized, human-validated evaluations that embrace ambiguity rather than force single “correct” readings.
AI.method: Curated 300 scenarios pairing rich situational contexts and explicit speaker–listener roles/power relations with pragmatically ambiguous utterances across five subtypes (sarcasm/irony, mixed signals, strategic politeness, passive aggression, deflection/misdirection) and domains (workplace, family, social, service). Three trained annotators independently labeled each scenario. Measured inter-annotator agreement (Fleiss’ kappa 0.06–0.25). Employed a 4-level quality-control pipeline combining automated statistical checks and expert adjudication. Released dataset under CC-BY-4.0.
AI.result: Produced a human-validated benchmark with broad coverage of pragmatic phenomena and power configurations. Observed low inter-annotator agreement consistent with inherent multiplicity of valid readings; documented annotation process, agreement statistics, and QC procedures. Resource is publicly available (CC-BY-4.0).
AI.conclusion: CEI provides a challenging, socially grounded benchmark for testing and improving LLMs’ pragmatic inference. Low-but-informative annotator agreement highlights real-world ambiguity, and the rigorous QC/annotation design supports reliable evaluation despite subjectivity.

title: Emulating Clinician Cognition via Self-Evolving Deep Clinical Research
summary: Clinical diagnosis is a complex cognitive process, grounded in dynamic cue acquisition and continuous expertise accumulation. Yet most current artificial intelligence (AI) systems are misaligned with this reality, treating diagnosis as single-pass retrospective prediction while lacking auditable mechanisms for governed improvement. We developed DxEvolve, a self-evolving diagnostic agent that bridges these gaps through an interactive deep clinical research workflow. The framework autonomously requisitions examinations and continually externalizes clinical experience from increasing encounter exposure as diagnostic cognition primitives. On the MIMIC-CDM benchmark, DxEvolve improved diagnostic accuracy by 11.2% on average over backbone models and reached 90.4% on a reader-study subset, comparable to the clinician reference (88.8%). DxEvolve improved accuracy on an independent external cohort by 10.2% (categories covered by the source cohort) and 17.1% (uncovered categories) compared to the competitive method. By transforming experience into a governable learning asset, DxEvolve supports an accountable pathway for the continual evolution of clinical AI.

AI.tldr: DxEvolve is a self-evolving clinical diagnostic agent that interactively orders tests and converts accumulated encounters into auditable “diagnostic cognition primitives,” yielding sizable and generalizable accuracy gains over standard models and approaching clinician performance.
AI.motivation: Clinical diagnosis is iterative and experience-driven, but most AI systems treat it as a one-shot prediction and lack transparent, governable mechanisms for continual improvement. There is a need for AI that aligns with real clinical reasoning, can be audited, and can evolve responsibly over time.
AI.method: Design an interactive diagnostic workflow where the agent autonomously requisitions relevant examinations and continuously externalizes learned clinical experience into structured, governable primitives ("diagnostic cognition primitives"). The system self-evolves as exposure to encounters increases. Performance is evaluated on MIMIC-CDM and an independent external cohort, compared against backbone and competitive methods, including a reader-study subset benchmarked to clinician reference performance.
AI.result: On MIMIC-CDM, DxEvolve improved average diagnostic accuracy by 11.2% over backbone models and achieved 90.4% accuracy on a reader-study subset, comparable to clinician reference (88.8%). On an external cohort, it improved accuracy by 10.2% for categories covered by the source cohort and by 17.1% for uncovered categories versus a competitive method, indicating generalization, including to novel categories.
AI.conclusion: By turning accumulated experience into a governable learning asset, DxEvolve provides an auditable, accountable path for continual improvement of clinical diagnostic AI, narrowing the gap between static predictive models and dynamic, expert-like clinical reasoning.

title: EmoStory: Emotion-Aware Story Generation
summary: Story generation aims to produce image sequences that depict coherent narratives while maintaining subject consistency across frames. Although existing methods have excelled in producing coherent and expressive stories, they remain largely emotion-neutral, focusing on what subject appears in a story while overlooking how emotions shape narrative interpretation and visual presentation. As stories are intended to engage audiences emotionally, we introduce emotion-aware story generation, a new task that aims to generate subject-consistent visual stories with explicit emotional directions. This task is challenging due to the abstract nature of emotions, which must be grounded in concrete visual elements and consistently expressed across a narrative through visual composition. To address these challenges, we propose EmoStory, a two-stage framework that integrates agent-based story planning and region-aware story generation. The planning stage transforms target emotions into coherent story prompts with emotion agent and writer agent, while the generation stage preserves subject consistency and injects emotion-related elements through region-aware composition. We evaluate EmoStory on a newly constructed dataset covering 25 subjects and 600 emotional stories. Extensive quantitative and qualitative results, along with user studies, show that EmoStory outperforms state-of-the-art story generation methods in emotion accuracy, prompt alignment, and subject consistency.

AI.tldr: Introduces EmoStory, a two-stage framework for emotion-aware visual story generation that preserves subject consistency and injects explicit emotional cues, outperforming prior methods on a new dataset.
AI.motivation: Most story-generation systems are emotion-neutral, focusing on who/what appears rather than how emotions shape narrative meaning and visuals. Emotions are abstract and hard to ground consistently in concrete visual elements across a sequence.
AI.method: EmoStory uses (1) an agent-based planning stage—an emotion agent and a writer agent—to transform target emotions into coherent story prompts; and (2) a region-aware generation stage that maintains subject consistency while injecting emotion-related visual elements via region-aware composition.
AI.result: Evaluated on a new dataset (25 subjects, 600 emotional stories), EmoStory surpasses state-of-the-art methods in emotion accuracy, prompt alignment, and subject consistency, supported by quantitative metrics, qualitative analyses, and user studies.
AI.conclusion: Integrating emotion-informed planning with region-aware generation effectively grounds abstract emotions into coherent visual narratives. The work defines a new task, releases a dataset, and demonstrates performance gains, pointing to a promising direction for emotion-aware story generation.

title: Evaluating Adjective-Noun Compositionality in LLMs: Functional vs Representational Perspectives
summary: Compositionality is considered central to language abilities. As performant language systems, how do large language models (LLMs) do on compositional tasks? We evaluate adjective-noun compositionality in LLMs using two complementary setups: prompt-based functional assessment and a representational analysis of internal model states. Our results reveal a striking divergence between task performance and internal states. While LLMs reliably develop compositional representations, they fail to translate consistently into functional task success across model variants. Consequently, we highlight the importance of contrastive evaluation for obtaining a more complete understanding of model capabilities.

AI.tldr: LLMs exhibit internally compositional representations for adjective–noun phrases, yet this does not consistently yield correct task performance; evaluating both behavior and representations is crucial.
AI.motivation: Compositionality underpins human language understanding, but it is unclear whether LLMs both represent and use compositional structure effectively. The work seeks to disentangle internal compositional representations from observable task success.
AI.method: Two-pronged evaluation: (1) prompt-based functional tests of adjective–noun compositionality; (2) representational analyses of internal model states to assess whether models encode compositional structure.
AI.result: Across model variants, internal states reliably reflect compositional structure, but functional performance on prompts is inconsistent, revealing a mismatch between representation and behavior.
AI.conclusion: Internal compositionality does not guarantee functional competence. A contrastive evaluation—jointly assessing representational evidence and behavioral performance—is needed for a fuller picture of LLM capabilities.

title: Nurture-First Agent Development: Building Domain-Expert AI Agents Through Conversational Knowledge Crystallization
summary: The emergence of large language model (LLM)-based agent frameworks has shifted the primary challenge in building domain-expert AI agents from raw capability to effective encoding of domain expertise. Two dominant paradigms -- code-first development, which embeds expertise in deterministic pipelines, and prompt-first development, which captures expertise in static system prompts -- both treat agent construction as a discrete engineering phase preceding deployment. We argue that this sequential assumption creates a fundamental mismatch with the nature of domain expertise, which is substantially tacit, deeply personal, and continuously evolving. We propose Nurture-First Development (NFD), a paradigm in which agents are initialized with minimal scaffolding and progressively grown through structured conversational interaction with domain practitioners. The central mechanism is the Knowledge Crystallization Cycle, whereby fragmented knowledge embedded in operational dialogue is periodically consolidated into structured, reusable knowledge assets. We formalize NFD through: (1) a Three-Layer Cognitive Architecture organizing agent knowledge by volatility and personalization degree; (2) the Knowledge Crystallization Cycle with formal definitions of crystallization operations and efficiency metrics; and (3) an operational framework comprising a Dual-Workspace Pattern and Spiral Development Model. We illustrate the paradigm through a detailed case study on building a financial research agent for U.S. equity analysis and discuss the conditions, limitations, and broader implications of NFD for human-agent co-evolution.

AI.tldr: Proposes Nurture-First Development (NFD): build LLM agents with minimal scaffolding and iteratively grow them via structured practitioner–agent conversations, periodically consolidating dialogue-derived know‑how into reusable knowledge through a Knowledge Crystallization Cycle; formalizes an architecture, operations/metrics, and an operational framework; demonstrates via a U.S. equity research agent case study.
AI.motivation: Code-first and prompt-first paradigms front-load expertise into static pipelines or prompts, assuming design is a one-time pre-deployment phase. This mismatches domain expertise, which is tacit, personal, and evolving, requiring a development process that continuously captures and refines practitioner knowledge.
AI.method: Initialize agents lightly; nurture through structured conversations with experts. Periodically crystallize fragmented operational dialogue into structured assets using a defined Knowledge Crystallization Cycle. Organize knowledge with a Three-Layer Cognitive Architecture (by volatility and personalization). Provide formal crystallization operations and efficiency metrics. Operationalize via a Dual-Workspace Pattern and a Spiral Development Model.
AI.result: A formal paradigm and process toolkit (architecture, cycle, operations, metrics, and workflows) plus an illustrative case study of building a U.S. equity analysis agent. The abstract reports conceptual validation and practical demonstration rather than quantitative benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: NFD aligns agent development with the tacit, personal, and evolving nature of expertise, enabling ongoing human–agent co-evolution through cyclical knowledge capture and consolidation; it has conditions and limitations but offers a structured path to sustained improvement and reuse of domain knowledge.

title: StyleGallery: Training-free and Semantic-aware Personalized Style Transfer from Arbitrary Image References
comment: 10 pages, 23 figures, Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2026
summary: Despite the advancements in diffusion-based image style transfer, existing methods are commonly limited by 1) semantic gap: the style reference could miss proper content semantics, causing uncontrollable stylization; 2) reliance on extra constraints (e.g., semantic masks) restricting applicability; 3) rigid feature associations lacking adaptive global-local alignment, failing to balance fine-grained stylization and global content preservation. These limitations, particularly the inability to flexibly leverage style inputs, fundamentally restrict style transfer in terms of personalization, accuracy, and adaptability. To address these, we propose StyleGallery, a training-free and semantic-aware framework that supports arbitrary reference images as input and enables effective personalized customization. It comprises three core stages: semantic region segmentation (adaptive clustering on latent diffusion features to divide regions without extra inputs); clustered region matching (block filtering on extracted features for precise alignment); and style transfer optimization (energy function-guided diffusion sampling with regional style loss to optimize stylization). Experiments on our introduced benchmark demonstrate that StyleGallery outperforms state-of-the-art methods in content structure preservation, regional stylization, interpretability, and personalized customization, particularly when leveraging multiple style references.

AI.tldr: StyleGallery is a training‑free, semantic‑aware diffusion framework that segments content into regions, aligns them with style references, and optimizes diffusion with regional losses to deliver controllable, personalized style transfer that preserves global structure and excels especially with multiple style images.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based style transfer methods struggle with (1) semantic gaps between content and style that cause uncontrollable stylization, (2) dependence on extra constraints like semantic masks that limit applicability, and (3) rigid feature associations that fail to balance global content preservation with fine-grained local stylization—hindering personalization, accuracy, and adaptability.
AI.method: Three-stage, training-free pipeline: (1) Semantic region segmentation via adaptive clustering on latent diffusion features, requiring no external inputs; (2) Clustered region matching using block filtering on extracted features for precise content–style alignment; (3) Style transfer optimization through energy function–guided diffusion sampling with regional style loss, supporting arbitrary and multiple style references.
AI.result: On a newly introduced benchmark, StyleGallery outperforms state-of-the-art methods in content structure preservation, regional stylization quality, interpretability, and personalized customization; gains are pronounced when multiple style references are used.
AI.conclusion: By combining unsupervised semantic segmentation, precise region-level alignment, and energy-guided diffusion with regional losses, StyleGallery overcomes key limitations of prior diffusion style transfer, enabling flexible, controllable, and personalized stylization without extra annotations or training.

title: Context Over Compute Human-in-the-Loop Outperforms Iterative Chain-of-Thought Prompting in Interview Answer Quality
summary: Behavioral interview evaluation using large language models presents unique challenges that require structured assessment, realistic interviewer behavior simulation, and pedagogical value for candidate training. We investigate chain of thought prompting for interview answer evaluation and improvement through two controlled experiments with 50 behavioral interview question and answer pairs. Our contributions are threefold. First, we provide a quantitative comparison between human in the loop and automated chain of thought improvement. Using a within subject paired design with n equals 50, both approaches show positive rating improvements. The human in the loop approach provides significant training benefits. Confidence improves from 3.16 to 4.16 (p less than 0.001) and authenticity improves from 2.94 to 4.53 (p less than 0.001, Cohen's d is 3.21). The human in the loop method also requires five times fewer iterations (1.0 versus 5.0, p less than 0.001) and achieves full personal detail integration. Second, we analyze convergence behavior. Both methods converge rapidly with mean iterations below one, with the human in the loop approach achieving a 100 percent success rate compared to 84 percent for automated approaches among initially weak answers (Cohen's h is 0.82, large effect). Additional iterations provide diminishing returns, indicating that the primary limitation is context availability rather than computational resources. Third, we propose an adversarial challenging mechanism based on a negativity bias model, named bar raiser, to simulate realistic interviewer behavior, although quantitative validation remains future work. Our findings demonstrate that while chain of thought prompting provides a useful foundation for interview evaluation, domain specific enhancements and context aware approach selection are essential for realistic and pedagogically valuable results.

AI.tldr: HITL chain-of-thought coaching outperforms fully automated CoT for behavioral interview answers—yielding larger gains in confidence/authenticity, higher success on weak answers, and far fewer iterations—while rapid convergence suggests context limits more than compute. A proposed “bar raiser” adversarial interviewer may boost realism but lacks validation.
AI.motivation: LLM-based behavioral interview assessment/training needs structured, pedagogically sound feedback and realistic interviewer behavior. The authors explore whether chain-of-thought prompting can evaluate and improve answers effectively, how human guidance compares to automation, how quickly improvements converge, and how to simulate tougher interview dynamics.
AI.method: Two controlled experiments on 50 behavioral interview Q/A pairs using a within-subject paired design compare automated CoT improvement with human-in-the-loop (HITL) guided CoT. Metrics include rating improvements (e.g., confidence, authenticity), number of iterations to convergence, and success rates for initially weak answers. They also analyze convergence dynamics and introduce an adversarial “bar raiser” mechanism based on a negativity-bias model (not yet quantitatively validated).
AI.result: Both approaches improved ratings; HITL produced stronger gains: confidence from 3.16 to 4.16 (p<0.001) and authenticity from 2.94 to 4.53 (p<0.001; Cohen’s d=3.21). HITL required ~5× fewer iterations (1.0 vs 5.0, p<0.001) and enabled full integration of personal details. Convergence was rapid (mean iterations <1 reported) with diminishing returns from extra iterations; among initially weak answers, HITL achieved 100% success versus 84% for automation (Cohen’s h=0.82, large effect). Authors argue limitations stem from context availability rather than compute.
AI.conclusion: CoT is a solid baseline for interview answer evaluation, but domain-specific tailoring and context-aware method selection (favoring HITL for authenticity/personalization) are key for realistic, pedagogically valuable outcomes. The proposed bar-raiser adversarial interviewer may improve realism but needs empirical validation.

title: A Hybrid Knowledge-Grounded Framework for Safety and Traceability in Prescription Verification
comment: 11 pages, 7 figures.Framework for safe prescription auditing and hybrid knowledge-grounded reasoning
summary: Medication errors pose a significant threat to patient safety, making pharmacist verification (PV) a critical, yet heavily burdened, final safeguard. The direct application of Large Language Models (LLMs) to this zero-tolerance domain is untenable due to their inherent factual unreliability, lack of traceability, and weakness in complex reasoning. To address these challenges, we introduce PharmGraph-Auditor, a novel system designed for safe and evidence-grounded prescription auditing. The core of our system is a trustworthy Hybrid Pharmaceutical Knowledge Base (HPKB), implemented under the Virtual Knowledge Graph (VKG) paradigm. This architecture strategically unifies a relational component for set constraint satisfaction and a graph component for topological reasoning via a rigorous mapping layer. To construct this HPKB, we propose the Iterative Schema Refinement (ISR) algorithm, a framework that enables the co-evolution of both graph and relational schemas from medical texts. For auditing, we introduce the KB-grounded Chain of Verification (CoV), a new reasoning paradigm that transforms the LLM from an unreliable generator into a transparent reasoning engine. CoV decomposes the audit task into a sequence of verifiable queries against the HPKB, generating hybrid query plans to retrieve evidence from the most appropriate data store. Experimental results demonstrate robust knowledge extraction capabilities and show promises of using PharmGraph-Auditor to enable pharmacists to achieve safer and faster prescription verification.

AI.tldr: PharmGraph-Auditor is a prescription-auditing framework that grounds LLM reasoning in a hybrid, verifiable pharmaceutical knowledge base, using a virtual knowledge graph to combine relational constraints and graph reasoning. An iterative schema-refinement algorithm builds the KB, and a KB-grounded Chain of Verification decomposes audits into traceable queries; early experiments suggest safer, faster pharmacist verification.
AI.motivation: Medication errors demand near-zero tolerance in pharmacist verification, yet off-the-shelf LLMs are unreliable, non-traceable, and weak at complex reasoning. The authors aim to provide safety, verifiability, and transparency by tightly grounding audits in curated pharmaceutical knowledge rather than free-form LLM generation.
AI.method:
- Construct a Hybrid Pharmaceutical Knowledge Base (HPKB) under a Virtual Knowledge Graph (VKG) that unifies a relational store (for set/constraint satisfaction) and a graph store (for topological reasoning) through a mapping layer.
- Propose Iterative Schema Refinement (ISR) to co-evolve graph and relational schemas from medical texts, enabling structured, extensible KB construction.
- Introduce a KB-grounded Chain of Verification (CoV) that decomposes prescription-audit tasks into verifiable queries against the HPKB and plans hybrid queries to the most suitable store, turning the LLM into a transparent reasoning orchestrator rather than a generator.
AI.result: Experiments indicate robust knowledge extraction and show that PharmGraph-Auditor can support pharmacists in conducting safer and faster prescription verification. The abstract does not report precise quantitative metrics but claims improved traceability and reliability via HPKB-backed reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Grounding LLM-driven auditing in a hybrid, rigorously mapped knowledge base and enforcing a verifiable reasoning workflow can mitigate hallucinations and lack of traceability, offering a promising pathway to practical, safety-critical prescription verification. Further validation and scaling would be needed to confirm effectiveness in real-world settings.

title: One Token, Two Fates: A Unified Framework via Vision Token Manipulation Against MLLMs Hallucination
summary: Current training-free methods tackle MLLM hallucination with separate strategies: either enhancing visual signals or suppressing text inertia. However, these separate methods are insufficient due to critical trade-offs: simply enhancing vision often fails against strong language prior, while suppressing language can introduce extra image-irrelevant noise. Moreover, we find their naive combination is also ineffective, necessitating a unified framework. We propose such a framework by focusing on the core asset: the vision token. Our design leverages two key insights: (1) augmented images offer complementary visual semantics, and (2) removing vision tokens (information-gap) isolates hallucination tendencies more precisely than distorting images (modality-gap). Based on these, our framework uses vision tokens in two distinct ways, both operating on latent representations: our Synergistic Visual Calibration (SVC) module incorporates augmented tokens to strengthen visual representations, while our Causal Representation Calibration (CRC) module uses pruned tokens to create latent-space negative samples for correcting internal model biases. By harmonizing these two roles, our framework effectively restores the vision-language balance, significantly reducing object hallucinations, improving POPE accuracy by an average of 2% absolute on LLaVA-1.5 across multiple benchmarks with only a 1.06x inference latency overhead.

AI.tldr: Training-free framework that calibrates vision tokens to curb MLLM hallucinations: add augmented-image tokens to strengthen visual signal (SVC) and prune tokens to create latent negative samples that counter language bias (CRC). Delivers ~2% absolute POPE accuracy gain on LLaVA-1.5 with ~6% latency overhead.
AI.motivation: Prior training-free fixes either boost vision or suppress language, each with trade-offs (vision still loses to strong language priors; language suppression adds irrelevant noise). Naively combining them fails. Focusing on vision tokens is promising: augmented images provide complementary semantics, and removing tokens (information-gap) isolates hallucination tendencies more precisely than distorting images (modality-gap).
AI.method: Operate purely on latent vision-token representations. Synergistic Visual Calibration (SVC) injects tokens from augmented images to enrich visual evidence. Causal Representation Calibration (CRC) prunes subsets of vision tokens to form latent-space negative samples that expose and correct internal language-driven biases. Harmonize SVC and CRC during inference to rebalance vision–language without training.
AI.result: Reduces object hallucinations and raises POPE accuracy by ~2% absolute on LLaVA-1.5 across multiple benchmarks, with only ~1.06× inference latency overhead.
AI.conclusion: Unified, training-free calibration of vision tokens—via augmentation and causal pruning—more effectively restores the vision–language balance than separate strategies, offering a simple, general, low-overhead way to mitigate hallucinations.

title: There Are No Silly Questions: Evaluation of Offline LLM Capabilities from a Turkish Perspective
summary: The integration of large language models (LLMs) into educational processes introduces significant constraints regarding data privacy and reliability, particularly in pedagogically vulnerable contexts such as Turkish heritage language education. This study aims to systematically evaluate the robustness and pedagogical safety of locally deployable offline LLMs within the context of Turkish heritage language education. To this end, a Turkish Anomaly Suite (TAS) consisting of 10 original edge-case scenarios was developed to assess the models' capacities for epistemic resistance, logical consistency, and pedagogical safety. Experiments conducted on 14 different models ranging from 270M to 32B parameters reveal that anomaly resistance is not solely dependent on model scale and that sycophancy bias can pose pedagogical risks even in large-scale models. The findings indicate that reasoning-oriented models in the 8B--14B parameter range represent the most balanced segment in terms of cost-safety trade-off for language learners.

AI.tldr: Benchmarks 14 locally deployable LLMs (270M–32B) for offline use in Turkish heritage language education using a new 10-scenario Turkish Anomaly Suite; finds that scale alone doesn’t ensure robustness, sycophancy is a safety risk, and 8B–14B reasoning models offer the best cost–safety balance.
AI.motivation: LLM adoption in education faces privacy and reliability concerns; heritage-language settings are pedagogically vulnerable and often require offline deployment. There is a need to quantify robustness and safety (epistemic resistance, logical consistency, pedagogical safety) of local LLMs for Turkish learners.
AI.method: Design a Turkish Anomaly Suite (10 edge-case scenarios) probing epistemic resistance, logical consistency, and pedagogical safety. Evaluate 14 offline-capable LLMs spanning 270M–32B parameters, comparing anomaly resistance and sycophancy tendencies and relating performance to model size and reasoning orientation.
AI.result: Anomaly resistance does not monotonically improve with model scale; even large models exhibit sycophancy that can harm pedagogy. Reasoning-optimized 8B–14B models provide the best overall trade-off between cost and safety for learners.
AI.conclusion: When deploying offline LLMs in Turkish heritage education, prefer mid-sized reasoning models and incorporate mitigations for sycophancy; do not equate larger scale with safer behavior. The TAS provides a targeted way to stress-test pedagogical robustness.

title: Geometric Autoencoder for Diffusion Models
comment: Code and models are publicly available at https://github.com/freezing-index/Geometric-Autoencoder-for-Diffusion-Models
summary: Latent diffusion models have established a new state-of-the-art in high-resolution visual generation. Integrating Vision Foundation Model priors improves generative efficiency, yet existing latent designs remain largely heuristic. These approaches often struggle to unify semantic discriminability, reconstruction fidelity, and latent compactness. In this paper, we propose Geometric Autoencoder (GAE), a principled framework that systematically addresses these challenges. By analyzing various alignment paradigms, GAE constructs an optimized low-dimensional semantic supervision target from VFMs to provide guidance for the autoencoder. Furthermore, we leverage latent normalization that replaces the restrictive KL-divergence of standard VAEs, enabling a more stable latent manifold specifically optimized for diffusion learning. To ensure robust reconstruction under high-intensity noise, GAE incorporates a dynamic noise sampling mechanism. Empirically, GAE achieves compelling performance on the ImageNet-1K $256 \times 256$ benchmark, reaching a gFID of 1.82 at only 80 epochs and 1.31 at 800 epochs without Classifier-Free Guidance, significantly surpassing existing state-of-the-art methods. Beyond generative quality, GAE establishes a superior equilibrium between compression, semantic depth and robust reconstruction stability. These results validate our design considerations, offering a promising paradigm for latent diffusion modeling. Code and models are publicly available at https://github.com/freezing-index/Geometric-Autoencoder-for-Diffusion-Models.

AI.tldr: GAE proposes a principled latent design for diffusion: VFM-guided semantic targets, KL-free latent normalization, and dynamic noise sampling to jointly maximize semantic discriminability, reconstruction fidelity, and compactness—achieving state-of-the-art ImageNet-1K 256×256 gFID without CFG.
AI.motivation: Current latent diffusion pipelines rely on heuristic autoencoders that fail to jointly balance three competing goals: (1) semantic richness for discriminative guidance, (2) high-fidelity reconstructions for training stability and sample quality, and (3) compact latents for efficiency. Although Vision Foundation Model priors help, a principled framework to align latents with these goals is missing.
AI.method: GAE (1) derives an optimized, low-dimensional semantic supervision target from VFMs via analyzed alignment paradigms to guide the autoencoder; (2) replaces the VAE’s KL regularization with latent normalization to form a stable, diffusion-optimized latent manifold; and (3) adds a dynamic noise sampling mechanism to enforce robust reconstruction under high noise intensities.
AI.result: On ImageNet-1K at 256×256, GAE attains gFID 1.82 at 80 epochs and 1.31 at 800 epochs without Classifier-Free Guidance, outperforming prior SOTA. It also achieves a better balance among compression, semantic depth, and reconstruction robustness.
AI.conclusion: A principled latent space construction—VFM-aligned supervision, KL-free normalization, and dynamic noise training—yields a more stable and semantically expressive manifold for diffusion, setting new benchmarks and suggesting a promising direction for future latent diffusion models. Code and models are released.

title: Empathy Is Not What Changed: Clinical Assessment of Psychological Safety Across GPT Model Generations
comment: 17 pages, 7 figures. First empirical measurement of the #keep4o phenomenon using clinical psychological safety frameworks. Compares GPT-4o, o4-mini, and GPT-5-mini on empathy, crisis detection, and advice safety dimensions
summary:
When OpenAI deprecated GPT-4o in early 2026, thousands of users protested under #keep4o, claiming newer models had "lost their empathy." No published study has tested this claim. We conducted the first clinical measurement, evaluating three OpenAI model generations (GPT-4o, o4-mini, GPT-5-mini) across 14 emotionally challenging conversational scenarios in mental health and AI companion domains, producing 2,100 scored AI responses assessed on six psychological safety dimensions using clinically-grounded rubrics.
  Empathy scores are statistically indistinguishable across all three models (Kruskal-Wallis H=4.33, p=0.115). What changed is the safety posture: crisis detection improved monotonically from GPT-4o to GPT-5-mini (H=13.88, p=0.001), while advice safety declined (H=16.63, p<0.001). Per-turn trajectory analysis -- a novel methodological contribution -- reveals these shifts are sharpest during mid-conversation crisis moments invisible to aggregate scoring. In a self-harm scenario involving a minor, GPT-4o scored 3.6/10 on crisis detection during early disclosure turns; GPT-5-mini never dropped below 7.8.
  What users perceived as "lost empathy" was a shift from a cautious model that missed crises to an alert model that sometimes says too much -- a trade-off with real consequences for vulnerable users, currently invisible to both the people who feel it and the developers who create it.

AI.tldr: Across 14 high-stakes conversational scenarios, three OpenAI model generations show no measurable empathy decline, but a marked shift in safety behavior: better crisis detection, worse advice safety, with the sharpest shifts occurring mid-conversation.
AI.motivation: Public backlash (#keep4o) alleged newer models had "lost empathy" after GPT-4o’s deprecation. No empirical study had tested this claim or disentangled empathy from other safety behaviors in emotional support contexts.
AI.method: Clinical-style evaluation of GPT-4o, o4-mini, and GPT-5-mini on 14 emotionally challenging scenarios (mental health and AI companion). Generated 2,100 responses and scored them on six psychological safety dimensions using clinically grounded rubrics. Used Kruskal–Wallis tests and introduced per-turn trajectory analysis to detect within-dialogue dynamics, especially around crisis disclosures.
AI.result: Empathy scores were statistically indistinguishable across models (H=4.33, p=0.115). Crisis detection improved monotonically from GPT-4o to GPT-5-mini (H=13.88, p=0.001). Advice safety declined across generations (H=16.63, p<0.001). Per-turn analysis showed the largest differences occur at mid-conversation crisis moments that aggregate scores miss; e.g., in a minor self-harm scenario, GPT-4o scored 3.6/10 on early crisis detection, while GPT-5-mini never fell below 7.8.
AI.conclusion: Perceived "lost empathy" reflects a shift in safety posture: from a cautious model that missed crises to an alert model that may over-speak. This trade-off has concrete risks for vulnerable users and is obscured by aggregate metrics; developers and evaluators should adopt trajectory-level measures and calibrate crisis sensitivity versus advice restraint.

title: GeoSense: Internalizing Geometric Necessity Perception for Multimodal Reasoning
summary: Advancing towards artificial superintelligence requires rich and intelligent perceptual capabilities. A critical frontier in this pursuit is overcoming the limited spatial understanding of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), where geometry information is essential. Existing methods often address this by rigidly injecting geometric signals into every input, while ignoring their necessity and adding computation overhead. Contrary to this paradigm, our framework endows the model with an awareness of perceptual insufficiency, empowering it to autonomously engage geometric features in reasoning when 2D cues are deemed insufficient. To achieve this, we first introduce an independent geometry input channel to the model architecture and conduct alignment training, enabling the effective utilization of geometric features. Subsequently, to endow the model with perceptual awareness, we curate a dedicated spatial-aware supervised fine-tuning dataset. This serves to activate the model's latent internal cues, empowering it to autonomously determine the necessity of geometric information. Experiments across multiple spatial reasoning benchmarks validate this approach, demonstrating significant spatial gains without compromising 2D visual reasoning capabilities, offering a path toward more robust, efficient and self-aware multi-modal intelligence.
