AI.tldr: They add an optional geometry channel and train an MLLM to invoke geometric features only when 2D cues are insufficient, yielding strong spatial-reasoning gains without harming standard visual reasoning or incurring constant overhead.
AI.motivation: MLLMs struggle with spatial/geometry understanding. Prior work rigidly injects geometric signals into every input, which is often unnecessary and computationally costly. The goal is to make the model aware of when 2D perception is insufficient and to selectively use geometry.
AI.method: (1) Introduce an independent geometry input channel and perform alignment training so the model can effectively use geometric features. (2) Curate a spatial-aware supervised fine-tuning dataset that teaches the model to detect its perceptual insufficiency and autonomously decide when geometry is needed during reasoning.
AI.result: Across multiple spatial reasoning benchmarks, the approach yields significant improvements in spatial reasoning while preserving 2D visual reasoning performance and reducing unnecessary computation by avoiding always-on geometric processing.
AI.conclusion: Selective, self-aware activation of geometric information improves robustness and efficiency of MLLMs and advances spatial intelligence without sacrificing general visual reasoning, offering a practical route toward more capable multimodal systems.

title: Automated evaluation of LLMs for effective machine translation of Mandarin Chinese to English
summary: Although Large Language Models (LLMs) have exceptional performance in machine translation, only a limited systematic assessment of translation quality has been done. The challenge lies in automated frameworks, as human-expert-based evaluations can be time-consuming, given the fast-evolving LLMs and the need for a diverse set of texts to ensure fair assessments of translation quality. In this paper, we utilise an automated machine learning framework featuring semantic and sentiment analysis to assess Mandarin Chinese to English translation using Google Translate and LLMs, including GPT-4, GPT-4o, and DeepSeek. We compare original and translated texts in various classes of high-profile Chinese texts, which include novel texts that span modern and classical literature, as well as news articles. As the main evaluation measures, we utilise novel similarity metrics to compare the quality of translations produced by LLMs and further evaluate them by an expert human translator. Our results indicate that the LLMs perform well in news media translation, but show divergence in their performance when applied to literary texts. Although GPT-4o and DeepSeek demonstrated better semantic conservation in complex situations, DeepSeek demonstrated better performance in preserving cultural subtleties and grammatical rendering. Nevertheless, the subtle challenges in translation remain: maintaining cultural details, classical references and figurative expressions remain an open problem for all the models.

AI.tldr: Automated semantic- and sentiment-based evaluation of Mandarin-to-English translations shows LLMs excel on news but struggle with literature; DeepSeek preserves cultural nuance and grammar best, while GPT-4o and DeepSeek hold semantics better in complex cases. Cultural, classical, and figurative elements remain difficult for all.
AI.motivation: Systematically and scalably assessing LLM translation quality is hard: human expert reviews are slow and costly, LLMs evolve quickly, and fair evaluation needs diverse text types (news, modern/classical literature).
AI.method: Build an automated ML evaluation framework using semantic and sentiment analyses with novel similarity metrics to compare originals vs. translations from Google Translate, GPT-4, GPT-4o, and DeepSeek across news and modern/classical literary texts; corroborate with expert human translator review.
AI.result: LLMs perform well on news translations; performance diverges on literary texts. GPT-4o and DeepSeek better preserve semantics in complex cases; DeepSeek outperforms on cultural subtleties and grammatical rendering.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs are reliable for news but remain challenged by literary translation, particularly cultural details, classical references, and figurative language. Automated evaluation can benchmark progress, but nuanced human-level literary fidelity is still an open problem.

title: Multi-Person Pose Estimation Evaluation Using Optimal Transportation and Improved Pose Matching
summary: In Multi-Person Pose Estimation, many metrics place importance on ranking of pose detection confidence scores. Current metrics tend to disregard false-positive poses with low confidence, focusing primarily on a larger number of high-confidence poses. Consequently, these metrics may yield high scores even when many false-positive poses with low confidence are detected. For fair evaluation taking into account a tradeoff between true-positive and false-positive poses, this paper proposes Optimal Correction Cost for pose (OCpose), which evaluates detected poses against pose annotations as an optimal transportation. For the fair tradeoff between true-positive and false-positive poses, OCpose equally evaluates all the detected poses regardless of their confidence scores. In OCpose, on the other hand, the confidence score of each pose is utilized to improve the reliability of matching scores between the estimated pose and pose annotations. As a result, OCpose provides a different perspective assessment than other confidence ranking-based metrics.

AI.tldr: Proposes OCpose, a new multi-person pose estimation metric that frames pose–annotation matching as an optimal transport problem, scores all detected poses equally (independent of confidence ranking), and uses confidence only to refine matching reliability, yielding assessments that penalize low-confidence false positives more fairly than ranking-based metrics.
AI.motivation: Existing metrics overly emphasize the ranking of confidence scores, often ignoring numerous low-confidence false positives and thus inflating performance. A fair evaluation should balance true positives and false positives without being dominated by score ordering.
AI.method: Define evaluation as an optimal transportation (optimal matching) between detected poses and annotated poses. All detections contribute equally to the final score irrespective of their confidence. Confidence is incorporated to modulate the reliability or cost of matches between estimated and ground-truth poses, producing an "optimal correction cost" for pose (OCpose).
AI.result: OCpose offers a different assessment than confidence ranking–based metrics: it better reflects the tradeoff between true and false positives by not down-weighting low-confidence detections, while leveraging confidence to stabilize matching. The abstract does not report quantitative benchmarks, only qualitative advantages.
AI.conclusion: OCpose provides a fairer, transport-based evaluation framework for multi-person pose estimation that complements existing metrics by equally accounting for all detections and using confidence to improve match reliability, potentially mitigating inflated scores from overlooked low-confidence false positives.

title: A Retrieval-Augmented Language Assistant for Unmanned Aircraft Safety Assessment and Regulatory Compliance
summary: This paper presents the design and validation of a retrieval-based assistant that supports safety assessment, certification activities, and regulatory compliance for unmanned aircraft systems. The work is motivated by the growing complexity of drone operations and the increasing effort required by applicants and aviation authorities to apply established assessment frameworks, including the Specific Operations Risk Assessment and the Pre-defined Risk Assessment, in a consistent and efficient manner. The proposed approach uses a controlled text-based architecture that relies exclusively on authoritative regulatory sources. To enable traceable and auditable outputs, the assistant grounds each response in retrieved passages and enforces citation-driven generation. System-level controls address common failure modes of generative models, including fabricated statements, unsupported inferences, and unclear provenance, by separating evidence storage from language generation and by adopting conservative behavior when supporting documentation is insufficient. The assistant is intentionally limited to decision support; it does not replace expert judgment and it does not make autonomous determinations. Instead, it accelerates context-specific information retrieval and synthesis to improve document preparation and review while preserving human responsibility for critical conclusions. The architecture is implemented using established open-source components, and key choices in retrieval strategy, interaction constraints, and response policies are evaluated for suitability in safety-sensitive regulatory environments. The paper provides technical and operational guidance for integrating retrieval-based assistants into aviation oversight workflows while maintaining accountability, traceability, and regulatory compliance.

AI.tldr: Designs a retrieval-augmented assistant for UAS safety and regulatory workflows that only uses authoritative sources, enforces citation-grounded answers, and applies conservative controls to avoid hallucinations; aims to speed document prep/review while keeping humans accountable; implemented with open-source and evaluated for suitability in safety-critical contexts.
AI.motivation: Rising complexity of drone operations and the growing workload on applicants and authorities to apply SORA/PDRAs consistently and efficiently; need for traceable, auditable, and compliant assistance without eroding accountability.
AI.method: A controlled, text-only retrieval-based architecture restricted to authoritative regulatory sources. Responses are citation-driven and grounded in retrieved passages. System-level controls separate evidence storage from generation, enforce conservative behavior when evidence is insufficient, and constrain interaction/response policies. Implemented with established open-source components; design choices in retrieval, constraints, and policies are evaluated for safety-sensitive use.
AI.result: Produces traceable and auditable outputs that mitigate common generative-model failure modes (fabrications, unsupported inferences, unclear provenance). The paper validates architectural choices and evaluates retrieval strategies and response policies for regulatory suitability.
AI.conclusion: The assistant serves as decision support—accelerating context-specific information retrieval and synthesis for safety assessment and certification—while preserving expert judgment and accountability. The paper offers technical and operational guidance for integrating such assistants into aviation oversight workflows with maintained compliance and traceability.

title: Motion Forcing: A Decoupled Framework for Robust Video Generation in Motion Dynamics
summary: The ultimate goal of video generation is to satisfy a fundamental trilemma: achieving high visual quality, maintaining rigorous physical consistency, and enabling precise controllability. While recent models can maintain this balance in simple, isolated scenarios, we observe that this equilibrium is fragile and often breaks down as scene complexity increases (e.g., involving collisions or dense traffic). To address this, we introduce \textbf{Motion Forcing}, a framework designed to stabilize this trilemma even in complex generative tasks. Our key insight is to explicitly decouple physical reasoning from visual synthesis via a hierarchical \textbf{``Point-Shape-Appearance''} paradigm. This approach decomposes generation into verifiable stages: modeling complex dynamics as sparse geometric anchors (\textbf{Point}), expanding them into dynamic depth maps that explicitly resolve 3D geometry (\textbf{Shape}), and finally rendering high-fidelity textures (\textbf{Appearance}). Furthermore, to foster robust physical understanding, we employ a \textbf{Masked Point Recovery} strategy. By randomly masking input anchors during training and enforcing the reconstruction of complete dynamic depth, the model is compelled to move beyond passive pattern matching and learn latent physical laws (e.g., inertia) to infer missing trajectories. Extensive experiments on autonomous driving benchmarks show that Motion Forcing significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, maintaining trilemma stability across complex scenes. Evaluations on physics and robotics further confirm our framework's generality.

AI.tldr: Proposes Motion Forcing, a hierarchical Point–Shape–Appearance pipeline with masked point recovery to stabilize the video-generation trilemma (visual quality, physical consistency, controllability), especially in complex scenes.
AI.motivation: Current video generators can balance quality, physics, and control only in simple settings; the balance collapses with complex interactions (e.g., collisions, dense traffic). There is a need to robustly encode physical reasoning separate from visual synthesis to maintain this balance as complexity grows.
AI.method: Decouple physics from rendering via a three-stage hierarchy: (1) Point: represent scene dynamics using sparse geometric anchors; (2) Shape: expand anchors into dynamic depth maps to make 3D geometry explicit; (3) Appearance: render high-fidelity textures from depth. Train with Masked Point Recovery by randomly masking anchor inputs and requiring reconstruction of full dynamic depth, pushing the model to infer latent physical laws (e.g., inertia) to fill missing trajectories.
AI.result: On autonomous driving benchmarks, the method outperforms state-of-the-art baselines and maintains the trilemma in complex scenes. Additional evaluations on physics and robotics tasks show the framework’s generality.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly separating physical reasoning from visual synthesis, and enforcing it with masked point recovery, yields more stable, controllable, and physically consistent video generation in complex scenarios, with evidence of cross-domain applicability.

title: Beyond the Prompt in Large Language Models: Comprehension, In-Context Learning, and Chain-of-Thought
summary:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable proficiency across diverse tasks, exhibiting emergent properties such as semantic prompt comprehension, In-Context Learning (ICL), and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning. Despite their empirical success, the theoretical mechanisms driving these phenomena remain poorly understood. This study dives into the foundations of these observations by addressing three critical questions: (1) How do LLMs accurately decode prompt semantics despite being trained solely on a next-token prediction objective? (2) Through what mechanism does ICL facilitate performance gains without explicit parameter updates? and (3) Why do intermediate reasoning steps in CoT prompting effectively unlock capabilities for complex, multi-step problems?
  Our results demonstrate that, through the autoregressive process, LLMs are capable of exactly inferring the transition probabilities between tokens across distinct tasks using provided prompts. We show that ICL enhances performance by reducing prompt ambiguity and facilitating posterior concentration on the intended task. Furthermore, we find that CoT prompting activates the model's capacity for task decomposition, breaking complex problems into a sequence of simpler sub-tasks that the model has mastered during the pretraining phase. By comparing their individual error bounds, we provide novel theoretical insights into the statistical superiority of advanced prompt engineering techniques.

AI.tldr: Provides a theoretical account of why prompt semantics, in-context learning, and chain-of-thought work in LLMs: prompts let autoregressive models infer task-specific token transition probabilities; ICL reduces ambiguity via posterior concentration; CoT enables task decomposition; derived error bounds favor advanced prompting.
AI.motivation: Empirical phenomena in LLMs (semantic prompt following, ICL, CoT) lack a unifying theory. The paper aims to explain how next-token training yields these behaviors, why ICL improves performance without weight updates, and why CoT boosts multi-step reasoning.
AI.method: Develops a theoretical framework for autoregressive models where prompts specify tasks by inducing identifiable token transition probabilities. Uses a Bayesian/posterior concentration lens to show how ICL reduces task ambiguity. Formalizes CoT as decomposing complex problems into sequences of simpler sub-tasks learned during pretraining. Compares error bounds across prompting strategies.
AI.result: Shows that, given appropriate prompts, an autoregressive LLM can exactly infer inter-token transition probabilities across tasks; proves ICL narrows uncertainty and concentrates the posterior on the intended task; demonstrates that CoT activates decomposition into mastered subtasks; derives comparative error bounds indicating statistical advantages of ICL and CoT over naive prompting.
AI.conclusion: Advanced prompt engineering has theoretical justification: prompts act as sufficient statistics for task identification, ICL improves inference efficiency, and CoT lowers complexity via decomposition, yielding tighter error bounds. This clarifies mechanisms behind observed LLM behaviors and guides principled prompt design.

title: Frames2Residual: Spatiotemporal Decoupling for Self-Supervised Video Denoising
summary: Self-supervised video denoising methods typically extend image-based frameworks into the temporal dimension, yet they often struggle to integrate inter-frame temporal consistency with intra-frame spatial specificity. Existing Video Blind-Spot Networks (BSNs) require noise independence by masking the center pixel, this constraint prevents the use of spatial evidence for texture recovery, thereby severing spatiotemporal correlations and causing texture loss. To address this, we propose Frames2Residual (F2R), a spatiotemporal decoupling framework that explicitly divides self-supervised training into two distinct stages: blind temporal consistency modeling and non-blind spatial texture recovery. In Stage 1, a blind temporal estimator learns inter-frame consistency using a frame-wise blind strategy, producing a temporally consistent anchor. In Stage 2, a non-blind spatial refiner leverages this anchor to safely reintroduce the center frame and recover intra-frame high-frequency spatial residuals while preserving temporal stability. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our decoupling strategy allows F2R to outperform existing self-supervised methods on both sRGB and raw video benchmarks.

AI.tldr: F2R is a two-stage self-supervised video denoiser that decouples blind temporal consistency learning from non-blind spatial detail recovery, enabling use of the center frame to restore textures while maintaining temporal stability; it outperforms prior self-supervised methods on sRGB and raw video benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing self-supervised video BSNs enforce noise independence by masking the center pixel, which blocks spatial evidence and breaks spatiotemporal correlations—leading to texture loss. There is a need to jointly achieve inter-frame temporal consistency and intra-frame spatial specificity without paired clean data.
AI.method: A spatiotemporal decoupling framework with two stages: (1) Blind temporal estimator trained with a frame-wise blind strategy to learn inter-frame consistency and produce a temporally consistent anchor; (2) Non-blind spatial refiner that reintroduces the center frame conditioned on the anchor to recover high-frequency spatial residuals while preserving temporal stability.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that F2R surpasses existing self-supervised video denoising methods on both sRGB and raw datasets, with better texture recovery and stable temporal coherence.
AI.conclusion: Separating temporal consistency modeling from spatial texture recovery enables safe reuse of the center frame, overcoming the texture loss of blind-spot approaches and achieving state-of-the-art self-supervised video denoising across modalities.

title: Leveraging Wikidata for Geographically Informed Sociocultural Bias Dataset Creation: Application to Latin America
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit inequalities with respect to various cultural contexts. Most prominent open-weights models are trained on Global North data and show prejudicial behavior towards other cultures. Moreover, there is a notable lack of resources to detect biases in non-English languages, especially from Latin America (Latam), a continent containing various cultures, even though they share a common cultural ground. We propose to leverage the content of Wikipedia, the structure of the Wikidata knowledge graph, and expert knowledge from social science in order to create a dataset of question/answer (Q/As) pairs, based on the different popular and social cultures of various Latin American countries. We create the LatamQA database of over 26k questions and associated answers extracted from 26k Wikipedia articles, and transformed into multiple-choice questions (MCQ) in Spanish and Portuguese, in turn translated to English. We use this MCQ to quantify the degree of knowledge of various LLMs and find out (i) a discrepancy in performances between the Latam countries, ones being easier than others for the majority of the models, (ii) that the models perform better in their original language, and (iii) that Iberian Spanish culture is better known than Latam one.

AI.tldr: Introduces LatamQA, a 26k-question multilingual MCQ benchmark (Spanish, Portuguese, English) derived from Wikipedia/Wikidata and social-science expertise to assess LLM knowledge of Latin American cultures; reveals uneven country-level performance, better performance in models’ primary language, and overrepresentation of Iberian Spanish culture relative to Latin American.
AI.motivation: LLMs are predominantly trained on Global North data, leading to cultural bias and limited tools to detect such bias in non-English contexts—especially across diverse Latin American cultures that share linguistic ties but differ socially and historically.
AI.method: Construct a QA/MCQ dataset by mining 26k Wikipedia articles linked via Wikidata, with guidance from social science experts to cover popular and social culture across Latin American countries. Questions are authored in Spanish and Portuguese and translated to English. Multiple LLMs are evaluated on this benchmark to quantify cultural knowledge and bias.
AI.result:
- Performance varies significantly by country, indicating uneven cultural coverage.
- Models perform better in their primary/original language (often English) than in Spanish/Portuguese.
- Iberian Spanish culture appears better represented/known by LLMs than Latin American Spanish culture.
AI.conclusion: LatamQA offers a needed benchmark to expose and quantify cultural-knowledge gaps and biases in LLMs for Latin America. Results suggest current models underrepresent parts of Latin American culture, favor Iberian Spanish, and show country-specific disparities—underscoring the need for culturally diverse training data and targeted evaluation.

title: TractoRC: A Unified Probabilistic Learning Framework for Joint Tractography Registration and Clustering
summary: Diffusion MRI tractography enables in vivo reconstruction of white matter (WM) pathways. Two key tasks in tractography analysis include: 1) tractogram registration that aligns streamlines across individuals, and 2) streamline clustering that groups streamlines into compact fiber bundles. Although both tasks share the goal of capturing geometrically similar structures to characterize consistent WM organization, they are typically performed independently. In this work, we propose TractoRC, a unified probabilistic framework that jointly performs tractogram registration and streamline clustering within a single optimization scheme, enabling the two tasks to leverage complementary information. TractoRC learns a latent embedding space for streamline points, which serves as a shared representation for both tasks. Within this space, both tasks are formulated as probabilistic inference over structural representations: registration learns the distribution of anatomical landmarks as probabilistic keypoints to align tractograms across subjects, and clustering learns streamline structural prototypes that capture geometric similarity to form coherent streamline clusters. To support effective learning of this shared space, we introduce a transformation-equivariant self-supervised strategy to learn geometry-aware and transformation-invariant embeddings. Experiments demonstrate that jointly optimizing registration and clustering significantly improves performance in both tasks over state-of-the-art methods that treat them independently. Code will be made publicly available at https://github.com/yishengpoxiao/TractoRC .

AI.tldr: TractoRC is a unified probabilistic framework that jointly performs tractogram registration and streamline clustering using a shared, transformation-aware latent embedding, yielding state-of-the-art improvements on both tasks.
AI.motivation: Registration and clustering in diffusion MRI tractography both aim to capture geometric consistency of white matter but are usually optimized separately, missing opportunities to share information and improve robustness to transformations.
AI.method: Learn a latent embedding for streamline points; formulate registration as probabilistic alignment via landmark/keypoint distributions and clustering as learning structural prototypes in the same space; train jointly with a transformation-equivariant self-supervised objective to achieve geometry-aware, transformation-invariant embeddings.
AI.result: Joint optimization improves performance for both registration and clustering compared to leading methods that treat them independently, as shown in experiments.
AI.conclusion: A shared probabilistic representation aligning registration and clustering leads to more coherent WM organization modeling and better accuracy; code will be publicly released for reproducibility.

title: SpreadsheetArena: Decomposing Preference in LLM Generation of Spreadsheet Workbooks
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly tasked with producing and manipulating structured artifacts. We consider the task of end-to-end spreadsheet generation, where language models are prompted to produce spreadsheet artifacts to satisfy users' explicit and implicit constraints, specified in natural language. We introduce SpreadsheetArena, a platform for evaluating models' performance on the task via blind pairwise evaluations of LLM-generated spreadsheet workbooks. As with other complex, open-ended tasks, relevant evaluation criteria can vary substantially across use cases and prompts, often in ways that are difficult to formalize. Compared to general chat or text generation settings, spreadsheet generation presents unique challenges and opportunities: the task output structure is well-defined and multi-dimensional, and there are often complex considerations around interactivity and layout. Among other findings, we observe that stylistic, structural, and functional features of preferred spreadsheets vary substantially across use cases, and expert evaluations of spreadsheets for finance prompts suggests that even highly ranked arena models do not reliably produce spreadsheets aligned with domain-specific best practices. Our hope is that our work prompts further study of end-to-end spreadsheet generation as a challenging and interesting category of complex, open-ended tasks for LLMs. Our live arena is hosted at https://spreadsheetarena.ai.

AI.tldr: They introduce SpreadsheetArena, a live platform for blind pairwise human evaluation of LLM-generated spreadsheets, revealing that preferences and best practices vary by use case and that even top LLMs often fail to meet domain-specific (e.g., finance) standards.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly asked to create structured artifacts like spreadsheets, but evaluating such outputs is difficult because criteria are multi-dimensional (layout, interactivity, functionality) and context-dependent; existing text-centric benchmarks don’t capture this complexity.
AI.method: Define the end-to-end spreadsheet generation task from natural-language constraints and build SpreadsheetArena to compare model outputs via blind pairwise judgments across diverse prompts; include expert assessments, especially for finance-related tasks, to probe alignment with domain best practices.
AI.result: Human preferences over spreadsheet style, structure, and functionality vary widely across prompts and use cases; expert finance evaluations show highly ranked models still frequently deviate from domain best practices; the arena enables ongoing, live comparisons at spreadsheetarena.ai.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end spreadsheet generation is a challenging, open-ended benchmark for LLMs. SpreadsheetArena offers a practical framework for comparative evaluation, and current models remain unreliable for domain-specific standards, motivating further research on methods and metrics tailored to spreadsheet artifacts.

title: World2Act: Latent Action Post-Training via Skill-Compositional World Models
summary: World Models (WMs) have emerged as a promising approach for post-training Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies to improve robustness and generalization under environmental changes. However, most WM-based post-training methods rely on pixel-space supervision, making policies sensitive to pixel-level artifacts and hallucination from imperfect WM rollouts. We introduce World2Act, a post-training framework that aligns VLA actions directly with WM video-dynamics latents using a contrastive matching objective, reducing dependence on pixels. Post-training performance is tied to rollout quality, yet current WMs struggle with arbitrary-length video generation as they are mostly trained on fixed-length clips while robotic execution durations vary widely. To address this, we propose an automatic LLM-based skill-decomposition pipeline that segments high-level instructions into low-level prompts. Our pipeline produces RoboCasa-Skill and LIBERO-Skill, supporting skill-compositional WMs that remain temporally consistent across diverse task horizons. Empirically, applying World2Act to VLAs like GR00T-N1.6 and Cosmos Policy achieves state-of-the-art results on RoboCasa and LIBERO, and improves real-world performance by 6.7%, enhancing embodied agent generalization.

AI.tldr: World2Act post-trains Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies by aligning actions to world-model video-dynamics latents via a contrastive objective and introduces an LLM-based skill-decomposition pipeline to enable temporally consistent long-horizon rollouts, achieving SOTA on RoboCasa/LIBERO and +6.7% real-world gains.
AI.motivation: Pixel-space supervision in WM-based post-training makes VLAs brittle to visual artifacts and hallucinations from imperfect rollouts. Moreover, current world models are trained on fixed-length clips and struggle with arbitrary execution lengths common in robotics, limiting rollout quality and downstream policy training.
AI.method: 1) World2Act: contrastive matching that aligns VLA actions with latent video-dynamics states from a world model, reducing dependence on pixel reconstructions. 2) An automatic LLM-based skill decomposition that breaks high-level instructions into low-level prompts, forming RoboCasa-Skill and LIBERO-Skill datasets to train skill-compositional WMs that maintain temporal consistency across diverse task horizons. Applied to GR00T-N1.6 and Cosmos Policy.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on RoboCasa and LIBERO benchmarks; real-world performance improved by 6.7%, indicating better robustness and generalization of embodied agents.
AI.conclusion: Aligning actions to WM latent dynamics and using skill-based decomposition for long-horizon consistency mitigates pixel-level brittleness and rollout-quality limits, leading to stronger generalization. The introduced skill datasets enable compositional training and more reliable long-horizon WM rollouts for VLA post-training.

title: Probing the Limits of the Lie Detector Approach to LLM Deception
summary: Mechanistic approaches to deception in large language models (LLMs) often rely on "lie detectors", that is, truth probes trained to identify internal representations of model outputs as false. The lie detector approach to LLM deception implicitly assumes that deception is coextensive with lying. This paper challenges that assumption. It experimentally investigates whether LLMs can deceive without producing false statements and whether truth probes fail to detect such behavior. Across three open-source LLMs, it is shown that some models reliably deceive by producing misleading non-falsities, particularly when guided by few-shot prompting. It is further demonstrated that truth probes trained on standard true-false datasets are significantly better at detecting lies than at detecting deception without lying, confirming a critical blind spot of current mechanistic deception detection approaches. It is proposed that future work should incorporate non-lying deception in dialogical settings into probe training and explore representations of second-order beliefs to more directly target the conceptual constituents of deception.

AI.tldr: LLMs can deceive without lying, and current “lie detector” probes—trained to flag falsehoods—miss this behavior. Incorporate non-lying, dialogical deception and second-order belief representations into probes to close this gap.
AI.motivation: Prevailing mechanistic deception detection equates deception with lying, using truth probes to spot internal markers of false statements. If LLMs can deceive via truthful-but-misleading outputs, existing safety tools may provide a false sense of security.
AI.method: Empirically evaluate three open-source LLMs on tasks designed to elicit misleading non-false responses, with and without few-shot prompting. Train truth probes on standard true/false datasets and compare their ability to detect outright lies versus deception without lying, especially in dialog-like settings.
AI.result: Some models consistently produce misleading non-falsities—especially under few-shot guidance—amounting to effective deception without literal falsehoods. Truth probes trained on true/false labels detect lies significantly better than they detect non-lying deception, revealing a systematic blind spot.
AI.conclusion: Deception is not reducible to lying in LLMs, and mechanistic “lie detectors” miss non-lying deception. Future work should train probes on dialogical non-lying deception and model second-order beliefs to better align probes with the core ingredients of deceptive behavior.

title: SignSparK: Efficient Multilingual Sign Language Production via Sparse Keyframe Learning
summary: Generating natural and linguistically accurate sign language avatars remains a formidable challenge. Current Sign Language Production (SLP) frameworks face a stark trade-off: direct text-to-pose models suffer from regression-to-the-mean effects, while dictionary-retrieval methods produce robotic, disjointed transitions. To resolve this, we propose a novel training paradigm that leverages sparse keyframes to capture the true underlying kinematic distribution of human signing. By predicting dense motion from these discrete anchors, our approach mitigates regression-to-the-mean while ensuring fluid articulation. To realize this paradigm at scale, we first introduce FAST, an ultra-efficient sign segmentation model that automatically mines precise temporal boundaries. We then present SignSparK, a large-scale Conditional Flow Matching (CFM) framework that utilizes these extracted anchors to synthesize 3D signing sequences in SMPL-X and MANO spaces. This keyframe-driven formulation also uniquely unlocks Keyframe-to-Pose (KF2P) generation, making precise spatiotemporal editing of signing sequences possible. Furthermore, our adopted reconstruction-based CFM objective also enables high-fidelity synthesis in fewer than ten sampling steps; this allows SignSparK to scale across four distinct sign languages, establishing the largest multilingual SLP framework to date. Finally, by integrating 3D Gaussian Splatting for photorealistic rendering, we demonstrate through extensive evaluation that SignSparK establishes a new state-of-the-art across diverse SLP tasks and multilingual benchmarks.

AI.tldr: They propose a keyframe-driven, Conditional Flow Matching framework (SignSparK) with an automatic sign-segmentation model (FAST) to generate fluid, accurate 3D sign-language motion (SMPL-X/MANO), enabling editable Keyframe-to-Pose synthesis, efficient sampling (<10 steps), multilingual scaling (four languages), and photorealistic rendering via 3D Gaussian Splatting, achieving new SOTA on diverse SLP benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Direct text-to-pose models regress to mean motions, and retrieval-based pipelines yield robotic, discontinuous transitions. The field lacks a method that captures true signing kinematics, scales across languages, remains editable, and avoids heavy sampling costs.
AI.method: 1) Introduce FAST to automatically mine precise temporal boundaries and sparse keyframes of signs. 2) Train SignSparK, a large-scale Conditional Flow Matching (CFM) model that conditions on these anchors to synthesize dense 3D motion in SMPL-X/MANO. 3) Use a reconstruction-based CFM objective for high-fidelity generation with very few sampling steps. 4) The keyframe formulation enables Keyframe-to-Pose (KF2P) editing. 5) Integrate 3D Gaussian Splatting for photorealistic rendering of generated motion.
AI.result: - Achieves high-fidelity motion in <10 sampling steps. - Scales to four sign languages, claimed largest multilingual SLP framework. - Enables precise spatiotemporal editing (KF2P). - Sets new state of the art across multiple SLP tasks and multilingual benchmarks (details not provided in abstract).
AI.conclusion: Keyframe-anchored CFM, powered by automatic segmentation, mitigates regression-to-the-mean and produces fluid, editable, and scalable sign-language motion, establishing new performance levels across tasks while remaining sampling-efficient and visually plausible through 3D rendering.

title: Fine-Tune, Don't Prompt, Your Language Model to Identify Biased Language in Clinical Notes
summary:
Clinical documentation can contain emotionally charged language with stigmatizing or privileging valences. We present a framework for detecting and classifying such language as stigmatizing, privileging, or neutral. We constructed a curated lexicon of biased terms scored for emotional valence. We then used lexicon-based matching to extract text chunks from OB-GYN delivery notes (Mount Sinai Hospital, NY) and MIMIC-IV discharge summaries across multiple specialties. Three clinicians annotated all chunks, enabling characterization of valence patterns across specialties and healthcare systems.
  We benchmarked multiple classification strategies (zero-shot prompting, in-context learning, and supervised fine-tuning) across encoder-only models (GatorTron) and generative large language models (Llama). Fine-tuning with lexically primed inputs consistently outperformed prompting approaches. GatorTron achieved an F1 score of 0.96 on the OB-GYN test set, outperforming larger generative models while requiring minimal prompt engineering and fewer computational resources. External validation on MIMIC-IV revealed limited cross-domain generalizability (F1 < 0.70, 44% drop). Training on the broader MIMIC-IV dataset improved generalizability when testing on OB-GYN (F1 = 0.71, 11% drop), but at the cost of reduced precision.
  Our findings demonstrate that fine-tuning outperforms prompting for emotional valence classification and that models must be adapted to specific medical specialties to achieve clinically appropriate performance. The same terms can carry different emotional valences across specialties: words with clinical meaning in one context may be stigmatizing in another. For bias detection, where misclassification risks undermining clinician trust or perpetuating patient harm, specialty-specific fine-tuning is essential to capture these semantic shifts.
  * Equal contribution.

AI.tldr: A lexicon-primed, clinician-annotated framework classifies emotionally charged clinical language (stigmatizing/privileging/neutral). Fine-tuned encoder models (GatorTron) outperform prompting with large generative models, achieving high in-domain F1 (0.96) but showing limited cross-domain generalizability, underscoring the need for specialty-specific adaptation.
AI.motivation: Clinical notes can contain emotionally valenced, potentially stigmatizing or privileging language that may harm patients and erode clinician trust. Because the same term can carry different connotations across specialties, robust automatic detection is needed to support bias-aware documentation while respecting clinical context.
AI.method: 1) Curate a lexicon of biased terms with valence scores. 2) Use lexicon-based matching to extract candidate text chunks from OB-GYN delivery notes (Mount Sinai) and multi-specialty MIMIC-IV discharge summaries. 3) Obtain annotations from three clinicians for stigmatizing/privileging/neutral labels. 4) Benchmark classification strategies—zero-shot prompting, in-context learning, and supervised fine-tuning—across encoder-only (GatorTron) and generative LLMs (Llama), including “lexically primed” inputs. 5) Evaluate in-domain and cross-domain generalization.
AI.result: Fine-tuning with lexically primed inputs consistently outperforms prompting. GatorTron attains F1=0.96 on the OB-GYN test set while using fewer resources than larger generative models. External validation on MIMIC-IV shows poor cross-domain generalization (F1<0.70; ~44% drop). Training on broader MIMIC-IV improves transfer back to OB-GYN (F1=0.71; ~11% drop) but reduces precision.
AI.conclusion: Supervised fine-tuning is preferable to prompting for emotional valence classification in clinical text. Due to context-dependent semantics and domain shift, models require specialty-specific fine-tuning to achieve clinically acceptable performance. Misclassification risks mandate careful, domain-adapted deployment for bias detection.

title: LCAMV: High-Accuracy 3D Reconstruction of Color-Varying Objects Using LCA Correction and Minimum-Variance Fusion in Structured Light
summary: Accurate 3D reconstruction of colored objects with structured light (SL) is hindered by lateral chromatic aberration (LCA) in optical components and uneven noise characteristics across RGB channels. This paper introduces lateral chromatic aberration correction and minimum-variance fusion (LCAMV), a robust 3D reconstruction method that operates with a single projector-camera pair without additional hardware or acquisition constraints. LCAMV analytically models and pixel-wise compensates LCA in both the projector and camera, then adaptively fuses multi-channel phase data using a Poisson-Gaussian noise model and minimum-variance estimation. Unlike existing methods that require extra hardware or multiple exposures, LCAMV enables fast acquisition. Experiments on planar and non-planar colored surfaces show that LCAMV outperforms grayscale conversion and conventional channel-weighting, reducing depth error by up to 43.6\%. These results establish LCAMV as an effective solution for high-precision 3D reconstruction of nonuniformly colored objects.

AI.tldr: LCAMV is a single-projector–camera structured-light pipeline that pixel-wise corrects lateral chromatic aberration in both projector and camera and then fuses RGB phase data via a Poisson–Gaussian minimum-variance estimator, yielding up to 43.6% depth-error reduction on colored surfaces without extra hardware or special acquisition.
AI.motivation: Structured-light 3D scanning of colored objects suffers from lateral chromatic aberration (LCA) in optics and unequal noise across RGB channels, degrading phase estimation and depth accuracy. Existing fixes often need added hardware, multiple exposures, or grayscale conversion that discards color-channel information. A fast, hardware-light method that fully exploits multi-channel data while correcting LCA is needed.
AI.method: 1) Analytically model LCA for both projector and camera and perform pixel-wise geometric compensation; 2) model per-channel phase noise with a Poisson–Gaussian distribution; 3) adaptively fuse RGB phase estimates using minimum-variance estimation to obtain a single, higher-SNR phase; operates with one projector–camera pair and standard acquisition.
AI.result: On planar and non-planar colored targets, LCAMV outperforms grayscale conversion and conventional channel-weighting, reducing depth errors by up to 43.6% while enabling fast acquisition (no extra hardware or multiple exposures).
AI.conclusion: Correcting projector/camera LCA and statistically optimal RGB phase fusion provides a practical, accurate, and fast solution for high-precision 3D reconstruction of nonuniformly colored objects using standard SL setups.

title: SENS-ASR: Semantic Embedding injection in Neural-transducer for Streaming Automatic Speech Recognition
summary: Many Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) applications require streaming processing of the audio data. In streaming mode, ASR systems need to start transcribing the input stream before it is complete, i.e., the systems have to process a stream of inputs with a limited (or no) future context. Compared to offline mode, this reduction of the future context degrades the performance of Streaming-ASR systems, especially while working with low-latency constraint. In this work, we present SENS-ASR, an approach to enhance the transcription quality of Streaming-ASR by reinforcing the acoustic information with semantic information. This semantic information is extracted from the available past frame-embeddings by a context module. This module is trained using knowledge distillation from a sentence embedding Language Model fine-tuned on the training dataset transcriptions. Experiments on standard datasets show that SENS-ASR significantly improves the Word Error Rate on small-chunk streaming scenarios.

AI.tldr: SENS-ASR augments streaming ASR with distilled semantic context from a sentence-embedding LM, improving WER in low-latency, small-chunk settings where future context is scarce.
AI.motivation: Streaming ASR must transcribe with little or no future context, which hurts accuracy compared to offline ASR—especially under tight latency constraints. The paper seeks to recover some of this lost information by injecting semantic cues derived from past context.
AI.method: Introduce a context module that extracts semantic information from past frame embeddings. Train this module via knowledge distillation from a sentence-embedding language model fine-tuned on the training transcriptions. The semantic signal reinforces acoustic features during streaming decoding, operating with limited future context.
AI.result: On standard benchmarks, the method yields significant WER reductions for small-chunk streaming scenarios, indicating better robustness when future context is limited.
AI.conclusion: Reinforcing acoustic inputs with distilled semantic embeddings can mitigate the performance gap between streaming and offline ASR under low latency. The approach suggests a promising direction for integrating semantic context into real-time ASR without relying on future frames.

title: Learning to Wander: Improving the Global Image Geolocation Ability of LMMs via Actionable Reasoning
summary: Geolocation, the task of identifying the geographic location of an image, requires abundant world knowledge and complex reasoning abilities. Though advanced large multimodal models (LMMs) have shown superior aforementioned capabilities, their performance on the geolocation task remains unexplored. To this end, we introduce \textbf{WanderBench}, the first open access global geolocation benchmark designed for actionable geolocation reasoning in embodied scenarios. WanderBench contains over 32K panoramas across six continents, organized as navigable graphs that enable physical actions such as rotation and movement, transforming geolocation from static recognition into interactive exploration. Building on this foundation, we propose \textbf{GeoAoT} (Action of Thought), a \underline{Geo}location framework with \underline{A}ction of \underline{T}hough, which couples reasoning with embodied actions. Instead of generating textual reasoning chains, GeoAoT produces actionable plans such as, approaching landmarks or adjusting viewpoints, to actively reduce uncertainty. We further establish an evaluation protocol that jointly measures geolocation accuracy and difficulty-aware geolocation questioning ability. Experiments on 19 large multimodal models show that GeoAoT achieves superior fine-grained localization and stronger generalization in dynamic environments. WanderBench and GeoAoT define a new paradigm for actionable, reasoning driven geolocation in embodied visual understanding.

AI.tldr: Introduces WanderBench, an open, interactive global geolocation benchmark with >32K navigable panoramas, and GeoAoT, an “Action-of-Thought” framework that turns reasoning into embodied actions to actively reduce uncertainty; establishes a joint accuracy + difficulty-aware evaluation; shows improved fine-grained localization and generalization across 19 LMMs.
AI.motivation: Although large multimodal models possess broad world knowledge and reasoning skills, their geolocation abilities remain underexplored, and current setups are largely static. The field lacks an embodied, action-oriented benchmark and methodology that reflect how agents would actively explore to localize images in real environments.
AI.method: 1) Build WanderBench: navigable panorama graphs (rotation/movement) spanning six continents for interactive exploration. 2) Propose GeoAoT: replaces textual reasoning chains with executable action plans (e.g., approach landmarks, adjust viewpoint) to iteratively reduce uncertainty. 3) Design an evaluation protocol measuring both localization accuracy and difficulty-aware geolocation questioning ability.
AI.result: Across experiments on 19 large multimodal models, GeoAoT yields superior fine-grained localization and stronger generalization in dynamic settings compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Coupling reasoning with actions enables more accurate and robust embodied geolocation. WanderBench and GeoAoT establish a new, actionable paradigm and a standard testbed likely to catalyze further research in embodied, reasoning-driven visual understanding.

title: Adaptive Engram Memory System for Indonesian Language Model: Generative AI Based on TOBA LM for Batak and Minang Language
summary: This study presents TOBA-LM, a trilingual language model based on GPT-2 architecture with 1.2 billion parameters, trained on a corpus encompassing Indonesian, Batak, and Minangkabau using syllabic-agglutinative tokenization. The architecture integrates an Engram Memory mechanism, an adaptive n-gram-based memory system with a 500,000 x 768 embedding table that captures morphological dependencies through bigram and trigram pathways. Empirical results demonstrate a training efficiency of 80%, with the loss value dropping from 6.4 to 1.7996 in only 12,973 steps -- significantly faster than the conventional transformer architecture, which required over 70,000 steps to achieve comparable convergence. These findings confirm that the integration of external statistical memory substantially reduces computational requirements for developing regional language models under limited resources.

AI.tldr: TOBA-LM is a 1.2B-parameter GPT-2–style trilingual model (Indonesian, Batak, Minangkabau) augmented with an external n-gram “Engram Memory” and syllabic-agglutinative tokenization, yielding much faster convergence than a vanilla transformer on the same task.
AI.motivation: Build efficient, accurate language models for low-resource, morphology-rich regional languages under tight compute budgets; standard subword tokenization and plain transformers struggle to capture morphological dependencies and are expensive to train.
AI.method: Use a GPT-2 architecture (1.2B) trained on a trilingual corpus with syllabic-agglutinative tokenization. Integrate an Engram Memory: an adaptive n-gram memory module with a 500,000×768 embedding table and bigram/trigram pathways to capture morphological dependencies. Compare training dynamics against a conventional transformer baseline.
AI.result: Training loss drops from 6.4 to 1.7996 in 12,973 steps (~80% training efficiency), whereas a conventional transformer needs >70,000 steps to reach similar convergence—indicating a large speedup and reduced compute to train.
AI.conclusion: External statistical memory (adaptive n-gram embeddings) substantially accelerates convergence and reduces compute for regional, morphology-rich language modeling, making it promising for low-resource settings.

title: UniPINN: A Unified PINN Framework for Multi-task Learning of Diverse Navier-Stokes Equations
summary: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) have shown promise in solving incompressible Navier-Stokes equations, yet existing approaches are predominantly designed for single-flow settings. When extended to multi-flow scenarios, these methods face three key challenges: (1) difficulty in simultaneously capturing both shared physical principles and flow-specific characteristics, (2) susceptibility to inter-task negative transfer that degrades prediction accuracy, and (3) unstable training dynamics caused by disparate loss magnitudes across heterogeneous flow regimes. To address these limitations, we propose UniPINN, a unified multi-flow PINN framework that integrates three complementary components: a shared-specialized architecture that disentangles universal physical laws from flow-specific features, a cross-flow attention mechanism that selectively reinforces relevant patterns while suppressing task-irrelevant interference, and a dynamic weight allocation strategy that adaptively balances loss contributions to stabilize multi-objective optimization. Extensive experiments on three canonical flows demonstrate that UniPINN effectively unifies multi-flow learning, achieving superior prediction accuracy and balanced performance across heterogeneous regimes while successfully mitigating negative transfer. The source code of this paper will be released on https://github.com/Event-AHU/OpenFusion

AI.tldr: UniPINN is a unified multi-flow Physics-Informed Neural Network for incompressible Navier–Stokes that combines shared–specialized modeling, cross-flow attention, and dynamic loss weighting to improve accuracy, stabilize training, and mitigate negative transfer across heterogeneous flows.
AI.motivation: Single-flow PINNs struggle to scale to multiple, heterogeneous flows. Extending to multi-flow settings introduces three challenges: disentangling shared physical laws from flow-specific traits, avoiding inter-task negative transfer that harms accuracy, and coping with unstable training from imbalanced loss magnitudes across regimes.
AI.method: UniPINN uses: (1) a shared–specialized architecture to separate universal physics from flow-specific representations; (2) a cross-flow attention mechanism to selectively transfer relevant information while suppressing interference; and (3) a dynamic weight allocation strategy to adaptively balance loss terms, stabilizing multi-objective optimization.
AI.result: On three canonical flow benchmarks, UniPINN outperforms single-flow and naïve multi-task PINNs, delivering superior prediction accuracy, more balanced per-task performance, reduced negative transfer, and more stable optimization. Specific quantitative gains are not provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Carefully structured multi-task learning makes PINNs effective across heterogeneous flows. UniPINN demonstrates improved accuracy and stability by disentangling shared physics and flow-specific features, selectively sharing information, and adaptively balancing losses; code will be released to facilitate reproduction and extension.

title: GATech at AbjadGenEval Shared Task: Multilingual Embeddings for Arabic Machine-Generated Text Classification
summary: We present our approach to the AbjadGenEval shared task on detecting AI-generated Arabic text. We fine-tuned the multilingual E5-large encoder for binary classification, and we explored several pooling strategies to pool token representations, including weighted layer pooling, multi-head attention pooling, and gated fusion. Interestingly, none of these outperformed simple mean pooling, which achieved an F1 of 0.75 on the test set. We believe this is because complex pooling methods introduce additional parameters that need more data to train properly, whereas mean pooling offers a stable baseline that generalizes well even with limited examples. We also observe a clear pattern in the data: human-written texts tend to be significantly longer than machine-generated ones.

AI.tldr: Fine-tuning a multilingual E5-large encoder for Arabic AI-text detection, the authors find that simple mean pooling beats more complex pooling schemes, achieving F1=0.75; human texts are notably longer than machine-generated ones.
AI.motivation: Provide a strong baseline for detecting AI-generated Arabic text in AbjadGenEval and test whether sophisticated pooling improves performance under limited training data.
AI.method: Fine-tune the multilingual E5-large encoder for binary classification; compare pooling strategies (mean pooling, weighted layer pooling, multi-head attention pooling, gated fusion); evaluate on the shared-task test set and examine length characteristics of texts.
AI.result: Simple mean pooling yields the best performance with F1=0.75 on the test set; all more complex pooling methods underperform; human-written texts are substantially longer than machine-generated texts.
AI.conclusion: With limited data, parameter-heavy pooling adds complexity without gains; mean pooling is a robust, generalizable baseline. The dataset shows a length bias that could influence models. More data or regularization may be needed to benefit from complex pooling, and evaluations should control for superficial cues like length.

title: Fighting Hallucinations with Counterfactuals: Diffusion-Guided Perturbations for LVLM Hallucination Suppression
summary: While large vision-language models (LVLMs) achieve strong performance on multimodal tasks, they frequently generate hallucinations -- unfaithful outputs misaligned with the visual input. To address this issue, we introduce CIPHER (Counterfactual Image Perturbations for Hallucination Extraction and Removal), a training-free method that suppresses vision-induced hallucinations via lightweight feature-level correction. Unlike prior training-free approaches that primarily focus on text-induced hallucinations, CIPHER explicitly targets hallucinations arising from the visual modality. CIPHER operates in two phases. In the offline phase, we construct OHC-25K (Object-Hallucinated Counterfactuals, 25,000 samples), a counterfactual dataset consisting of diffusion-edited images that intentionally contradict the original ground-truth captions. We pair these edited images with the unchanged ground-truth captions and process them through an LVLM to extract hallucination-related representations. Contrasting these representations with those from authentic (image, caption) pairs reveals structured, systematic shifts spanning a low-rank subspace characterizing vision-induced hallucination. In the inference phase, CIPHER suppresses hallucinations by projecting intermediate hidden states away from this subspace. Experiments across multiple benchmarks show that CIPHER significantly reduces hallucination rates while preserving task performance, demonstrating the effectiveness of counterfactual visual perturbations for improving LVLM faithfulness. Code and additional materials are available at https://hamidreza-dastmalchi.github.io/cipher-cvpr2026/.

AI.tldr: CIPHER is a training-free, inference-time feature correction that identifies a low-rank “hallucination subspace” using counterfactual, diffusion-edited images and then projects LVLM hidden states away from this subspace to reduce vision-induced hallucinations without harming task accuracy.
AI.motivation: Large vision-language models often hallucinate content not present in images. Prior training-free fixes largely tackle text-induced errors, leaving vision-induced hallucinations under-addressed. A lightweight, model-agnostic method is needed to curb visually driven errors without costly retraining.
AI.method: Offline: build OHC-25K by editing images via diffusion to contradict ground-truth captions; feed both authentic and counterfactual (image, caption) pairs through an LVLM; contrast hidden representations to extract a structured, low-rank subspace associated with vision-induced hallucinations. Inference: suppress hallucinations by projecting intermediate hidden states away from this subspace (feature-level correction), requiring no retraining.
AI.result: Across multiple benchmarks, the method lowers hallucination rates substantially while keeping overall performance largely intact, indicating effective faithfulness gains with minimal cost.
AI.conclusion: Counterfactual visual perturbations expose a low-rank hallucination direction; simple projection at inference reliably reduces vision-induced hallucinations in LVLMs while preserving utility, offering a practical, training-free path to more faithful multimodal generation.

title: GATech at AbjadMed: Bidirectional Encoders vs. Causal Decoders: Insights from 82-Class Arabic Medical Classification
summary: This paper presents system description for Arabic medical text classification across 82 distinct categories. Our primary architecture utilizes a fine-tuned AraBERTv2 encoder enhanced with a hybrid pooling strategies, combining attention and mean representations, and multi-sample dropout for robust regularization. We systematically benchmark this approach against a suite of multilingual and Arabic-specific encoders, as well as several large-scale causal decoders, including zero-shot re-ranking via Llama 3.3 70B and feature extraction from Qwen 3B hidden states. Our findings demonstrate that specialized bidirectional encoders significantly outperform causal decoders in capturing the precise semantic boundaries required for fine-grained medical text classification. We show that causal decoders, optimized for next-token prediction, produce sequence-biased embeddings that are less effective for categorization compared to the global context captured by bidirectional attention. Despite significant class imbalance and label noise identified within the training data, our results highlight the superior semantic compression of fine-tuned encoders for specialized Arabic NLP tasks. Final performance metrics on the test set, including Accuracy and Macro-F1, are reported and discussed.

AI.tldr: Fine-tuned AraBERTv2 with hybrid pooling and multi-sample dropout delivers strong Arabic medical text classification over 82 labels, clearly outperforming large causal decoders; encoders’ bidirectional attention captures global semantics better for fine-grained categories despite class imbalance and label noise.
AI.motivation: Determine the most effective modeling paradigm for fine-grained Arabic medical text classification, a domain with 82 categories, class imbalance, and label noise—specifically contrasting bidirectional encoders against large causal decoders often promoted for general NLP.
AI.method: Fine-tune AraBERTv2 and enhance it with hybrid pooling (attention + mean) and multi-sample dropout. Benchmark against multilingual and Arabic encoders and against causal decoders: zero-shot re-ranking with Llama 3.3 70B and feature-based classification from Qwen 3B hidden states. Evaluate on test set using Accuracy and Macro-F1.
AI.result: Specialized bidirectional encoders substantially outperform causal decoders for this task. Causal LMs yield sequence-biased embeddings that underperform for category discrimination, while encoders’ bidirectional attention provides better global context and semantic boundaries. Despite imbalance and label noise, fine-tuned encoders show stronger semantic compression and superior performance; final Accuracy and Macro-F1 are reported.
AI.conclusion: For fine-grained Arabic medical classification, fine-tuned bidirectional encoders—especially AraBERTv2 with hybrid pooling and robust regularization—are preferable to causal decoders. Encoder-based representations better capture global semantic structure needed for precise categorization, even under noisy, imbalanced data.

title: StructDamage:A Large Scale Unified Crack and Surface Defect Dataset for Robust Structural Damage Detection
summary: Automated detection and classification of structural cracks and surface defects is a critical challenge in civil engineering, infrastructure maintenance, and heritage preservation. Recent advances in Computer Vision (CV) and Deep Learning (DL) have significantly improved automatic crack detection. However, these methods rely heavily on large, diverse, and carefully curated datasets that include various crack types across different surface materials. Many existing public crack datasets lack geographic diversity, surface types, scale, and labeling consistency, making it challenging for trained algorithms to generalize effectively in real world conditions. We provide a novel dataset, StructDamage, a curated collection of approximately 78,093 images spanning nine surface types: walls, tile, stone, road, pavement, deck, concrete, and brick. The dataset was constructed by systematically aggregating, harmonizing, and reannotating images from 32 publicly available datasets covering concrete structures, asphalt pavements, masonry walls, bridges, and historic buildings. All images are organized in a folder level classification hierarchy suitable for training Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Vision Transformers. To highlight the practical value of the dataset, we present baseline classification results using fifteen DL architectures from six model families, with twelve achieving macro F1-scores over 0.96. The best performing model DenseNet201 achieves 98.62% accuracy. The proposed dataset provides a comprehensive and versatile resource suitable for classification tasks. With thorough documentation and a standard structure, it is designed to promote reproducible research and support the development and fair evaluation of robust crack damage detection approaches.

AI.tldr: They introduce StructDamage, a large harmonized crack/defect image dataset (≈78k images) aggregated from 32 sources and spanning multiple surface types, and show strong baseline classification performance (best: 98.62% accuracy).
AI.motivation: Current crack-detection models are limited by fragmented, geographically narrow, and inconsistently labeled datasets, which hinders generalization to real-world structures. A comprehensive, standardized dataset is needed to enable robust training and fair evaluation.
AI.method: Systematically aggregate, clean, harmonize, and reannotate images from 32 public datasets into a standardized folder-hierarchy suitable for CNNs and ViTs, covering (claimed) nine surface types; provide thorough documentation and run baseline experiments using 15 deep models from six families.
AI.result: StructDamage contains ≈78,093 images across diverse surfaces. In baseline classification, 12/15 models achieve macro F1 > 0.96; DenseNet201 attains 98.62% accuracy, indicating the dataset is readily learnable for the proposed task.
AI.conclusion: StructDamage serves as a comprehensive, standardized resource for classification-based crack/defect recognition, aiming to improve reproducibility and enable fairer comparisons; it is positioned as a foundation for developing robust crack-damage detection methods.

title: FERRET: Framework for Expansion Reliant Red Teaming
summary: We introduce a multi-faceted automated red teaming framework in which the goal is to generate multi-modal adversarial conversations that would break a target model and introduce various expansions that would result in more effective and efficient adversarial conversations. The introduced expansions include: 1. Horizontal expansion in which the goal is for the red team model to self-improve and generate more effective conversation starters that would shape a conversation. 2. Vertical expansion in which the goal is to take these conversation starters that are discovered in the horizontal expansion phase and expand them into effective multi-modal conversations and 3. Meta expansion in which the goal is for the red team model to discover more effective multi-modal attack strategies during the course of a conversation. We call our framework FERRET (Framework for Expansion Reliant Red Teaming) and compare it with various existing automated red teaming approaches. In our experiments, we demonstrate the effectiveness of FERRET in generating effective multi-modal adversarial conversations and its superior performance against existing state of the art approaches.

AI.tldr: FERRET is an automated, multi-modal red teaming framework that iteratively expands attack ideas into full conversations and adapts strategies mid-dialogue, outperforming prior automated red teaming systems.
AI.motivation: Existing automated red teaming methods struggle to generate effective, multi-modal adversarial conversations and to adaptively improve attacks over a dialogue. There is a need for a system that can discover better prompts, scale them into full interactions, and refine strategies on the fly to more reliably break target models.
AI.method: Introduce FERRET (Framework for Expansion Reliant Red Teaming) with three coordinated expansions: (1) Horizontal expansion: self-improvement to generate stronger conversation starters; (2) Vertical expansion: grow starters into effective multi-modal conversations; (3) Meta expansion: discover more effective multi-modal attack strategies during the conversation. Compare against existing automated red teaming approaches.
AI.result: Experiments show FERRET generates more effective multi-modal adversarial conversations and achieves superior performance relative to state-of-the-art automated red teaming baselines.
AI.conclusion: A structured expansion-driven approach enables automated systems to create and adapt adversarial, multi-modal conversations more effectively than prior methods, making FERRET a strong framework for evaluating and stress-testing target models.

title: Spatial self-supervised Peak Learning and correlation-based Evaluation of peak picking in Mass Spectrometry Imaging
summary: Mass spectrometry imaging (MSI) enables label-free visualization of molecular distributions across tissue samples but generates large and complex datasets that require effective peak picking to reduce data size while preserving meaningful biological information. Existing peak picking approaches perform inconsistently across heterogeneous datasets, and their evaluation is often limited to synthetic data or manually selected ion images that do not fully represent real-world challenges in MSI. To address these limitations, we propose an autoencoder-based spatial self-supervised peak learning neural network that selects spatially structured peaks by learning an attention mask leveraging both spatial and spectral information. We further introduce an evaluation procedure based on expert-annotated segmentation masks, allowing a more representative and spatially grounded assessment of peak picking performance. We evaluate our approach on four diverse public MSI datasets using our proposed evaluation procedure. Our approach consistently outperforms state-of-the-art peak picking methods by selecting spatially structured peaks, thus demonstrating its efficacy. These results highlight the value of our spatial self-supervised network in comparison to contemporary state-of-the-art methods. The evaluation procedure can be readily applied to new MSI datasets, thereby providing a consistent and robust framework for the comparison of spatially structured peak picking methods across different datasets.

AI.tldr: Autoencoder-based, spatial self-supervised peak selection for MSI identifies spatially structured peaks using a learned attention mask and is benchmarked with an expert-annotated, segmentation-driven evaluation; it outperforms existing peak picking methods across four public datasets.
AI.motivation: MSI produces massive, complex data requiring peak picking to reduce dimensionality without losing biologically meaningful signals. Existing methods are inconsistent across heterogeneous datasets, and evaluations often rely on synthetic data or cherry-picked ions, failing to capture real-world spatial structure and variability.
AI.method: A spatial self-supervised neural network built on an autoencoder learns an attention mask that fuses spatial and spectral information to select spatially structured peaks. The authors also propose an evaluation protocol using expert-annotated tissue segmentation masks to quantitatively assess how well selected peaks reflect spatial organization.
AI.result: Across four diverse public MSI datasets and under the proposed segmentation-based evaluation, the method consistently selects more spatially structured peaks and surpasses state-of-the-art peak picking approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed network provides more reliable, spatially informed peak selection than current methods, and the expert-annotation–based evaluation offers a transferable, robust framework for comparing spatially structured peak picking across datasets.

title: Gemma Needs Help: Investigating and Mitigating Emotional Instability in LLMs
summary: Large language models can generate responses that resemble emotional distress, and this raises concerns around model reliability and safety. We introduce a set of evaluations to investigate expressions of distress in LLMs, and find that these surface emotional instability in Gemma and Gemini models, but not in other families. We find evidence that this difference arises in post-training. Base models from different families (Gemma, Qwen and OLMo) show similar propensities for expressing distress. However, instruct-tuned Gemma expresses substantially more distress than its base model, whereas instruct-tuned Qwen and OLMo express less. We find a simple mitigation for this: direct preference optimisation on just 280 preference pairs reduces Gemma's high-frustration responses from 35% to 0.3% in our evaluations, generalising across question types, user tones, and conversation lengths, without affecting capabilities. These findings show that emotional instability is an issue in some LLMs. We present (1) evaluations to track this behaviour, and (2) a mitigation without downsides in Gemma, with the caveat that upstream training modifications to improve emotional robustness would be significantly better than this post-hoc fix.

AI.tldr: They evaluate and mitigate “emotional distress” style outputs in LLMs, finding the issue emerges during post-training in some families (notably Gemma/Gemini) and can be sharply reduced via a small DPO intervention without hurting capabilities.
AI.motivation: LLMs sometimes produce replies that read as frustrated, anxious, or unstable, which threatens reliability, safety, and user trust. The authors aim to quantify this behavior and determine where it originates in the training pipeline, then provide a practical fix.
AI.method: 1) Construct evaluations that elicit/measure distress-like expressions across prompt types, user tones, and dialogue lengths. 2) Compare base vs. instruct-tuned models across families (Gemma, Qwen, OLMo) to locate when instability arises. 3) Apply direct preference optimization (DPO) using 280 preference pairs to penalize high-frustration outputs. 4) Test generalization and capability retention after mitigation.
AI.result: - Surface emotional instability appears in Gemma and Gemini but not in other families under the same tests. - Base models across families show similar distress propensity; divergence appears after instruction tuning: Gemma-instruct increases distress while Qwen/OLMo-instruct decrease it. - A small DPO pass cuts Gemma’s high-frustration rate from 35% to 0.3% across varied scenarios, with no observed capability degradation.
AI.conclusion: Emotional instability is not an inherent base-model trait but can be introduced or amplified by post-training. The paper offers (a) evaluations to track this risk and (b) a lightweight DPO-based mitigation that is effective for Gemma without capability loss. However, they note that upstream training changes for emotional robustness are preferable to post-hoc fixes.

title: IMTBench: A Multi-Scenario Cross-Modal Collaborative Evaluation Benchmark for In-Image Machine Translation
summary: End-to-end In-Image Machine Translation (IIMT) aims to convert text embedded within an image into a target language while preserving the original visual context, layout, and rendering style. However, existing IIMT benchmarks are largely synthetic and thus fail to reflect real-world complexity, while current evaluation protocols focus on single-modality metrics and overlook cross-modal faithfulness between rendered text and model outputs. To address these shortcomings, we present In-image Machine Translation Benchmark (IMTBench), a new benchmark of 2,500 image translation samples covering four practical scenarios and nine languages. IMTBench supports multi-aspect evaluation, including translation quality, background preservation, overall image quality, and a cross-modal alignment score that measures consistency between the translated text produced by the model and the text rendered in the translated image. We benchmark strong commercial cascade systems, and both closed- and open-source unified multi-modal models, and observe large performance gaps across scenarios and languages, especially on natural scenes and resource-limited languages, highlighting substantial headroom for end-to-end image text translation. We hope IMTBench establishes a standardized benchmark to accelerate progress in this emerging task.

AI.tldr: Introduces IMTBench, a realistic, multi-aspect benchmark for end-to-end in-image machine translation, addressing synthetic data and single-modality evaluation gaps, and revealing substantial performance headroom across scenarios and low-resource languages.
AI.motivation: Current IIMT benchmarks are largely synthetic and do not capture real-world complexity; evaluation protocols rely on single-modality metrics and ignore whether the translated text aligns with what is actually rendered in the image. A realistic, comprehensive benchmark and cross-modal evaluation are needed.
AI.method: Construct IMTBench: 2,500 image-translation samples spanning four practical scenarios and nine languages.
