Provide multi-aspect evaluation covering translation quality, background preservation, overall image quality, and a cross-modal alignment score between model-generated text and the text rendered in the translated image. Benchmark various systems, including commercial cascade pipelines and both closed- and open-source unified multimodal models.
AI.result: Across models, there are large performance gaps by scenario and language, with particularly poor results on natural scenes and resource-limited languages. The new cross-modal metric and multi-aspect evaluation expose weaknesses that single-modality metrics miss.
AI.conclusion: IMTBench offers a standardized, realistic benchmark and richer evaluation protocol for end-to-end in-image translation. Current systems have ample room for improvement, and the benchmark is positioned to catalyze progress in this emerging task.

title: Measuring and Eliminating Refusals in Military Large Language Models
summary: Military Large Language Models (LLMs) must provide accurate information to the warfighter in time-critical and dangerous situations. However, today's LLMs are imbued with safety behaviors that cause the LLM to refuse many legitimate queries in the military domain, particularly those related to violence, terrorism, or military technology. Our gold benchmark for assessing refusal rates, which was developed by veterans of the US Army and special forces, is to our knowledge the first dataset of its kind. We present results for refusal and deflection rates on 31 public models and 3 military models. We observe hard rejection rates as high as 98.2% and soft deflection rates ranging from 0% to 21.3%. We also present results on two additional synthetic datasets and show their correlations with the gold dataset. Finally, we perform abliteration using the Heretic library on a military-tuned gpt-oss-20b model, showing an absolute increase in answer rate of 66.5 points but an average relative decrease of 2% on other military tasks. In our concluding remarks, we argue for deeper specialization, including with mid-training and end-to-end post-training, to achieve zero refusals and maximum military task accuracy for closed military models.

AI.tldr: They build a veteran-authored benchmark to quantify refusal/deflection of LLMs on military queries, show very high refusal rates across many public models, validate with synthetic sets, and demonstrate that safety-ablated tuning greatly boosts answer rates with minimal average performance loss on other military tasks, arguing for deeper, specialized training to achieve zero refusals in closed military models.
AI.motivation: Warfighters need timely, accurate, domain-specific answers; generic safety layers in current LLMs over-refuse legitimate military queries (e.g., violence/terrorism/tech), impeding operational use. There is a lack of a rigorous, domain-grounded measure of refusal and a need for methods to reduce unwarranted refusals without degrading task performance.
AI.method: 1) Construct a 'gold' refusal/deflection benchmark authored by US Army and special forces veterans. 2) Measure hard rejections and soft deflections across 31 public and 3 military models. 3) Create two synthetic datasets and assess correlation with the gold set. 4) Apply an 'abliteration' (safety-removal) procedure using the Heretic library to a military-tuned gpt-oss-20b, then evaluate answer-rate gains and performance trade-offs on other military tasks.
AI.result: Observed hard rejection rates up to 98.2% and soft deflection rates between 0% and 21.3% across models. The synthetic datasets correlate with the gold benchmark (exact coefficients not stated). Safety ablation on gpt-oss-20b yields a +66.5 point absolute increase in answer rate with an average ~2% relative decrease on other military tasks.
AI.conclusion: Current models over-refuse in military contexts; targeted specialization is needed. The authors advocate mid-training and end-to-end post-training for closed military models to eliminate refusals while maximizing accuracy, with preliminary evidence that aggressive refusal reduction can be achieved with limited collateral performance loss.

title: UHD Image Deblurring via Autoregressive Flow with Ill-conditioned Constraints
summary: Ultra-high-definition (UHD) image deblurring poses significant challenges for UHD restoration methods, which must balance fine-grained detail recovery and practical inference efficiency. Although prominent discriminative and generative methods have achieved remarkable results, a trade-off persists between computational cost and the ability to generate fine-grained detail for UHD image deblurring tasks. To further alleviate these issues, we propose a novel autoregressive flow method for UHD image deblurring with an ill-conditioned constraint. Our core idea is to decompose UHD restoration into a progressive, coarse-to-fine process: at each scale, the sharp estimate is formed by upsampling the previous-scale result and adding a current-scale residual, enabling stable, stage-wise refinement from low to high resolution. We further introduce Flow Matching to model residual generation as a conditional vector field and perform few-step ODE sampling with efficient Euler/Heun solvers, enriching details while keeping inference affordable. Since multi-step generation at UHD can be numerically unstable, we propose an ill-conditioning suppression scheme by imposing condition-number regularization on a feature-induced attention matrix, improving convergence and cross-scale consistency. Our method demonstrates promising performance on blurred images at 4K (3840$\times$2160) or higher resolutions.

AI.tldr: Proposes a progressive, coarse-to-fine autoregressive flow with Flow Matching and ill-conditioning suppression to achieve stable, few-step UHD (4K+) image deblurring that recovers fine details with practical inference cost.
AI.motivation: UHD deblurring needs both fine-grained detail and efficiency, but existing discriminative/generative methods face a trade-off and can become numerically unstable at high resolutions, especially with multi-step generation.
AI.method: (1) Multi-scale decomposition: progressively upsample the previous sharp estimate and add a learned residual at each scale. (2) Residuals are generated via Flow Matching as a conditional vector field, enabling few-step ODE sampling with Euler/Heun solvers for efficiency. (3) Ill-conditioning suppression: impose condition-number regularization on a feature-induced attention matrix to stabilize generation, improve convergence, and ensure cross-scale consistency.
AI.result: Shows promising UHD (4K or higher) deblurring performance, with enriched details, affordable inference via few-step sampling, and improved numerical stability; exact metrics are not provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: The approach balances detail fidelity and inference efficiency for UHD deblurring through progressive refinement and stabilized flow-based residual generation, offering a practical and scalable solution for 4K+ restoration tasks.

title: Evaluating Progress in Graph Foundation Models: A Comprehensive Benchmark and New Insights
summary: Graph foundation models (GFM) aim to acquire transferable knowledge by pre-training on diverse graphs, which can be adapted to various downstream tasks. However, domain shift in graphs is inherently two-dimensional: graphs differ not only in what they describe (topic domains) but also in how they are represented (format domains). Most existing GFM benchmarks vary only topic domains, thereby obscuring how knowledge transfers across both dimensions. We present a new benchmark that jointly evaluates topic and format gaps across the full GFM pipeline, including multi-domain self-supervised pre-training and few-shot downstream adaptation, and provides a timely evaluation of recent GFMs in the rapidly evolving landscape. Our protocol enables controlled assessment in four settings: (i) pre-training on diverse topics and formats, while adapting to unseen downstream datasets; (ii) same pre-training as in (i), while adapting to seen datasets; (iii) pre-training on a single topic domain, while adapting to other topics; (iv) pre-training on a base format, while adapting to other formats. This two-axis evaluation disentangles semantic generalization from robustness to representational shifts. We conduct extensive evaluations of eight state-of-the-art GFMs on 33 datasets spanning seven topic domains and six format domains, surfacing new empirical observations and practical insights for future research. Codes/data are available at https://github.com/smufang/GFMBenchmark.

AI.tldr: They introduce a two-axis benchmark for graph foundation models that jointly tests topic (semantic) and format (representation) shifts, enabling controlled evaluation of pre-training and few-shot adaptation across both dimensions.
AI.motivation: Existing GFM benchmarks mostly vary only topic domains and ignore representation/format shifts, making it unclear how well GFMs transfer knowledge when both the subject matter and the graph encoding change.
AI.method: A unified benchmark and evaluation protocol spanning the full GFM pipeline: multi-domain self-supervised pre-training followed by few-shot adaptation. It defines four controlled settings to isolate effects of topic vs. format gaps and evaluates recent GFMs under these settings.
AI.result: Extensive experiments with eight state-of-the-art GFMs on 33 datasets covering seven topic domains and six format domains. The study reveals new empirical observations about semantic generalization and robustness to representational shifts (details not specified in the abstract) and provides practical insights.
AI.conclusion: Two-axis evaluation is necessary to disentangle and measure semantic generalization and format robustness in GFMs. The benchmark exposes limitations and guides future research; resources are released to support reproducibility.

title: Visually-Guided Controllable Medical Image Generation via Fine-Grained Semantic Disentanglement
summary: Medical image synthesis is crucial for alleviating data scarcity and privacy constraints. However, fine-tuning general text-to-image (T2I) models remains challenging, mainly due to the significant modality gap between complex visual details and abstract clinical text. In addition, semantic entanglement persists, where coarse-grained text embeddings blur the boundary between anatomical structures and imaging styles, thus weakening controllability during generation. To address this, we propose a Visually-Guided Text Disentanglement framework. We introduce a cross-modal latent alignment mechanism that leverages visual priors to explicitly disentangle unstructured text into independent semantic representations. Subsequently, a Hybrid Feature Fusion Module (HFFM) injects these features into a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) through separated channels, enabling fine-grained structural control. Experimental results in three datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms existing approaches in terms of generation quality and significantly improves performance on downstream classification tasks. The source code is available at https://github.com/hx111/VG-MedGen.

AI.tldr: They propose VG-MedGen, a visually-guided text disentanglement framework that aligns text with visual priors and injects disentangled semantics via a hybrid fusion module into a Diffusion Transformer, yielding more controllable, higher-quality medical image synthesis and improved downstream classification across three datasets.
AI.motivation: Medical imaging data are scarce and sensitive. Fine-tuning general T2I models for medical domains is hard due to a modality gap between detailed visuals and abstract clinical text, and because coarse text embeddings entangle anatomy (content) with imaging style (appearance), reducing controllability.
AI.method: Introduce Visually-Guided Text Disentanglement: (1) a cross-modal latent alignment step uses visual priors to explicitly disentangle unstructured clinical text into independent semantic representations (e.g., anatomy vs. style); (2) a Hybrid Feature Fusion Module (HFFM) feeds these separated features through distinct channels into a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) for fine-grained structural control during generation.
AI.result: On three datasets, the method outperforms prior approaches in image quality and markedly boosts performance on downstream classification tasks. Code is released at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: Disentangling text semantics with visual guidance and fusing them separately into DiT improves controllable medical image synthesis and benefits downstream analysis, mitigating modality gap and semantic entanglement. This suggests a promising path for domain-adapted T2I in medical imaging.

title: A Principle-Driven Adaptive Policy for Group Cognitive Stimulation Dialogue for Elderly with Cognitive Impairment
summary: Cognitive impairment is becoming a major public health challenge. Cognitive Stimulation Therapy (CST) is an effective intervention for cognitive impairment, but traditional methods are difficult to scale, and existing digital systems struggle with group dialogues and cognitive stimulation principles. While Large Language Models (LLMs) are powerful, their application in this context faces key challenges: cognitive stimulation dialogue paradigms, a lack of therapeutic reasoning, and static-only user modeling. To address these issues, we propose a principle-driven adaptive policy actualized through a Group Cognitive Stimulation Dialogue (GCSD) system. We first construct a dataset with over 500 hours of real-world CST conversations and 10,000+ simulated dialogues generated via our Principle-Guided Scenario Simulation strategy. Our GCSD system then integrates four core modules to overcome LLM limitations: (i) a multi-speaker context controller to resolve role confusion; (ii) dynamic participant cognitive state modeling for personalized interaction; (iii) a cognitive stimulation-focused attention loss to instill cognitive stimulation reasoning; and (iv) a multi-dimensional reward strategy to enhance response value. Experimental results demonstrate that GCSD significantly outperforms baseline models across various evaluation metrics. Future work will focus on long-term clinical validation to bridge the gap between computational performance and clinical efficacy.

AI.tldr: They introduce a principle-driven Group Cognitive Stimulation Dialogue (GCSD) system that adapts LLMs for delivering group Cognitive Stimulation Therapy. Using 500+ hours of real CST data and 10k+ simulated dialogues, GCSD adds multi-speaker control, dynamic cognitive-state modeling, a stimulation-focused attention loss, and multi-dimensional rewards, outperforming baselines; clinical validation is pending.
AI.motivation: Cognitive impairment is rising, CST is effective but hard to scale. Existing digital tools and general LLMs struggle with group dialogue management, therapeutic reasoning aligned with CST principles, and dynamic, personalized user modeling. A system is needed to translate CST principles into adaptive, multi-participant conversational therapy.
AI.method: 1) Curate a large dataset: 500+ hours of real CST conversations plus >10k simulated dialogues via a Principle-Guided Scenario Simulation strategy. 2) Build GCSD with four modules: (i) multi-speaker context controller to manage roles and turn-taking; (ii) dynamic participant cognitive-state modeling for personalization; (iii) cognitive-stimulation-focused attention loss to encourage therapeutic reasoning; (iv) multi-dimensional reward strategy to optimize response quality and therapeutic value.
AI.result: On multiple evaluation metrics, GCSD significantly outperforms LLM baselines and prior digital CST systems; quantitative details are not given in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Embedding CST principles into an LLM-based, group-capable dialogue system improves alignment with therapeutic goals and measured performance, but rigorous, long-term clinical trials are required to confirm real-world therapeutic efficacy.

title: Sparse Task Vector Mixup with Hypernetworks for Efficient Knowledge Transfer in Whole-Slide Image Prognosis
summary: Whole-Slide Images (WSIs) are widely used for estimating the prognosis of cancer patients. Current studies generally follow a cancer-specific learning paradigm. However, the available training samples for one cancer type are usually scarce in pathology. Consequently, the model often struggles to learn generalizable knowledge, thus performing worse on the tumor samples with inherent high heterogeneity. Although multi-cancer joint learning and knowledge transfer approaches have been explored recently to address it, they either rely on large-scale joint training or extensive inference across multiple models, posing new challenges in computational efficiency. To this end, this paper proposes a new scheme, Sparse Task Vector Mixup with Hypernetworks (STEPH). Unlike previous ones, it efficiently absorbs generalizable knowledge from other cancers for the target via model merging: i) applying task vector mixup to each source-target pair and then ii) sparsely aggregating task vector mixtures to obtain an improved target model, driven by hypernetworks. Extensive experiments on 13 cancer datasets show that STEPH improves over cancer-specific learning and an existing knowledge transfer baseline by 5.14% and 2.01%, respectively. Moreover, it is a more efficient solution for learning prognostic knowledge from other cancers, without requiring large-scale joint training or extensive multi-model inference. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/liupei101/STEPH.

AI.tldr: STEPH efficiently merges knowledge across cancers for WSI-based prognosis by using hypernetwork-guided sparse task-vector mixup, yielding better generalization without heavy joint training or multi-model inference.
AI.motivation: Cancer-specific WSI models suffer from limited samples and high tumor heterogeneity, leading to poor generalization. Existing multi-cancer learning or transfer solutions improve performance but demand large-scale joint training or costly multi-model inference.
AI.method: Propose Sparse Task Vector Mixup with Hypernetworks (STEPH): for each source–target cancer pair, compute and mix task vectors, then use a hypernetwork to produce sparse aggregation weights to combine these mixtures into a single improved target model—achieving model merging instead of joint training or multi-model inference.
AI.result: Across 13 cancer datasets, STEPH improves prognostic performance by 5.14% over cancer-specific learning and by 2.01% over a knowledge-transfer baseline, while being more computationally efficient.
AI.conclusion: Model merging via hypernetwork-driven sparse task-vector mixup provides an effective, scalable, and efficient way to transfer generalizable prognostic knowledge across cancers in WSIs; code is publicly available.

title: TriageSim: A Conversational Emergency Triage Simulation Framework from Structured Electronic Health Records
summary: Research in emergency triage is restricted to structured electronic health records (EHR) due to regulatory constraints on nurse-patient interactions. We introduce TriageSim, a simulation framework for generating persona-conditioned triage conversations from structured records. TriageSim enables multi-turn nurse-patient interactions with explicit control over disfluency and decision behaviour, producing a corpus of ~800 synthetic transcripts and corresponding audio. We use a combination of automated analysis for linguistic, behavioural and acoustic fidelity alongside manual evaluation for medical fidelity using a random subset of 50 conversations. The utility of the generated corpus is examined via conversational triage classification. We observe modest agreement for acuity levels across three modalities: generated synthetic text, ASR transcripts, and direct audio inputs. The code, persona schemata and triage policy prompts for TriageSim will be available upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: TriageSim is a framework that generates persona‑conditioned, multi‑turn nurse–patient triage dialogues (text + audio) from structured EHRs with controllable disfluency/decision behavior, yielding ~800 conversations validated for fidelity and showing modest cross‑modal agreement in acuity classification across text, ASR, and audio.
AI.motivation: Regulatory and privacy constraints limit access to real nurse–patient conversational data, keeping triage research confined to structured EHRs; the field needs realistic, controllable conversational datasets to develop and evaluate triage systems.
AI.method: Build a simulation pipeline that converts structured records into multi‑turn, persona‑conditioned dialogues with tunable disfluency and decision behaviors; synthesize paired audio; conduct automated linguistic/behavioral/acoustic fidelity checks and manual medical fidelity review on a 50‑conversation subset; assess utility via conversational triage classification across synthetic text, ASR transcripts, and direct audio inputs.
AI.result: Created a corpus of ~800 synthetic transcripts with corresponding audio; automated and manual evaluations indicate reasonable linguistic/behavioral/acoustic and medical fidelity; triage acuity predictions exhibit only modest agreement across generated text, ASR outputs, and audio inputs.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic, controllable triage simulations can enable research when real interactions are restricted, but current cross‑modal consistency in acuity classification is limited; the authors will release code, persona schemata, and policy prompts to support further work.

title: DSFlash: Comprehensive Panoptic Scene Graph Generation in Realtime
summary: Scene Graph Generation (SGG) aims to extract a detailed graph structure from an image, a representation that holds significant promise as a robust intermediate step for complex downstream tasks like reasoning for embodied agents. However, practical deployment in real-world applications - especially on resource constrained edge devices - requires speed and resource efficiency, challenges that have received limited attention in existing research. To bridge this gap, we introduce DSFlash, a low-latency model for panoptic scene graph generation designed to overcome these limitations. DSFlash can process a video stream at 56 frames per second on a standard RTX 3090 GPU, without compromising performance against existing state-of-the-art methods. Crucially, unlike prior approaches that often restrict themselves to salient relationships, DSFlash computes comprehensive scene graphs, offering richer contextual information while maintaining its superior latency. Furthermore, DSFlash is light on resources, requiring less than 24 hours to train on a single, nine-year-old GTX 1080 GPU. This accessibility makes DSFlash particularly well-suited for researchers and practitioners operating with limited computational resources, empowering them to adapt and fine-tune SGG models for specialized applications.

AI.tldr: DSFlash is a fast, resource-efficient panoptic scene graph generation (SGG) model that outputs comprehensive scene graphs at 56 FPS on an RTX 3090, matching state-of-the-art accuracy while being trainable in under 24 hours on a single GTX 1080.
AI.motivation: Real-world and edge deployments of SGG demand low latency and modest compute/memory, yet prior work has focused less on efficiency and often restricts relationships to only salient ones. There is a need for comprehensive scene graphs without sacrificing speed or accessibility.
AI.method: Introduce DSFlash, a low-latency architecture for panoptic SGG optimized for throughput and resource efficiency. It generates full, comprehensive scene graphs (not just salient relations) from video frames while maintaining high speed, and is designed to be trainable on older, single-GPU hardware.
AI.result: Processes video at 56 FPS on a standard RTX 3090 with no performance compromise relative to state-of-the-art methods; outputs richer contextual (comprehensive) scene graphs; can be trained in less than 24 hours on a single, nine-year-old GTX 1080 GPU.
AI.conclusion: DSFlash enables practical, accessible SGG by combining high-speed, comprehensive scene graph generation with a light training footprint, making it suitable for deployment on limited hardware and for researchers/practitioners to adapt and fine-tune for domain-specific uses.

title: The Prediction-Measurement Gap: Toward Meaning Representations as Scientific Instruments
summary: Text embeddings have become central to computational social science and psychology, enabling scalable measurement of meaning and mixed-method inference. Yet most representation learning is optimized and evaluated for prediction and retrieval, yielding a prediction-measurement gap: representations that perform well as features may be poorly suited as scientific instruments. The paper argues that scientific meaning analysis motivates a distinct family of objectives - scientific usability - emphasizing geometric legibility, interpretability and traceability to linguistic evidence, robustness to non-semantic confounds, and compatibility with regression-style inference over semantic directions. Grounded in cognitive and neuro-psychological views of meaning, the paper assesses static word embeddings and contextual transformer representations against these requirements: static spaces remain attractive for transparent measurement, whereas contextual spaces offer richer semantics but entangle meaning with other signals and exhibit geometric and interpretability issues that complicate inference. The paper then outlines a course-setting agenda around (i) geometry-first design for gradients and abstraction, including hierarchy-aware spaces constrained by psychologically privileged levels; (ii) invertible post-hoc transformations that recondition embedding geometry and reduce nuisance influence; and (iii) meaning atlases and measurement-oriented evaluation protocols for reliable and traceable semantic inference. As the field debates the limits of scale-first progress, measurement-ready representations offer a principled new frontier.

AI.tldr: Prediction-optimized text embeddings are ill-suited as scientific measurement tools. The paper defines “scientific usability” (legible geometry, interpretability, traceability, robustness to confounds, regression-compatibility), finds static embeddings more transparent while contextual ones are richer but entangled and harder to analyze, and proposes geometry-first design, invertible reconditioning, and measurement-oriented evaluation to build measurement-ready representations.
AI.motivation: Computational social science and psychology need embeddings that reliably measure meaning for inference, not just predict or retrieve. Current training objectives create a prediction–measurement gap, motivating representations that are interpretable, robust to non-semantic noise, and suitable for regression-style analyses anchored in linguistic evidence.
AI.method: Conceptual and evaluative: articulate criteria for “scientific usability” grounded in cognitive and neuro-psychological theories; assess static word embeddings and contextual transformer representations against these criteria; outline a design and evaluation agenda (geometry-first spaces, invertible post-hoc transformations, and meaning atlases with measurement-focused benchmarks).
AI.result: Static embeddings remain attractive for transparent, traceable measurement; contextual embeddings encode richer semantics but conflate meaning with other signals and suffer geometric and interpretability issues that hinder inference. The paper specifies concrete directions—geometry-aware representations, reconditioning transforms, and new evaluation protocols—to mitigate these problems.
AI.conclusion: Scientific meaning analysis requires objectives distinct from prediction. Building measurement-ready, legible, and robust representations—supported by geometry-first design, invertible conditioning, and measurement-oriented evaluation—offers a principled path forward beyond scale-first approaches.

title: Prompting with the human-touch: evaluating model-sensitivity of foundation models for musculoskeletal CT segmentation
summary: Promptable Foundation Models (FMs), initially introduced for natural image segmentation, have also revolutionized medical image segmentation. The increasing number of models, along with evaluations varying in datasets, metrics, and compared models, makes direct performance comparison between models difficult and complicates the selection of the most suitable model for specific clinical tasks. In our study, 11 promptable FMs are tested using non-iterative 2D and 3D prompting strategies on a private and public dataset focusing on bone and implant segmentation in four anatomical regions (wrist, shoulder, hip and lower leg). The Pareto-optimal models are identified and further analyzed using human prompts collected through a dedicated observer study. Our findings are: 1) The segmentation performance varies a lot between FMs and prompting strategies; 2) The Pareto-optimal models in 2D are SAM and SAM2.1, in 3D nnInteractive and Med-SAM2; 3) Localization accuracy and rater consistency vary with anatomical structures, with higher consistency for simple structures (wrist bones) and lower consistency for complex structures (pelvis, tibia, implants); 4) The segmentation performance drops using human prompts, suggesting that performance reported on "ideal" prompts extracted from reference labels might overestimate the performance in a human-driven setting; 5) All models were sensitive to prompt variations. While two models demonstrated intra-rater robustness, it did not scale to inter-rater settings. We conclude that the selection of the most optimal FM for a human-driven setting remains challenging, with even high-performing FMs being sensitive to variations in human input prompts. Our code base for prompt extraction and model inference is available: https://github.com/CarolineMagg/segmentation-FM-benchmark/

AI.tldr: They benchmark 11 promptable segmentation foundation models across 2D and 3D settings for bone and implant tasks, find large performance variability, identify Pareto-optimal models (SAM/SAM2.1 in 2D; nnInteractive/Med-SAM2 in 3D), and show that real human prompts reduce performance and expose high sensitivity to prompt variations, making model choice for human-driven workflows nontrivial.
AI.motivation: Direct comparison of promptable foundation models for medical image segmentation is difficult due to heterogeneous datasets, metrics, and evaluation protocols. Clinically, users rely on human prompts, so understanding which models perform best and how robust they are to prompt variability and rater differences is essential for safe, effective deployment.
AI.method: Evaluate 11 promptable FMs using standardized, non-iterative 2D and 3D prompting on private and public datasets targeting bone and implant segmentation in wrist, shoulder, hip, and lower leg. Determine Pareto-optimal models across metrics, then run an observer study to collect real human prompts to assess localization accuracy, rater consistency, and sensitivity to prompt variations versus ideal prompts extracted from reference labels.
AI.result: - Performance varies widely across models and prompting strategies. - Pareto-optimal models: 2D—SAM and SAM2.1; 3D—nnInteractive and Med-SAM2. - Rater consistency depends on anatomy: higher for simpler structures (wrist bones), lower for complex ones (pelvis, tibia, implants). - Human prompts lead to notable performance drops compared with ideal prompts, indicating prior evaluations may overestimate real-world performance. - All models are sensitive to prompt variations; two models showed intra-rater robustness, but this did not extend to inter-rater settings.
AI.conclusion: Selecting an optimal FM for human-in-the-loop medical segmentation remains challenging because even top performers are sensitive to human prompt variability. Reported results using ideal prompts risk overstating clinical performance. Future work should focus on prompt-robust training/evaluation and human-centered protocols. Code for reproducibility is provided.

title: The Generation-Recognition Asymmetry: Six Dimensions of a Fundamental Divide in Formal Language Theory
comment: Submitted to Information and Computation. 32 pages, 6 figures, 4 tables
summary: Every formal grammar defines a language and can in principle be used in three ways: to generate strings (production), to recognize them (parsing), or -- given only examples -- to infer the grammar itself (grammar induction). Generation and recognition are extensionally equivalent -- they characterize the same set -- but operationally asymmetric in multiple independent ways. Inference is a qualitatively harder problem: it does not have access to a known grammar. Despite the centrality of this triad to compiler design, natural language processing, and formal language theory, no survey has treated it as a unified, multidimensional phenomenon. We identify six dimensions along which generation and recognition diverge: computational complexity, ambiguity, directionality, information availability, grammar inference, and temporality. We show that the common characterization "generation is easy, parsing is hard" is misleading: unconstrained generation is trivial, but generation under constraints can be NP-hard. The real asymmetry is that parsing is always constrained (the input is given) while generation need not be. Two of these dimensions -- directionality and temporality -- have not previously been identified as dimensions of the generation-recognition asymmetry. We connect the temporal dimension to the surprisal framework of Hale (2001) and Levy (2008), arguing that surprisal formalizes the temporal asymmetry between a generator (surprisal = 0) and a parser that predicts under uncertainty (surprisal > 0). We review bidirectional systems in NLP and observe that bidirectionality has been available for fifty years yet has not transferred to most domain-specific applications. We conclude with a discussion of large language models, which architecturally unify generation and recognition while operationally preserving the asymmetry.

AI.tldr: Unified survey of generation, recognition (parsing), and grammar induction in formal grammars; identifies six asymmetry dimensions (incl. two novel: directionality, temporality), shows constraint—not task type—drives hardness, links temporality to surprisal, reviews bidirectional NLP, and discusses LLMs that architecturally unify but operationally preserve the asymmetry.
AI.motivation: Despite the centrality of generation, recognition, and induction to compilers, NLP, and formal language theory, there is no unified, multidimensional treatment. Prevailing wisdom (“generation is easy, parsing is hard”) is oversimplified; the field lacks a framework capturing where and why asymmetries arise and how they relate to probabilistic processing (surprisal) and modern systems (bidirectional models, LLMs).
AI.method: Conceptual and survey methodology: define a taxonomy of six divergence dimensions (computational complexity, ambiguity, directionality, information availability, grammar inference, temporality); analyze constrained vs. unconstrained generation complexity; relate temporality to surprisal theory (Hale, Levy); review decades of bidirectional NLP systems; discuss implications for LLM architectures.
AI.result:
- Six dimensions of generation–recognition divergence articulated; two (directionality, temporality) are newly highlighted.
- Demonstration that unconstrained generation is trivial, but constraint-driven generation can be NP-hard; parsing is inherently constrained.
- Formal linkage of temporal asymmetry to surprisal: generators have surprisal 0; parsers incur surprisal > 0 under uncertainty.
- Observation that bidirectional approaches have existed for ~50 years but remain underused in domain-specific applications.
- Analysis that LLMs unify generation and recognition architecturally while leaving operational asymmetries intact.
AI.conclusion: Hardness stems from constraints rather than an intrinsic ease/hardness dichotomy between generation and parsing. Temporality and directionality are key, previously underemphasized axes of asymmetry; surprisal quantifies the parser’s temporal uncertainty. Greater adoption of bidirectional designs is warranted, and while LLMs blur architectural lines between generation and recognition, they still preserve the core operational asymmetry.

title: Towards Cognitive Defect Analysis in Active Infrared Thermography with Vision-Text Cues
summary: Active infrared thermography (AIRT) is currently witnessing a surge of artificial intelligence (AI) methodologies being deployed for automated subsurface defect analysis of high performance carbon fiber-reinforced polymers (CFRP). Deploying AI-based AIRT methodologies for inspecting CFRPs requires the creation of time consuming and expensive datasets of CFRP inspection sequences to train neural networks. To address this challenge, this work introduces a novel language-guided framework for cognitive defect analysis in CFRPs using AIRT and vision-language models (VLMs). Unlike conventional learning-based approaches, the proposed framework does not require developing training datasets for extensive training of defect detectors, instead it relies solely on pretrained multimodal VLM encoders coupled with a lightweight adapter to enable generative zero-shot understanding and localization of subsurface defects. By leveraging pretrained multimodal encoders, the proposed system enables generative zero-shot understanding of thermographic patterns and automatic detection of subsurface defects. Given the domain gap between thermographic data and natural images used to train VLMs, an AIRT-VLM Adapter is proposed to enhance the visibility of defects while aligning the thermographic domain with the learned representations of VLMs. The proposed framework is validated using three representative VLMs; specifically, GroundingDINO, Qwen-VL-Chat, and CogVLM. Validation is performed on 25 CFRP inspection sequences with impacts introduced at different energy levels, reflecting realistic defects encountered in industrial scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that the AIRT-VLM adapter achieves signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) gains exceeding 10 dB compared with conventional thermographic dimensionality-reduction methods, while enabling zero-shot defect detection with intersection-over-union values reaching 70%.

AI.tldr: They propose a zero-shot, language-guided AIRT framework that uses pretrained vision–language models plus a lightweight adapter to detect and localize subsurface defects in CFRP without training new datasets, achieving >10 dB SNR gains and up to 70% IoU.
AI.motivation: AI for AIRT on CFRP typically needs costly, time-consuming labeled thermography datasets and faces a domain gap between thermographic data and natural-image-trained models.
AI.method: Use pretrained multimodal VLM encoders (GroundingDINO, Qwen-VL-Chat, CogVLM) guided by text prompts, augmented with an AIRT-VLM Adapter that enhances defect visibility and aligns thermographic signals with VLM feature spaces, enabling generative zero-shot understanding and localization. Validate on 25 CFRP inspection sequences with varied impact energies.
AI.result: The adapter yields >10 dB SNR improvement over conventional thermographic dimensionality-reduction baselines and enables zero-shot defect detection with intersection-over-union up to 70%.
AI.conclusion: Language-guided, adapter-augmented VLMs can perform zero-shot AIRT defect analysis in CFRP without dedicated training datasets, showing promising accuracy and robustness for practical industrial inspections.

title: Reason and Verify: A Framework for Faithful Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) significantly improves the factuality of Large Language Models (LLMs), yet standard pipelines often lack mechanisms to verify inter- mediate reasoning, leaving them vulnerable to hallucinations in high-stakes domains. To address this, we propose a domain-specific RAG framework that integrates explicit rea- soning and faithfulness verification. Our architecture augments standard retrieval with neural query rewriting, BGE-based cross-encoder reranking, and a rationale generation module that grounds sub-claims in specific evidence spans. We further introduce an eight-category verification taxonomy that enables fine-grained assessment of rationale faithfulness, distinguishing between explicit and implicit support patterns to facilitate structured error diagnosis. We evaluate this framework on the BioASQ and PubMedQA benchmarks, specifically analyzing the impact of dynamic in-context learning and rerank- ing under constrained token budgets. Experiments demonstrate that explicit rationale generation improves accuracy over vanilla RAG baselines, while dynamic demonstration selection combined with robust reranking yields further gains in few-shot settings. Using Llama-3-8B-Instruct, our approach achieves 89.1% on BioASQ-Y/N and 73.0% on Pub- MedQA, competitive with systems using significantly larger models. Additionally, we perform a pilot study combining human expert assessment with LLM-based verification to explore how explicit rationale generation improves system transparency and enables more detailed diagnosis of retrieval failures in biomedical question answering.

AI.tldr: They develop a biomedical RAG system that adds explicit rationale generation and a faithfulness-checking taxonomy on top of retrieval, query rewriting, and strong reranking, yielding better accuracy and transparency on BioASQ and PubMedQA with a small LLM.
AI.motivation: Standard RAG improves factuality but does not verify intermediate reasoning, leaving room for hallucinations—especially risky in biomedical QA under tight token budgets and few-shot constraints. The authors aim to ensure that generated claims are grounded in evidence and to diagnose retrieval/reasoning failures.
AI.method: Augment RAG with: (1) neural query rewriting; (2) BGE cross-encoder reranking; (3) a rationale module that decomposes answers into sub-claims grounded in evidence spans; and (4) an eight-category verification taxonomy distinguishing explicit vs. implicit support to assess faithfulness. They also use dynamic in-context demonstration selection and analyze effects under token constraints. A pilot combines human expert review with LLM-based verification.
AI.result: Explicit rationale generation improves accuracy over vanilla RAG; adding dynamic demonstration selection and robust reranking further boosts few-shot performance. With Llama‑3‑8B‑Instruct they obtain 89.1% on BioASQ Y/N and 73.0% on PubMedQA, competitive with larger-model systems. The pilot shows better transparency and more granular diagnosis of retrieval failures.
AI.conclusion: Domain-specific RAG that integrates explicit reasoning and faithfulness verification enhances both performance and interpretability in biomedical QA, working effectively with small models and enabling structured error analysis under practical constraints.

title: P-GSVC: Layered Progressive 2D Gaussian Splatting for Scalable Image and Video
summary: Gaussian splatting has emerged as a competitive explicit representation for image and video reconstruction. In this work, we present P-GSVC, the first layered progressive 2D Gaussian splatting framework that provides a unified solution for scalable Gaussian representation in both images and videos. P-GSVC organizes 2D Gaussian splats into a base layer and successive enhancement layers, enabling coarse-to-fine reconstructions. To effectively optimize this layered representation, we propose a joint training strategy that simultaneously updates Gaussians across layers, aligning their optimization trajectories to ensure inter-layer compatibility and a stable progressive reconstruction. P-GSVC supports scalability in terms of both quality and resolution. Our experiments show that the joint training strategy can gain up to 1.9 dB improvement in PSNR for video and 2.6 dB improvement in PSNR for image when compared to methods that perform sequential layer-wise training. Project page: https://longanwang-cs.github.io/PGSVC-webpage/

AI.tldr: P-GSVC introduces a layered, progressively refinable 2D Gaussian splatting framework for both images and videos, trained jointly across layers to ensure compatibility, delivering scalable quality/resolution and notable PSNR gains over sequential layer-wise training.
AI.motivation: Existing Gaussian splatting methods lack a unified, scalable representation that supports progressive (coarse-to-fine) reconstruction for both images and videos. Sequential layer-wise optimization often leads to inter-layer mismatch and unstable progression. The authors aim to enable adaptive quality/resolution scaling and stable progressive decoding across modalities.
AI.method: Organize 2D Gaussian splats into a base layer plus multiple enhancement layers to allow coarse-to-fine reconstruction. Introduce a joint training strategy that simultaneously optimizes Gaussians in all layers, aligning their optimization trajectories to maintain inter-layer compatibility and stability. The framework supports scalability in both perceptual quality and spatial resolution for images and videos.
AI.result: Joint training outperforms sequential layer-wise training, yielding up to +1.9 dB PSNR on video and +2.6 dB PSNR on images, while enabling stable progressive reconstruction and unified handling of images and videos.
AI.conclusion: Layered progressive 2D Gaussian splatting with joint cross-layer optimization provides an effective, scalable representation for images and videos, improving reconstruction quality and enabling practical coarse-to-fine delivery across resolutions and qualities.

title: Lost in Backpropagation: The LM Head is a Gradient Bottleneck
summary: The last layer of neural language models (LMs) projects output features of dimension $D$ to logits in dimension $V$, the size of the vocabulary, where usually $D \ll V$. This mismatch is known to raise risks of limited expressivity in neural LMs, creating a so-called softmax bottleneck. We show the softmax bottleneck is not only an expressivity bottleneck but also an optimization bottleneck. Backpropagating $V$-dimensional gradients through a rank-$D$ linear layer induces unavoidable compression, which alters the training feedback provided to the vast majority of the parameters. We present a theoretical analysis of this phenomenon and measure empirically that 95-99% of the gradient norm is suppressed by the output layer, resulting in vastly suboptimal update directions. We conduct controlled pretraining experiments showing that the gradient bottleneck makes trivial patterns unlearnable, and drastically affects the training dynamics of LLMs. We argue that this inherent flaw contributes to training inefficiencies at scale independently of the model architecture, and raises the need for new LM head designs.

AI.tldr: The standard low-rank LM head (D→V with D≪V) not only limits expressivity (softmax bottleneck) but also compresses gradients during backprop, suppressing most of their norm and yielding suboptimal updates; this harms learning dynamics and calls for redesigned output layers.
AI.motivation: While the softmax bottleneck is known as an expressivity limit from D≪V in LM heads, its effect on optimization has been underexplored. The authors aim to understand whether the same low-rank projection also degrades gradient flow and training efficiency at scale.
AI.method: They present a theoretical analysis showing that backpropagating V-dimensional gradients through a rank-D linear layer inherently compresses gradient information. Empirically, they quantify gradient-norm suppression by the output layer and run controlled pretraining experiments to test learnability of simple patterns and to observe impacts on LLM training dynamics.
AI.result: Passing gradients through the D×V output projection suppresses 95–99% of the gradient norm, causing update directions to be far from optimal. This gradient bottleneck can make trivial patterns unlearnable and significantly distorts training dynamics in LLM pretraining.
AI.conclusion: The conventional LM head imposes an inherent optimization bottleneck, independent of architecture, contributing to training inefficiency. Mitigating this likely requires alternative LM head designs that avoid or reduce gradient compression.

title: PET-F2I: A Comprehensive Benchmark and Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning of LLMs for PET/CT Report Impression Generation
summary: PET/CT imaging is pivotal in oncology and nuclear medicine, yet summarizing complex findings into precise diagnostic impressions is labor-intensive. While LLMs have shown promise in medical text generation, their capability in the highly specialized domain of PET/CT remains underexplored. We introduce PET-F2I-41K (PET Findings-to-Impression Benchmark), a large-scale benchmark for PET/CT impression generation using LLMs, constructed from over 41k real-world reports. Using PET-F2I-41K, we conduct a comprehensive evaluation of 27 models across proprietary frontier LLMs, open-source generalist models, and medical-domain LLMs, and we develop a domain-adapted 7B model (PET-F2I-7B) fine-tuned from Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct via LoRA. Beyond standard NLG metrics (e.g., BLEU-4, ROUGE-L, BERTScore), we propose three clinically grounded metrics - Entity Coverage Rate (ECR), Uncovered Entity Rate (UER), and Factual Consistency Rate (FCR) - to assess diagnostic completeness and factual reliability. Experiments reveal that neither frontier nor medical-domain LLMs perform adequately in zero-shot settings. In contrast, PET-F2I-7B achieves substantial gains (e.g., 0.708 BLEU-4) and a 3.0x improvement in entity coverage over the strongest baseline, while offering advantages in cost, latency, and privacy. Beyond this modeling contribution, PET-F2I-41K establishes a standardized evaluation framework to accelerate the development of reliable and clinically deployable reporting systems for PET/CT.

AI.tldr: Introduces PET-F2I-41K, a 41k-report benchmark and a domain-adapted 7B model for generating PET/CT impressions, plus clinically grounded metrics, showing large gains over zero-shot and baseline systems.
AI.motivation: Summarizing PET/CT findings into precise impressions is time-consuming and error-prone; existing LLMs’ abilities in this niche are unclear, and current NLG metrics may not capture clinical adequacy and factuality. A domain-specific benchmark and evaluation schema are needed to enable reliable automation.
AI.method: Construct PET-F2I-41K from >41k real-world PET/CT reports (findings-to-impression). Evaluate 27 LLMs (frontier, open-source, and medical-domain) with standard NLG metrics (BLEU-4, ROUGE-L, BERTScore) and propose three clinical metrics: Entity Coverage Rate (ECR), Uncovered Entity Rate (UER), and Factual Consistency Rate (FCR). Develop PET-F2I-7B by LoRA fine-tuning Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct on the benchmark and compare zero-shot vs. fine-tuned performance, along with cost/latency/privacy.
AI.result: Zero-shot frontier and medical LLMs underperform. PET-F2I-7B achieves strong gains, e.g., BLEU-4 of 0.708 and a 3× improvement in entity coverage over the strongest baseline, while reducing cost and latency and improving privacy.
AI.conclusion: PET-F2I-41K provides a standardized, clinically aware evaluation framework for PET/CT impression generation. A compact, domain-adapted model can outperform larger or specialized zero-shot systems, supporting progress toward reliable, deployable PET/CT reporting tools.

title: OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking
summary: Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal, namely the user reply, tool output, terminal or GUI state change that follows each action, yet no existing agentic RL system recovers it as a live, online learning source. We present OpenClaw-RL, a framework built on a simple observation: next-state signals are universal, and policy can learn from all of them simultaneously. Personal conversations, terminal executions, GUI interactions, SWE tasks, and tool-call traces are not separate training problems. They are all interactions that can be used to train the same policy in the same loop. Next-state signals encode two forms of information: evaluative signals, which indicate how well the action performed and are extracted as scalar rewards via a PRM judge; and directive signals, which indicate how the action should have been different and are recovered through Hindsight-Guided On-Policy Distillation (OPD). We extract textual hints from the next state, construct an enhanced teacher context, and provide token-level directional advantage supervision that is richer than any scalar reward. Due to the asynchronous design, the model serves live requests, the PRM judges ongoing interactions, and the trainer updates the policy at the same time, with zero coordination overhead between them. Applied to personal agents, OpenClaw-RL enables an agent to improve simply by being used, recovering conversational signals from user re-queries, corrections, and explicit feedback. Applied to general agents, the same infrastructure supports scalable RL across terminal, GUI, SWE, and tool-call settings, where we additionally demonstrate the utility of process rewards. Code: https://github.com/Gen-Verse/OpenClaw-RL

AI.tldr: OpenClaw-RL is an online RL framework that treats every agent interaction’s next-state (user reply, tool/GUI/terminal outcome) as universal supervision, combining scalar rewards and directive token-level guidance to continually improve a single policy across diverse tasks.
AI.motivation: Agentic RL today underuses live interaction data and fractures training across domains (chat, tools, terminal/GUI, SWE). The authors aim to turn ubiquitous next-state signals into a unified, online learning source so agents can improve simply by being used.
AI.method: OpenClaw-RL extracts two complementary signals from each next state: (1) evaluative signals converted to scalar rewards via a PRM judge; and (2) directive signals via Hindsight-Guided On-Policy Distillation (OPD), which mines textual hints from the next state, builds an enhanced teacher context, and supplies token-level directional advantage supervision. An asynchronous system serves live requests, judges interactions, and updates the policy concurrently with no coordination overhead. The same loop trains personal and general agents (terminal, GUI, SWE, tool-calls) and can incorporate process rewards.
AI.result: Qualitatively, agents improve online from real use: personal agents learn from re-queries, corrections, and explicit feedback; general agents benefit across terminal/GUI/SWE/tool-call tasks, with added gains from process rewards. The framework demonstrates practical scalability and universality of next-state supervision.
AI.conclusion: Treating next-state signals as universal, mixed evaluative/directive supervision enables a single, continuously learning policy across heterogeneous agent tasks. OpenClaw-RL operationalizes this with asynchronous serving-judging-training and richer token-level guidance than scalar rewards alone, yielding practical online improvement in both personal and general agent settings.

title: UniStitch: Unifying Semantic and Geometric Features for Image Stitching
summary: Traditional image stitching methods estimate warps from hand-crafted geometric features, whereas recent learning-based solutions leverage semantic features from neural networks instead. These two lines of research have largely diverged along separate evolution, with virtually no meaningful convergence to date. In this paper, we take a pioneering step to bridge this gap by unifying semantic and geometric features with UniStitch, a unified image stitching framework from multimodal features. To align discrete geometric features (i.e., keypoint) with continuous semantic feature maps, we present a Neural Point Transformer (NPT) module, which transforms unordered, sparse 1D geometric keypoints into ordered, dense 2D semantic maps. Then, to integrate the advantages of both representations, an Adaptive Mixture of Experts (AMoE) module is designed to fuse geometric and semantic representations. It dynamically shifts focus toward more reliable features during the fusion process, allowing the model to handle complex scenes, especially when either modality might be compromised. The fused representation can be adopted into common deep stitching pipelines, delivering significant performance gains over any single feature. Experiments show that UniStitch outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods with a large margin, paving the way for a unified paradigm between traditional and learning-based image stitching.

AI.tldr: UniStitch unifies geometric keypoints and semantic feature maps for image stitching via a Neural Point Transformer and an Adaptive Mixture of Experts, yielding state-of-the-art accuracy and robustness.
AI.motivation: Traditional stitching relies on geometric keypoints; recent methods use learned semantic features. These directions evolved separately, causing limitations when one modality fails (e.g., textureless areas or semantic ambiguity). The paper aims to bridge this gap for more reliable stitching across complex scenes.
AI.method: Proposes UniStitch with two key modules: (1) Neural Point Transformer (NPT) that converts unordered, sparse 1D geometric keypoints into ordered, dense 2D semantic-like maps; (2) Adaptive Mixture of Experts (AMoE) that fuses geometric and semantic representations by dynamically emphasizing the more reliable modality. The fused representation is plugged into standard deep stitching pipelines.
AI.result: Across experiments, UniStitch significantly outperforms prior state-of-the-art methods, and surpasses models using only geometric or only semantic features, especially in challenging scenarios.
AI.conclusion: A unified, multimodal feature paradigm for image stitching is feasible and beneficial. By aligning and adaptively fusing geometric and semantic cues, UniStitch achieves superior accuracy and robustness, pointing toward convergence between traditional and learning-based approaches.

title: Adaptive Activation Cancellation for Hallucination Mitigation in Large Language Models
summary: Large Language Models frequently generate fluent but factually incorrect text. We propose Adaptive Activation Cancellation (AAC), a real-time inference-time framework that treats hallucination-associated neural activations as structured interference within the transformer residual stream, drawing an explicit analogy to classical adaptive noise cancellation from signal processing. The framework identifies Hallucination Nodes (H-Nodes) via layer-wise linear probing and suppresses them using a confidence-weighted forward hook during auto-regressive generation -- requiring no external knowledge, no fine-tuning, and no additional inference passes. Evaluated across OPT-125M, Phi-3-mini, and LLaMA 3-8B on TruthfulQA and HaluEval, the real-time hook is the only intervention that consistently improves downstream accuracy on all three scales. Critically, the method is strictly surgical: WikiText-103 perplexity and MMLU reasoning accuracy are preserved at exactly 0.0% degradation across all three model scales, a property that distinguishes AAC from interventions that trade fluency or general capability for factual improvement. On the LLaMA 3-8B scale, the hook additionally yields positive generation-level gains (MC1 +0.04; MC2 +0.003; Token-F1 +0.003) while achieving probe-space selectivity 5.94x - 3.5x higher than the ITI baseline -- demonstrating that targeted neuron-level suppression can simultaneously improve factual accuracy and preserve model capability.

AI.tldr: AAC is a training-free, inference-time method that identifies and suppresses hallucination-associated activations via confidence-weighted forward hooks, improving factuality on TruthfulQA and HaluEval across models (125M–8B) with zero degradation to perplexity or MMLU and small gains on LLaMA 3-8B; it is more selective than the ITI baseline.
AI.motivation: Mitigate LLM hallucinations without sacrificing fluency or general capabilities and without relying on external knowledge, fine-tuning, or extra inference passes; conceptualize hallucination signals as structured interference analogous to noise in signal processing.
AI.method: Use layer-wise linear probes to detect Hallucination Nodes (H-Nodes) in the transformer residual stream; during autoregressive generation, apply a confidence-weighted forward hook that suppresses these activations in real time. Requires no external knowledge, no fine-tuning, and no additional passes.
AI.result: Across OPT-125M, Phi-3-mini, and LLaMA 3-8B on TruthfulQA and HaluEval, the real-time hook consistently improves downstream accuracy. It causes exactly 0.0% degradation to WikiText-103 perplexity and MMLU accuracy at all scales. On LLaMA 3-8B it yields MC1 +0.04, MC2 +0.003, Token-F1 +0.003, and achieves 3.5x–5.94x higher probe-space selectivity than the ITI baseline.
AI.conclusion: Targeted neuron-level suppression at inference can reduce hallucinations while preserving (and slightly improving) general capability, making AAC a practical, surgical, and scale-agnostic alternative to heavier interventions.

title: R4-CGQA: Retrieval-based Vision Language Models for Computer Graphics Image Quality Assessment
summary: Immersive Computer Graphics (CGs) rendering has become ubiquitous in modern daily life. However, comprehensively evaluating CG quality remains challenging for two reasons: First, existing CG datasets lack systematic descriptions of rendering quality; and second existing CG quality assessment methods cannot provide reasonable text-based explanations. To address these issues, we first identify six key perceptual dimensions of CG quality from the user perspective and construct a dataset of 3500 CG images with corresponding quality descriptions. Each description covers CG style, content, and perceived quality along the selected dimensions. Furthermore, we use a subset of the dataset to build several question-answer benchmarks based on the descriptions in order to evaluate the responses of existing Vision Language Models (VLMs). We find that current VLMs are not sufficiently accurate in judging fine-grained CG quality, but that descriptions of visually similar images can significantly improve a VLM's understanding of a given CG image. Motivated by this observation, we adopt retrieval-augmented generation and propose a two-stream retrieval framework that effectively enhances the CG quality assessment capabilities of VLMs. Experiments on several representative VLMs demonstrate that our method substantially improves their performance on CG quality assessment.

AI.tldr: Paper builds a CG-quality dataset with rich textual descriptions across six perceptual dimensions, shows current VLMs struggle with fine-grained CG quality, and boosts their performance via a retrieval-augmented, two-stream framework using similar-image descriptions.
AI.motivation: Comprehensive CG quality evaluation is hard because existing datasets lack systematic, perceptual quality annotations and current assessment methods (incl. VLMs) cannot yield accurate, text-based explanations. The authors aim to create a perceptually grounded resource and method that enable fine-grained, explainable CG quality judgments.
AI.method: (1) Elicit six user-centric perceptual dimensions of CG quality. (2) Construct a dataset of 3,500 CG images with descriptions covering style, content, and perceived quality along those dimensions. (3) Build QA benchmarks from a subset to evaluate VLMs. (4) Diagnose that VLMs are weak on fine-grained CG quality but benefit from contextual descriptions of visually similar images. (5) Propose a retrieval-augmented generation approach with a two-stream retrieval framework to feed similar-image descriptions to VLMs and enhance assessment.
AI.result: Baseline VLMs are not sufficiently accurate at fine-grained CG quality judgments. Incorporating descriptions from visually similar images markedly improves understanding. The proposed two-stream retrieval RAG approach substantially improves multiple representative VLMs on the new CG quality QA benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: A new, perceptually annotated CG dataset and QA benchmarks expose limitations of current VLMs on fine-grained CG quality and enable explainable assessment. Retrieval-augmented, two-stream methods effectively enhance VLM capability, suggesting a promising path toward more accurate, text-explainable CG quality evaluation.

title: ViDia2Std: A Parallel Corpus and Methods for Low-Resource Vietnamese Dialect-to-Standard Translation
summary: Vietnamese exhibits extensive dialectal variation, posing challenges for NLP systems trained predominantly on standard Vietnamese. Such systems often underperform on dialectal inputs, especially from underrepresented Central and Southern regions. Previous work on dialect normalization has focused narrowly on Central-to-Northern dialect transfer using synthetic data and limited dialectal diversity. These efforts exclude Southern varieties and intra-regional variants within the North. We introduce ViDia2Std, the first manually annotated parallel corpus for dialect-to-standard Vietnamese translation covering all 63 provinces. Unlike prior datasets, ViDia2Std includes diverse dialects from Central, Southern, and non-standard Northern regions often absent from existing resources, making it the most dialectally inclusive corpus to date. The dataset consists of over 13,000 sentence pairs sourced from real-world Facebook comments and annotated by native speakers across all three dialect regions. To assess annotation consistency, we define a semantic mapping agreement metric that accounts for synonymous standard mappings across annotators. Based on this criterion, we report agreement rates of 86% (North), 82% (Central), and 85% (South). We benchmark several sequence-to-sequence models on ViDia2Std. mBART-large-50 achieves the best results (BLEU 0.8166, ROUGE-L 0.9384, METEOR 0.8925), while ViT5-base offers competitive performance with fewer parameters. ViDia2Std demonstrates that dialect normalization substantially improves downstream tasks, highlighting the need for dialect-aware resources in building robust Vietnamese NLP systems.

AI.tldr: ViDia2Std introduces the first manually annotated, dialect-to-standard Vietnamese parallel corpus covering all 63 provinces (13k+ real-world sentences), enabling robust dialect normalization; benchmarks show strong seq2seq baselines (mBART-large-50 best) and downstream gains.
AI.motivation: Vietnamese NLP systems trained on standard Vietnamese underperform on diverse dialectal inputs—especially Central and Southern—because prior normalization work is narrow (mostly Central→Northern), synthetic, and excludes many dialects (including non-standard Northern variants). A comprehensive, dialect-inclusive resource is needed.
AI.method: Construct a manually annotated parallel corpus mapping dialectal Vietnamese to standard Vietnamese. Source real Facebook comments from all 63 provinces; use native annotators from North, Central, and South. Define a semantic mapping agreement metric that tolerates multiple synonymous standard forms. Benchmark multiple sequence-to-sequence models (e.g., mBART-large-50, ViT5-base) on the task.
AI.result: Corpus: 13k+ sentence pairs spanning North, Central, and South. Inter-annotator semantic agreement: 86% (North), 82% (Central), 85% (South). mBART-large-50 performs best (BLEU 0.8166, ROUGE-L 0.9384, METEOR 0.8925); ViT5-base is competitive with fewer parameters. Normalization boosts performance on downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: ViDia2Std is the most dialectally inclusive Vietnamese normalization resource to date. High-quality manual annotations, broad dialect coverage, and strong baselines demonstrate that dialect-aware data is crucial for robust Vietnamese NLP and that normalization meaningfully improves downstream applications.

title: Attribution as Retrieval: Model-Agnostic AI-Generated Image Attribution
comment: To appear in CVPR 2026, Code is at https://github.com/hongsong-wang/LIDA
summary: With the rapid advancement of AIGC technologies, image forensics will encounter unprecedented challenges. Traditional methods are incapable of dealing with increasingly realistic images generated by rapidly evolving image generation techniques. To facilitate the identification of AI-generated images and the attribution of their source models, generative image watermarking and AI-generated image attribution have emerged as key research focuses in recent years. However, existing methods are model-dependent, requiring access to the generative models and lacking generality and scalability to new and unseen generators. To address these limitations, this work presents a new paradigm for AI-generated image attribution by formulating it as an instance retrieval problem instead of a conventional image classification problem. We propose an efficient model-agnostic framework, called Low-bIt-plane-based Deepfake Attribution (LIDA). The input to LIDA is produced by Low-Bit Fingerprint Generation module, while the training involves Unsupervised Pre-Training followed by subsequent Few-Shot Attribution Adaptation. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that LIDA achieves state-of-the-art performance for both Deepfake detection and image attribution under zero- and few-shot settings. The code is at https://github.com/hongsong-wang/LIDA

AI.tldr: Model-agnostic deepfake attribution reframed as instance retrieval using low-bit-plane fingerprints, with unsupervised pretraining and few-shot adaptation, achieving state-of-the-art zero-/few-shot detection and attribution.
AI.motivation: AI-generated images are increasingly realistic, making traditional forensics ineffective. Existing watermarking/attribution methods often depend on access to specific generative models, limiting generality and scalability to unseen generators. A general, scalable attribution method is needed.
AI.method: Reformulate AI-generated image attribution as an instance retrieval problem rather than classification. Propose LIDA, a model-agnostic framework: (1) Low-Bit Fingerprint Generation to derive robust low-bit-plane representations; (2) Unsupervised Pre-Training to learn generalizable features; (3) Few-Shot Attribution Adaptation for new/unseen generators.
AI.result: Across comprehensive experiments, LIDA achieves state-of-the-art performance for both deepfake detection and source-model attribution under zero-shot and few-shot scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Treating attribution as retrieval and leveraging low-bit fingerprints with unsupervised pretraining yields a scalable, general solution. LIDA is effective for detecting AI-generated images and attributing them to source models without needing access to the generators.

title: Sabiá-4 Technical Report
summary: This technical report presents Sabiá-4 and Sabiazinho-4, a new generation of Portuguese language models with a focus on Brazilian Portuguese language. The models were developed through a four-stage training pipeline: continued pre-training on Portuguese and Brazilian legal corpora, long-context extension to 128K tokens, supervised fine-tuning on instruction data spanning chat, code, legal tasks, and function calling, and preference alignment. We evaluate the models on six benchmark categories: conversational capabilities in Brazilian Portuguese, knowledge of Brazilian legislation, long-context understanding, instruction following, standardized exams, and agentic capabilities including tool use and web navigation. Results show that Sabiá-4 and Sabiazinho-4 achieve a favorable cost-performance trade-off compared to other models, positioning them in the upper-left region of the pricing-accuracy chart. The models show improvements over previous generations in legal document drafting, multi-turn dialogue quality, and agentic task completion.

AI.tldr: Sabiá-4 and Sabiazinho-4 are Brazilian Portuguese–focused LLMs with 128K context, trained via continued PT/BR-legal pretraining, instruction SFT (chat/code/legal/function-calling), and preference alignment; evaluated across six BR-centric benchmarks, they offer strong price–performance and improve prior Sabiá generations in legal drafting, multi-turn dialogue, and agentic tasks.
AI.motivation: Provide high-quality, cost-efficient LLMs specialized for Brazilian Portuguese, especially for legal knowledge, long-context processing, and agentic/tool-using scenarios, addressing gaps in general-purpose models and improving on previous Sabiá models.
AI.method: Four-stage pipeline: (1) continued pretraining on Portuguese and Brazilian legal corpora; (2) long-context extension to 128K tokens; (3) supervised fine-tuning on diverse instruction data (chat, code, legal tasks, function calling); (4) preference alignment. Evaluation spans six categories: BR-PT conversation, Brazilian legislation knowledge, long-context understanding, instruction following, standardized exams, and agentic capabilities (tool use, web navigation).
AI.result: Models are positioned in the favorable upper-left of a pricing–accuracy chart, indicating a strong cost–performance trade-off. They show improvements over previous generations in legal document drafting, multi-turn dialogue quality, and agentic task completion, and competitive performance across the six benchmark categories.
AI.conclusion: Sabiá-4 and Sabiazinho-4 emerge as practical, cost-effective LLMs for Brazilian Portuguese with notable strengths in legal domains, long-context reasoning, and tool-enabled agent workflows. While the abstract highlights relative gains and price–performance, it omits absolute metrics and dataset details, which would be needed to fully assess generalizability and state-of-the-art standing.

title: Need for Speed: Zero-Shot Depth Completion with Single-Step Diffusion
summary: We introduce Marigold-SSD, a single-step, late-fusion depth completion framework that leverages strong diffusion priors while eliminating the costly test-time optimization typically associated with diffusion-based methods. By shifting computational burden from inference to finetuning, our approach enables efficient and robust 3D perception under real-world latency constraints. Marigold-SSD achieves significantly faster inference with a training cost of only 4.5 GPU days. We evaluate our method across four indoor and two outdoor benchmarks, demonstrating strong cross-domain generalization and zero-shot performance compared to existing depth completion approaches. Our approach significantly narrows the efficiency gap between diffusion-based and discriminative models. Finally, we challenge common evaluation protocols by analyzing performance under varying input sparsity levels. Page: https://dtu-pas.github.io/marigold-ssd/

AI.tldr: Marigold-SSD is a single-step, late-fusion depth completion method that uses diffusion priors without costly test-time optimization, delivering much faster inference while maintaining strong accuracy and cross-domain generalization.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based depth completion methods are accurate but slow due to test-time optimization; discriminative models are fast but may lack strong priors. The goal is to combine diffusion priors with real-time feasibility by moving computation from inference to training.
AI.method: A single-step, late-fusion framework that integrates strong diffusion priors for depth completion while eliminating iterative test-time refinement. The approach shifts computational load to finetuning, enabling efficient inference under latency constraints.
AI.result: Significantly faster inference with only ~4.5 GPU-days of training. Strong performance across four indoor and two outdoor benchmarks, with notable cross-domain generalization and zero-shot capability, reducing the efficiency gap between diffusion-based and discriminative approaches.
AI.conclusion: Marigold-SSD delivers efficient, robust depth completion by fusing diffusion priors in a one-shot pipeline, making diffusion-based methods practical for real-time 3D perception and highlighting the need to evaluate under varying input sparsity levels.

title: S-GRADES -- Studying Generalization of Student Response Assessments in Diverse Evaluative Settings
summary: Evaluating student responses, from long essays to short factual answers, is a key challenge in educational NLP. Automated Essay Scoring (AES) focuses on holistic writing qualities such as coherence and argumentation, while Automatic Short Answer Grading (ASAG) emphasizes factual correctness and conceptual understanding. Despite their shared goal, these paradigms have progressed in isolation with fragmented datasets, inconsistent metrics, and separate communities. We introduce S-GRADES (Studying Generalization of Student Response Assessments in Diverse Evaluative Settings), a web-based benchmark that consolidates 14 diverse grading datasets under a unified interface with standardized access and reproducible evaluation protocols. The benchmark is fully open-source and designed for extensibility, enabling continuous integration of new datasets and evaluation settings. To demonstrate the utility of S-GRADES, we evaluate three state-of-the-art large language models across the benchmark using multiple reasoning strategies in prompting. We further examine the effects of exemplar selection and cross-dataset exemplar transfer. Our analyses illustrate how benchmark-driven evaluation reveals reliability and generalization gaps across essay and short-answer grading tasks, highlighting the importance of standardized, cross-paradigm assessment.

AI.tldr: S-GRADES is an open-source, extensible benchmark that unifies 14 datasets for grading student responses (both AES and ASAG) with standardized access and evaluation, and shows that current state-of-the-art LLMs exhibit reliability and generalization gaps across tasks and settings.
AI.motivation: Evaluation of student responses is fragmented between AES and ASAG, using different datasets, metrics, and research communities, which hampers comparability, progress, and understanding of generalization. A unified, cross-paradigm benchmark is needed to assess reliability and transfer systematically.
AI.method: Develop a web-based benchmark (S-GRADES) consolidating 14 grading datasets under a single interface with standardized, reproducible evaluation protocols; design for extensibility. Evaluate three state-of-the-art LLMs using multiple prompting strategies, analyze exemplar selection, and study cross-dataset exemplar transfer.
AI.result: Using S-GRADES to test LLMs uncovers reliability and generalization gaps between essay and short-answer grading; prompting strategies and exemplar selection affect performance, and cross-dataset exemplar transfer has measurable effects.
AI.conclusion: Standardized, cross-paradigm evaluation is crucial for trustworthy automated grading. S-GRADES provides the infrastructure to reveal current limitations and to support ongoing, comparable research across AES and ASAG.
