title: Layer Consistency Matters: Elegant Latent Transition Discrepancy for Generalizable Synthetic Image Detection
summary: Recent rapid advancement of generative models has significantly improved the fidelity and accessibility of AI-generated synthetic images. While enabling various innovative applications, the unprecedented realism of these synthetics makes them increasingly indistinguishable from authentic photographs, posing serious security risks, such as media credibility and content manipulation. Although extensive efforts have been dedicated to detecting synthetic images, most existing approaches suffer from poor generalization to unseen data due to their reliance on model-specific artifacts or low-level statistical cues. In this work, we identify a previously unexplored distinction that real images maintain consistent semantic attention and structural coherence in their latent representations, exhibiting more stable feature transitions across network layers, whereas synthetic ones present discernible distinct patterns. Therefore, we propose a novel approach termed latent transition discrepancy (LTD), which captures the inter-layer consistency differences of real and synthetic images. LTD adaptively identifies the most discriminative layers and assesses the transition discrepancies across layers. Benefiting from the proposed inter-layer discriminative modeling, our approach exceeds the base model by 14.35\% in mean Acc across three datasets containing diverse GANs and DMs. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LTD outperforms recent state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior detection accuracy, generalizability, and robustness. The code is available at https://github.com/yywencs/LTD

AI.tldr: They detect AI-generated images by measuring how consistently features transition across neural network layers, finding real photos have stable, coherent latent transitions while synthetics do not. Their LTD method leverages this cue to outperform prior detectors and generalize better to unseen generators.
AI.motivation: Synthetic images from modern GANs and diffusion models are highly realistic and often indistinguishable from real photos, creating risks for media integrity. Existing detectors overfit to model-specific or low-level artifacts, yielding poor cross-model and cross-dataset generalization. The authors seek a more intrinsic, model-agnostic signal to separate real from synthetic.
AI.method: Propose Latent Transition Discrepancy (LTD), which analyzes inter-layer dynamics of features inside a network. LTD measures consistency of semantic attention and structural coherence across layers, adaptively selects the most discriminative layers, and quantifies transition discrepancies to classify images as real or synthetic. The approach is plug-and-play over a base model and emphasizes inter-layer discriminative modeling rather than static feature artifacts.
AI.result: Across three datasets spanning diverse GANs and diffusion models, LTD improves mean accuracy of the base model by 14.35% and surpasses recent state-of-the-art detectors, with stronger detection accuracy, generalization to unseen generators, and robustness under various conditions.
AI.conclusion: Inter-layer feature transition consistency is a robust, model-agnostic cue for synthetic image detection. By exploiting latent transition discrepancies, LTD achieves superior accuracy and generalization compared to artifact-based methods, offering a practical and resilient solution for real-vs-synthetic image forensics. Code released at the linked repository.

title: GR-SAP: Generative Replay for Safety Alignment Preservation during Fine-Tuning
summary: Recent studies show that the safety alignment of large language models (LLMs) can be easily compromised even by seemingly non-adversarial fine-tuning. To preserve safety alignment during fine-tuning, a widely used strategy is to jointly optimize safety and task objectives by mixing in the original alignment data, which is typically inaccessible even for open-weight LLMs. Inspired by generative replay in continual learning, we propose Generative Replay for Safety Alignment Preservation (GR-SAP), a unified framework that synthesizes domain-specific alignment data from LLMs and integrate them during downstream adaption to preserve safety alignment. Theoretical and empirical analyses demonstrate this synthetic data serves as a reliable proxy for the original alignment data. Experiments across various models and downstream tasks show that GR-SAP substantially mitigates fine-tuning-induced safety degradation while maintaining comparable downstream performance. Our code is available at https://github.com/chili-lab/gr-sap.

AI.tldr: GR-SAP uses generative replay to synthesize domain-specific safety-aligned data and mixes it into fine-tuning, preserving LLM safety without needing the original alignment dataset. Theory and experiments show it mitigates safety degradation while maintaining task performance.
AI.motivation: Benign fine-tuning can erode LLM safety alignment, yet the standard fix—mixing in the original alignment data—is often impossible because such data are inaccessible. The authors seek a practical, model-agnostic way to retain safety during downstream adaptation without proprietary alignment sets, drawing on continual learning ideas.
AI.method: Generative Replay for Safety Alignment Preservation (GR-SAP): (1) prompt LLM(s) to generate domain-specific safety-aligned examples; (2) integrate these synthetic alignment examples into the downstream fine-tuning objective alongside task data; (3) validate, theoretically and empirically, that the synthetic data approximates the role of original alignment data as a regularizer preserving safety behavior.
AI.result: Across multiple models and tasks, GR-SAP substantially reduces safety degradation induced by fine-tuning, while keeping downstream task performance comparable to baselines. Analyses support that synthetic alignment data acts as a reliable proxy for the original alignment set.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic generative replay is a viable, general strategy to preserve safety alignment during downstream fine-tuning when the original alignment data is unavailable, achieving safety retention without sacrificing utility.

title: Is this Idea Novel? An Automated Benchmark for Judgment of Research Ideas
summary: Judging the novelty of research ideas is crucial for advancing science, enabling the identification of unexplored directions, and ensuring contributions meaningfully extend existing knowledge rather than reiterate minor variations. However, given the exponential growth of scientific literature, manually judging the novelty of research ideas through literature reviews is labor-intensive, subjective, and infeasible at scale. Therefore, recent efforts have proposed automated approaches for research idea novelty judgment. Yet, evaluation of these approaches remains largely inconsistent and is typically based on non-standardized human evaluations, hindering large-scale, comparable evaluations. To address this, we introduce RINoBench, the first comprehensive benchmark for large-scale evaluation of research idea novelty judgments. It comprises 1,381 research ideas derived from and judged by human experts as well as nine automated evaluation metrics designed to assess both rubric-based novelty scores and textual justifications of novelty judgments. Using this benchmark, we evaluate several state-of-the-art large language models (LLMs) on their ability to judge the novelty of research ideas. Our findings reveal that while LLM-generated reasoning closely mirrors human rationales, this alignment does not reliably translate into accurate novelty judgments, which diverge significantly from human gold standard judgments - even among leading reasoning-capable models. Data and code available at: https://github.com/TimSchopf/RINoBench.

AI.tldr: RINoBench is a standardized benchmark to evaluate how well automated systems—especially LLMs—judge the novelty of research ideas; it shows LLM rationales can resemble human reasoning, but their novelty judgments still misalign with expert gold standards.
AI.motivation: Manual novelty assessment via literature reviews is slow, subjective, and unscalable amid exploding scientific output. Existing automated methods lack consistent, comparable evaluation, impeding progress and fair benchmarking.
AI.method: Construct a benchmark of 1,381 research ideas with expert novelty judgments and design nine automated evaluation metrics that score both rubric-based novelty ratings and the quality of textual justifications. Use this suite to test several state-of-the-art LLMs on novelty judgment tasks.
AI.result: LLM-generated explanations often mirror human rationales, but this alignment does not translate into accurate novelty judgments; model decisions diverge notably from expert gold standards, including for strong reasoning-capable models.
AI.conclusion: RINoBench provides the first comprehensive, scalable, and standardized framework for assessing research idea novelty judgments. Current LLMs are not yet reliable novelty judges despite plausible reasoning, highlighting the need for improved methods and evaluation practices.

title: HyPER-GAN: Hybrid Patch-Based Image-to-Image Translation for Real-Time Photorealism Enhancement
summary: Generative models are widely employed to enhance the photorealism of synthetic data for training computer vision algorithms. However, they often introduce visual artifacts that degrade the accuracy of these algorithms and require high computational resources, limiting their applicability in real-time training or evaluation scenarios. In this paper, we propose Hybrid Patch Enhanced Realism Generative Adversarial Network (HyPER-GAN), a lightweight image-to-image translation method based on a U-Net-style generator designed for real-time inference. The model is trained using paired synthetic and photorealism-enhanced images, complemented by a hybrid training strategy that incorporates matched patches from real-world data to improve visual realism and semantic consistency. Experimental results demonstrate that HyPER-GAN outperforms state-of-the-art paired image-to-image translation methods in terms of inference latency, visual realism, and semantic robustness. Moreover, it is illustrated that the proposed hybrid training strategy indeed improves visual quality and semantic consistency compared to training the model solely with paired synthetic and photorealism-enhanced images. Code and pretrained models are publicly available for download at: https://github.com/stefanos50/HyPER-GAN

AI.tldr: HyPER-GAN is a lightweight U-Net–based image-to-image GAN that uses paired synthetic data plus matched real-image patches to boost photorealism in real time, reducing artifacts and improving semantic fidelity while outperforming prior paired translation methods in speed, realism, and robustness.
AI.motivation: Synthetic-to-real enhancement is common for training vision models, but current generators introduce artifacts and are computationally heavy, which hurts downstream accuracy and prevents real-time use. A method is needed that preserves semantic structure, improves realism, and runs efficiently.
AI.method: A U-Net–style generator for image-to-image translation trained adversarially on paired synthetic and photorealism-enhanced images. A hybrid training strategy injects matched patches from real images to guide texture/appearance, improving realism and semantic consistency without heavy models; designed for real-time inference.
AI.result: Experiments show lower inference latency and better visual realism and semantic robustness than state-of-the-art paired i2i methods. An ablation indicates the hybrid patch strategy yields superior visual quality and semantic consistency compared with training only on paired synthetic + enhanced data.
AI.conclusion: HyPER-GAN delivers real-time photorealism enhancement with fewer artifacts and better semantic preservation, validating the hybrid patch approach. Public code and pretrained models facilitate adoption and reproducibility.

title: Splat2Real: Novel-view Scaling for Physical AI with 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: Physical AI faces viewpoint shift between training and deployment, and novel-view robustness is essential for monocular RGB-to-3D perception. We cast Real2Render2Real monocular depth pretraining as imitation-learning-style supervision from a digital twin oracle: a student depth network imitates expert metric depth/visibility rendered from a scene mesh, while 3DGS supplies scalable novel-view observations. We present Splat2Real, centered on novel-view scaling: performance depends more on which views are added than on raw view count. We introduce CN-Coverage, a coverage+novelty curriculum that greedily selects views by geometry gain and an extrapolation penalty, plus a quality-aware guardrail fallback for low-reliability teachers. Across 20 TUM RGB-D sequences with step-matched budgets (N=0 to 2000 additional rendered views, with N unique <= 500 and resampling for larger budgets), naive scaling is unstable; CN-Coverage mitigates worst-case regressions relative to Robot/Coverage policies, and GOL-Gated CN-Coverage provides the strongest medium-high-budget stability with the lowest high-novelty tail error. Downstream control-proxy results versus N provides embodied-relevance evidence by shifting safety/progress trade-offs under viewpoint shift.

AI.tldr: Splat2Real uses 3D Gaussian Splatting to generate targeted novel views and trains a monocular depth network by imitating oracle depth/visibility from a digital-twin mesh. A new CN-Coverage curriculum selects views for maximal geometric coverage with a novelty (extrapolation) penalty and quality-aware gating. On TUM RGB-D, it stabilizes scaling and reduces high-novelty tail errors versus naive/robotic/coverage baselines, with best medium–high budget robustness from a gated variant.
AI.motivation: Physical robots face train–deploy viewpoint shift; simply adding more rendered views can hurt monocular RGB-to-3D performance. The authors seek a principled way to scale novel views so that added data improves robustness, especially under unseen viewpoints, while guarding against unreliable render teachers.
AI.method: Real2Render2Real depth pretraining framed as imitation learning: a student mimics rendered metric depth/visibility from a scene mesh. 3DGS provides scalable novel-view observations. CN-Coverage greedily selects views by geometry gain (coverage) with a novelty/extrapolation penalty; a quality-aware guardrail/gating (GOL-gated) downweights or falls back when teacher reliability is low. Budgets span N=0–2000 additional renders (<=500 uniques with resampling).
AI.result: Across 20 TUM RGB-D sequences, naive view scaling is unstable. CN-Coverage reduces worst-case regressions vs Robot and Coverage policies. GOL-gated CN-Coverage yields the strongest stability at medium–high budgets and lowest high-novelty tail error. Downstream control-proxy performance varies with N, indicating shifts in safety/progress trade-offs under viewpoint shift.
AI.conclusion: Which views you add matters more than how many. A coverage+novelty curriculum with reliability-aware gating makes Real2Render2Real pretraining more stable and robust to novel viewpoints, improving tail performance and offering embodied relevance for control under viewpoint shift.

title: Large language models can disambiguate opioid slang on social media
summary: Social media text shows promise for monitoring trends in the opioid overdose crisis; however, the overwhelming majority of social media text is unrelated to opioids. When leveraging social media text to monitor trends in the ongoing opioid overdose crisis, a common strategy for identifying relevant content is to use a lexicon of opioid-related terms as inclusion criteria. However, many slang terms for opioids, such as "smack" or "blues," have common non-opioid meanings, making them ambiguous. The advanced textual reasoning capability of large language models (LLMs) presents an opportunity to disambiguate these slang terms at scale. We present three tasks on which to evaluate four state-of-the-art LLMs (GPT-4, GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and Claude Sonnet 4.5): a lexicon-based setting, in which the LLM must disambiguate a specific term within the context of a given post; a lexicon-free setting, in which the LLM must identify opioid-related posts from context without a lexicon; and an emergent slang setting, in which the LLM must identify opioid-related posts with simulated new slang terms. All four LLMs showed excellent performance across all tasks. In both subtasks of the lexicon-based setting, LLM F1 scores ("fenty" subtask: 0.824-0.972; "smack" subtask: 0.540-0.862) far exceeded those of the best lexicon strategy (0.126 and 0.009, respectively). In the lexicon-free task, LLM F1 scores (0.544-0.769) surpassed those of lexicons (0.080-0.540), and LLMs demonstrated uniformly higher recall. On emergent slang, all LLMs had higher accuracy (average: 0.784), F1 score (average: 0.712), precision (average: 0.981), and recall (average: 0.587) than the two lexicons assessed. Our results show that LLMs can be used to identify relevant content for low-prevalence topics, including but not limited to opioid references, enhancing data provided to downstream analyses and predictive models.

AI.tldr: LLMs markedly outperform lexicon-based filters at detecting opioid-related social media posts—including ambiguous and emergent slang—yielding higher F1, precision, and recall, and thus better inputs for downstream monitoring and prediction.
AI.motivation: Monitoring the opioid overdose crisis via social media is hampered by low prevalence of relevant posts and ambiguous slang terms that have common non-opioid meanings. Simple lexicon filters either miss true positives or admit many false positives. The study explores whether LLMs’ contextual reasoning can disambiguate slang and generalize beyond fixed lexicons.
AI.method: Four state-of-the-art LLMs (GPT-4, GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Claude Sonnet 4.5) were evaluated on three settings: (1) lexicon-based disambiguation of a target term within a post (e.g., “fenty,” “smack”); (2) lexicon-free detection of opioid-related posts from context alone; and (3) emergent slang detection using simulated new terms. Performance was compared to lexicon baselines using F1, precision, recall, and accuracy.
AI.result: Across all tasks, LLMs outperformed lexicons. Lexicon-based disambiguation: F1 for LLMs was 0.824–0.972 on “fenty” and 0.540–0.862 on “smack,” vs best lexicon F1 of 0.126 and 0.009. Lexicon-free detection: LLM F1 was 0.544–0.769 vs lexicons 0.080–0.540, with uniformly higher recall. Emergent slang: LLMs achieved higher accuracy (avg 0.784), F1 (avg 0.712), precision (avg 0.981), and recall (avg 0.587) than two lexicon baselines.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can robustly identify opioid-related content in noisy, low-prevalence social media streams, including ambiguous and novel slang, substantially surpassing lexicon methods and improving inputs for downstream analyses and predictive models.

title: Mitigating Translationese Bias in Multilingual LLM-as-a-Judge via Disentangled Information Bottleneck
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have become a standard for multilingual evaluation, yet they exhibit a severe systematic translationese bias. In this paper, translationese bias is characterized as LLMs systematically favoring machine-translated text over human-authored references, particularly in low-resource languages. We attribute this bias to spurious correlations with (i) latent manifold alignment with English and (ii) cross-lingual predictability. To mitigate this bias, we propose DIBJudge, a robust fine-tuning framework that learns a minimally sufficient, judgment-critical representation via variational information compression, while explicitly isolating spurious factors into the dedicated bias branch. Furthermore, we incorporate a cross-covariance penalty that explicitly suppresses statistical dependence between robust and bias representations, thereby encouraging effective disentanglement. Extensive evaluations on multilingual reward modeling benchmarks and a dedicated translationese bias evaluation suite demonstrate that the proposed DIBJudge consistently outperforms strong baselines and substantially mitigates translationese bias.

AI.tldr: LLM-as-judge systems over-prefer machine-translated (translationese) text, especially in low-resource languages; DIBJudge uses an information-bottleneck-based, disentangled representation with a cross-covariance penalty to suppress spurious factors, reducing bias and improving multilingual reward modeling.
AI.motivation: Multilingual evaluations rely on LLM judges that systematically favor translationese over genuine human text, harming fairness and reliability—most acutely for low-resource languages. The authors hypothesize this arises from spurious correlations tied to English manifold alignment and higher cross-lingual predictability of translationese.
AI.method: DIBJudge fine-tunes a judge model to learn a minimally sufficient, judgment-critical representation via variational information compression (information bottleneck), while routing spurious signals into a separate bias branch. A cross-covariance penalty discourages statistical dependence between robust and bias representations, encouraging disentanglement. Trained/evaluated within multilingual reward modeling setups.
AI.result: Across multilingual reward modeling benchmarks and a targeted translationese-bias suite, DIBJudge consistently outperforms strong baselines and substantially reduces the tendency to over-score translationese versus human-authored references.
AI.conclusion: Explicit disentanglement plus information compression is effective for mitigating translationese bias in LLM judges, yielding fairer and more reliable multilingual evaluation. This approach suggests a generalizable pathway for debiasing other spurious cross-lingual correlations.

title: Less is More: Decoder-Free Masked Modeling for Efficient Skeleton Representation Learning
comment: Please visit our project page at https://kaist-viclab.github.io/SLiM_site/
summary: The landscape of skeleton-based action representation learning has evolved from Contrastive Learning (CL) to Masked Auto-Encoder (MAE) architectures. However, each paradigm faces inherent limitations: CL often overlooks fine-grained local details, while MAE is burdened by computationally heavy decoders. Moreover, MAE suffers from severe computational asymmetry -- benefiting from efficient masking during pre-training but requiring exhaustive full-sequence processing for downstream tasks. To resolve these bottlenecks, we propose SLiM (Skeleton Less is More), a novel unified framework that harmonizes masked modeling with contrastive learning via a shared encoder. By eschewing the reconstruction decoder, SLiM not only eliminates computational redundancy but also compels the encoder to capture discriminative features directly. SLiM is the first framework with decoder-free masked modeling of representative learning. Crucially, to prevent trivial reconstruction arising from high skeletal-temporal correlation, we introduce semantic tube masking, alongside skeletal-aware augmentations designed to ensure anatomical consistency across diverse temporal granularities. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SLiM consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance across all downstream protocols. Notably, our method delivers this superior accuracy with exceptional efficiency, reducing inference computational cost by 7.89x compared to existing MAE methods.

AI.tldr: SLiM proposes a decoder-free masked modeling framework that unifies masked modeling and contrastive learning with a shared encoder for skeleton-based action representation, using semantic tube masking and skeletal-aware augmentations to learn discriminative features efficiently and achieving SOTA accuracy with ~7.9x lower inference cost than MAE baselines.
AI.motivation: Contrastive learning tends to miss fine-grained local skeletal dynamics, while MAE methods rely on heavy reconstruction decoders and suffer from computational asymmetry—cheap pretraining via masking but expensive full-sequence inference. The field needs a method that preserves local detail, removes decoder overhead, and avoids trivial reconstructions caused by high temporal correlations in skeleton data.
AI.method: A unified, decoder-free framework: (1) a single shared encoder trained jointly with masked modeling and contrastive objectives, eliminating the reconstruction decoder; (2) semantic tube masking that hides coherent spatio-temporal joint trajectories to prevent trivial fill-in; (3) skeletal-aware augmentations that maintain anatomical plausibility across temporal scales so the encoder must capture meaningful structure; the encoder directly learns discriminative representations for downstream tasks.
AI.result: Across standard downstream evaluation protocols, SLiM reaches state-of-the-art performance while being far more efficient at inference, reducing compute by 7.89x compared with MAE-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: Decoder-free masked modeling is viable and effective for skeleton action representation when paired with contrastive learning and carefully designed masking/augmentations. SLiM removes computational redundancy, compels the encoder to learn discriminative features, and sets new SOTA with substantial efficiency gains.

title: Dynamic Knowledge Fusion for Multi-Domain Dialogue State Tracking
summary: The performance of task-oriented dialogue models is strongly tied to how well they track dialogue states, which records and updates user information across multi-turn interactions. However, current multi-domain DST encounters two key challenges: the difficulty of effectively modeling dialogue history and the limited availability of annotated data, both of which hinder model performance. To tackle the aforementioned problems, we develop a dynamic knowledge fusion framework applicable to multi-domain DST. The model operates in two stages: first, an encoder-only network trained with contrastive learning encodes dialogue history and candidate slots, selecting relevant slots based on correlation scores; second, dynamic knowledge fusion leverages the structured information of selected slots as contextual prompts to enhance the accuracy and consistency of dialogue state tracking. This design enables more accurate integration of dialogue context and domain knowledge. Results obtained from multi-domain dialogue benchmarks indicate that our method notably improves both tracking accuracy and generalization, validating its capability in handling complex dialogue scenarios.

AI.tldr: Two-stage framework for multi-domain dialogue state tracking that selects relevant slots via contrastive learning and then fuses their structured knowledge as prompts, yielding higher accuracy and better generalization on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Multi-domain DST struggles to model long, multi-turn history and suffers from scarce annotations, limiting accuracy and robustness. A method that better integrates dialogue context with domain knowledge while being data-efficient is needed.
AI.method: Stage 1: An encoder-only network trained with contrastive learning encodes dialogue history and candidate slots, computing correlation scores to select the most relevant slots. Stage 2: Dynamic knowledge fusion uses the structured information of these selected slots as contextual prompts to guide DST, improving accuracy and consistency across domains.
AI.result: On standard multi-domain dialogue benchmarks, the approach significantly improves tracking accuracy and generalization compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic knowledge fusion paired with contrastive slot selection effectively integrates context and domain knowledge, alleviating history-modeling and data-scarcity issues for complex multi-domain DST, leading to more accurate and consistent state tracking.

title: Are Video Reasoning Models Ready to Go Outside?
summary: In real-world deployment, vision-language models often encounter disturbances such as weather, occlusion, and camera motion. Under such conditions, their understanding and reasoning degrade substantially, revealing a gap between clean, controlled (i.e., unperturbed) evaluation settings and real-world robustness. To address this limitation, we propose ROVA, a novel training framework that improves robustness by modeling a robustness-aware consistency reward under spatio-temporal corruptions. ROVA introduces a difficulty-aware online training strategy that prioritizes informative samples based on the model's evolving capability. Specifically, it continuously re-estimates sample difficulty via self-reflective evaluation, enabling adaptive training with a robustness-aware consistency reward. We also introduce PVRBench, a new benchmark that injects real-world perturbations into embodied video datasets to assess both accuracy and reasoning quality under realistic disturbances. We evaluate ROVA and baselines on PVRBench, UrbanVideo, and VisBench, where open-source and proprietary models suffer up to 35% and 28% drops in accuracy and reasoning under realistic perturbations. ROVA effectively mitigates performance degradation, boosting relative accuracy by at least 24% and reasoning by over 9% compared with baseline models (QWen2.5/3-VL, InternVL2.5, Embodied-R). These gains transfer to clean standard benchmarks, yielding consistent improvements.

AI.tldr: ROVA is a training framework that improves VLM robustness to real-world spatio-temporal corruptions via a robustness-aware consistency reward and a difficulty-aware online curriculum, and introduces PVRBench for evaluation; it delivers sizable robustness gains that also transfer to clean benchmarks.
AI.motivation: VLMs degrade under real-world disturbances (weather, occlusion, camera motion), exposing a gap between clean, controlled evaluations and practical robustness. The work aims to close this gap by training models to remain accurate and reason well despite such perturbations and by offering a realistic benchmark to measure this.
AI.method: ROVA models a robustness-aware consistency reward under spatio-temporal corruptions and pairs it with a difficulty-aware online training strategy. The method re-estimates per-sample difficulty through self-reflective evaluation to prioritize informative samples as the model evolves, enabling adaptive training. Additionally, the authors introduce PVRBench, which injects realistic perturbations into embodied video datasets to evaluate both accuracy and reasoning quality under disturbances.
AI.result: Across PVRBench, UrbanVideo, and VisBench, baseline open-source and proprietary VLMs experience up to 35% and 28% drops in accuracy and reasoning under perturbations. With ROVA, relative accuracy improves by at least 24% and reasoning by over 9% versus strong baselines (QWen2.5/3-VL, InternVL2.5, Embodied-R). These improvements also transfer to clean standard benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: ROVA effectively mitigates robustness degradation under real-world perturbations while maintaining or improving clean-benchmark performance. PVRBench offers a realistic, systematic way to assess robustness and reasoning, helping close the gap between controlled evaluations and deployment conditions.

title: How To Embed Matters: Evaluation of EO Embedding Design Choices
summary: Earth observation (EO) missions produce petabytes of multispectral imagery, increasingly analyzed using large Geospatial Foundation Models (GeoFMs). Alongside end-to-end adaptation, workflows make growing use of intermediate representations as task-agnostic embeddings, enabling models to compute representations once and reuse them across downstream tasks. Consequently, when GeoFMs act as feature extractors, decisions about how representations are obtained, aggregated, and combined affect downstream performance and pipeline scalability. Understanding these trade-offs is essential for scalable embedding-based EO workflows, where compact embeddings can replace raw data while remaining broadly useful. We present a systematic analysis of embedding design in GeoFM-based EO workflows. Leveraging NeuCo-Bench, we study how backbone architecture, pretraining strategy, representation depth, spatial aggregation, and representation combination influence EO task performance. We demonstrate the usability of GeoFM embeddings by aggregating them into fixed-size representations more than 500x smaller than the raw input data. Across models, we find consistent trends: transformer backbones with mean pooling provide strong default embeddings, intermediate ResNet layers can outperform final layers, self-supervised objectives exhibit task-specific strengths, and combining embeddings from different objectives often improves robustness.

AI.tldr: They benchmark how to best build and use compact, reusable embeddings from Geospatial Foundation Models for Earth observation tasks, showing practical recipes that are far smaller than raw data yet retain strong performance.
AI.motivation: EO pipelines increasingly rely on task-agnostic embeddings to scale analysis across petabytes of data, but it is unclear which design choices (extraction depth, pooling, backbones, pretraining, and combination strategies) yield the best trade-off between accuracy, robustness, and storage/computation efficiency.
AI.method: Using NeuCo-Bench, they systematically evaluate embedding design axes—backbone architecture (e.g., transformers vs. ResNets), pretraining objectives (including self-supervised), representation depth (intermediate vs. final layers), spatial aggregation (e.g., mean pooling), and strategies for combining embeddings from different objectives—while compressing imagery into fixed-size representations.
AI.result: They produce fixed-size embeddings over 500x smaller than raw inputs and observe consistent trends: transformers with mean pooling are strong defaults; intermediate ResNet layers can outperform final layers; self-supervised pretraining shows task-dependent advantages; and combining embeddings from different objectives typically improves robustness across tasks.
AI.conclusion: Compact GeoFM embeddings can effectively substitute raw EO data for downstream tasks. Practical guidance emerges: default to transformer backbones with mean pooling, consider intermediate layers (especially for ResNets), pick self-supervised objectives to match task needs, and combine embeddings from varied objectives to boost robustness and generalization.

title: Aligning Large Language Models with Searcher Preferences
summary: The paradigm shift from item-centric ranking to answer-centric synthesis is redefining the role of search engines. While recent industrial progress has applied generative techniques to closed-set item ranking in e-commerce, research and deployment of open-ended generative search on large content platforms remain limited. This setting introduces challenges, including robustness to noisy retrieval, non-negotiable safety guarantees, and alignment with diverse user needs. In this work, we introduce SearchLLM, the first large language model (LLM) for open-ended generative search. We design a hierarchical, multi-dimensional reward system that separates bottom-line constraints, including factual grounding, basic answer quality and format compliance, from behavior optimization objectives that promote robustness to noisy retrieval and alignment with user needs. Concretely, our reward model evaluates responses conditioned on the user query, session history, and retrieved evidence set, combining rule-based checks with human-calibrated LLM judges to produce an interpretable score vector over these dimensions. We introduce a Gated Aggregation Strategy to derive the training reward for optimizing SearchLLM with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). We deploy SearchLLM in the AI search entry of RedNote. Offline evaluations and online A/B tests show improved generation quality and user engagement, increasing Valid Consumption Rate by 1.03% and reducing Re-search Rate by 2.81%, while upholding strict safety and reliability standards.

AI.tldr: Introduces SearchLLM, a production LLM for open‑ended generative search, trained with a hierarchical multi‑dimensional reward scheme and optimized via GRPO; deployed at scale with modest but significant engagement gains while preserving safety and factuality.
AI.motivation: Search is shifting from ranking items to synthesizing answers. Open‑ended generative search on large content platforms is underexplored and faces challenges: robustness to noisy retrieval, hard safety/factuality constraints, and alignment with diverse user intents.
AI.method: Design SearchLLM with a reward model that outputs an interpretable vector across dimensions: bottom‑line constraints (factual grounding, basic quality, format) and behavior objectives (robustness to noisy retrieval, user alignment). The reward model conditions on query, session history, and retrieved evidence, combining rule‑based checks with human‑calibrated LLM judges. A Gated Aggregation Strategy converts the vector into a training signal, and the policy is optimized using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO).
AI.result: Deployed in RedNote’s AI search. Offline and online A/B tests show improved generation quality and engagement, with +1.03% Valid Consumption Rate and −2.81% Re‑search Rate, while maintaining strict safety and reliability.
AI.conclusion: A hierarchical, gated reward approach enables safe, robust open‑ended generative search at scale. SearchLLM demonstrates measurable user engagement gains without compromising safety, suggesting a viable path for production‑grade answer‑centric search.

title: A$^2$-Edit: Precise Reference-Guided Image Editing of Arbitrary Objects and Ambiguous Masks
summary: We propose \textbf{A$^2$-Edit}, a unified inpainting framework for arbitrary object categories, which allows users to replace any target region with a reference object using only a coarse mask. To address the issues of severe homogenization and limited category coverage in existing datasets, we construct a large-scale, multi-category dataset \textbf{UniEdit-500K}, which includes 8 major categories, 209 fine-grained subcategories, and a total of 500,104 image pairs. Such rich category diversity poses new challenges for the model, requiring it to automatically learn semantic relationships and distinctions across categories. To this end, we introduce the \textbf{Mixture of Transformer} module, which performs differentiated modeling of various object categories through dynamic expert selection, and further enhances cross-category semantic transfer and generalization through collaboration among experts. In addition, we propose a \textbf{Mask Annealing Training Strategy} (MATS) that progressively relaxes mask precision during training, reducing the model's reliance on accurate masks and improving robustness across diverse editing tasks. Extensive experiments on benchmarks such as VITON-HD and AnyInsertion demonstrate that A$^2$-Edit consistently outperforms existing approaches across all metrics, providing a new and efficient solution for arbitrary object editing.

AI.tldr: A^2-Edit is a unified, reference-guided inpainting framework that replaces masked regions with objects using only coarse masks. It couples a new large-scale, diverse dataset (UniEdit-500K) with a Mixture-of-Transformer expert module and a Mask Annealing Training Strategy, achieving state-of-the-art results on VITON-HD and AnyInsertion.
AI.motivation: Existing editing datasets are homogenized and narrow in category coverage, and current methods often rely on precise masks and struggle to generalize across categories. The goal is robust, category-agnostic object replacement with minimal user input (coarse masks) while capturing fine-grained semantic distinctions.
AI.method: 1) Curate UniEdit-500K with 8 major categories and 209 subcategories (500,104 pairs) to enable broad cross-category learning. 2) Introduce a Mixture of Transformer with dynamic expert selection to model category-specific nuances while enabling cross-category semantic transfer through expert collaboration. 3) Apply Mask Annealing Training Strategy that gradually loosens mask precision during training to reduce mask dependency and increase robustness.
AI.result: Across benchmarks (VITON-HD and AnyInsertion), A^2-Edit consistently surpasses prior methods on all reported metrics, showing stronger generalization across categories and resilience to imprecise masks. Specific numeric gains are not provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: A^2-Edit provides an effective, scalable solution for arbitrary object editing from coarse masks, and UniEdit-500K plus the Mixture-of-Transformer + MATS pipeline improve cross-category semantic modeling and robustness. The framework is positioned as a new state of the art for general-purpose visual object editing.

title: Learning to Negotiate: Multi-Agent Deliberation for Collective Value Alignment in LLMs
summary: The alignment of large language models (LLMs) has progressed substantially in single-agent settings through paradigms such as RLHF and Constitutional AI, with recent work exploring scalable alternatives such as RLAIF and evolving alignment objectives. However, these approaches remain limited in multi-stakeholder settings, where conflicting values arise and deliberative negotiation capabilities are required. This work proposes a multi-agent negotiation-based alignment framework that aligns LLMs to Collective Agency (CA)-an existing alignment objective introduced to promote the continual expansion of agency-while simultaneously improving conflict-resolution capability. To enable scalable training, two self-play instances of the same LLM, assigned opposing personas, engage in structured turn-based dialogue to synthesize mutually beneficial solutions. We generate synthetic moral-dilemma prompts and conflicting persona pairs, and optimize the policy via RLAIF using GRPO with an external LLM reward model. While rewards are computed from CA scores assigned to the final completion, gradients are applied to dialogue tokens to directly improve deliberative interaction dynamics. Experiments show that the resulting model achieves CA alignment comparable to a single-agent baseline while substantially improving conflict-resolution performance without degrading general language capabilities. These results suggest that negotiation-driven deliberation training provides a practical path toward LLMs that better support collective decision-making in value-conflict scenarios.

AI.tldr: Train an LLM with self-play negotiation between oppositional personas, optimizing a Collective Agency (CA) objective via RLAIF/GRPO with rewards from an external LLM. Gradients target dialogue tokens to shape deliberation, yielding CA alignment on par with baselines and markedly better conflict resolution without hurting general abilities.
AI.motivation: Single-agent alignment (RLHF, Constitutional AI) scales but struggles when multiple stakeholders hold conflicting values, where negotiation and deliberation are essential. The work seeks an approach that scales to such settings while aligning to the CA objective (promoting the continual expansion of agency) and improving conflict-resolution competence.
AI.method: Use two instances of the same LLM in self-play, each given an opposing persona. They conduct structured, turn-based dialogues on synthetic moral-dilemma prompts and persona conflicts. Train with RLAIF using GRPO and an external LLM reward model: rewards are CA scores computed on the final completion, while gradients are applied to dialogue tokens to directly improve the interaction dynamics and negotiation behavior.
AI.result: The trained model matches a single-agent baseline on CA alignment, substantially improves conflict-resolution outcomes, and maintains general language performance.
AI.conclusion: Negotiation-driven deliberation via self-play provides a scalable route to align LLMs with CA while enhancing their ability to resolve value conflicts, making them more suitable for collective decision-making contexts.

title: Bioinspired CNNs for border completion in occluded images
summary: We exploit the mathematical modeling of the border completion problem in the visual cortex to design convolutional neural network (CNN) filters that enhance robustness to image occlusions. We evaluate our CNN architecture, BorderNet, on three occluded datasets (MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and EMNIST) under two types of occlusions: stripes and grids. In all cases, BorderNet demonstrates improved performance, with gains varying depending on the severity of the occlusions and the dataset.

AI.tldr: BorderNet is a CNN architecture with filters inspired by visual cortex border completion, yielding improved robustness to occlusion across MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and EMNIST under stripe and grid occlusions.
AI.motivation: Standard CNNs degrade under occlusions, whereas the human visual system can perceptually complete borders. The paper seeks to transfer this biological prior into CNNs to improve recognition on occluded images.
AI.method: Mathematically model border completion mechanisms from the visual cortex and instantiate them as specialized convolutional filters within a CNN dubbed BorderNet. Evaluate on synthetically occluded versions of MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and EMNIST using stripe and grid masks at varying severities.
AI.result: Across all datasets and both occlusion types, BorderNet outperforms baselines, with performance gains increasing with occlusion severity and varying by dataset (exact numbers not provided in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: Embedding biologically inspired border-completion filters into CNNs improves robustness to image occlusions, suggesting a promising path for integrating neuro-inspired priors into deep vision models.

title: PEEM: Prompt Engineering Evaluation Metrics for Interpretable Joint Evaluation of Prompts and Responses
summary: Prompt design is a primary control interface for large language models (LLMs), yet standard evaluations largely reduce performance to answer correctness, obscuring why a prompt succeeds or fails and providing little actionable guidance. We propose PEEM (Prompt Engineering Evaluation Metrics), a unified framework for joint and interpretable evaluation of both prompts and responses. PEEM defines a structured rubric with 9 axes: 3 prompt criteria (clarity/structure, linguistic quality, fairness) and 6 response criteria (accuracy, coherence, relevance, objectivity, clarity, conciseness), and uses an LLM-based evaluator to output (i) scalar scores on a 1-5 Likert scale and (ii) criterion-specific natural-language rationales grounded in the rubric. Across 7 benchmarks and 5 task models, PEEM's accuracy axis strongly aligns with conventional accuracy while preserving model rankings (aggregate Spearman rho about 0.97, Pearson r about 0.94, p < 0.001). A multi-evaluator study with four models shows consistent relative judgments (pairwise rho = 0.68-0.85), supporting evaluator-agnostic deployment. Beyond alignment, PEEM captures complementary linguistic failure modes and remains informative under prompt perturbations: prompt-quality trends track downstream accuracy under iterative rewrites, semantic adversarial manipulations induce clear score degradation, and meaning-preserving paraphrases yield high stability (robustness rate about 76.7-80.6%). Finally, using only PEEM scores and rationales as feedback, a zero-shot prompt rewriting loop improves downstream accuracy by up to 11.7 points, outperforming supervised and RL-based prompt-optimization baselines. Overall, PEEM provides a reproducible, criterion-driven protocol that links prompt formulation to response behavior and enables systematic diagnosis and optimization of LLM interactions.

AI.tldr: PEEM is an LLM-judged, rubric-driven framework that jointly evaluates prompts and responses on 9 criteria, aligns tightly with standard accuracy, is reasonably evaluator-agnostic and robust to paraphrase, surfaces linguistic failure modes, and its feedback alone can improve task accuracy by up to 11.7 points.
AI.motivation: Conventional LLM evaluations focus on answer correctness, obscuring why prompts succeed or fail and offering little actionable guidance for prompt improvement. The authors seek an interpretable, reproducible, and joint assessment of prompt quality and response behavior that can diagnose failures and drive optimization.
AI.method: Define a 9-axis rubric (3 prompt: clarity/structure, linguistic quality, fairness; 6 response: accuracy, coherence, relevance, objectivity, clarity, conciseness). Use an LLM evaluator to produce 1–5 Likert scores plus rubric-grounded rationales per criterion. Validate across 7 benchmarks and 5 task models; assess evaluator consistency with four different evaluators; test robustness via prompt rewrites, semantic adversaries, and paraphrases; and run a zero-shot prompt-rewriting loop using only PEEM feedback.
AI.result: PEEM’s accuracy axis correlates strongly with conventional accuracy and preserves model rankings (Spearman ≈0.97, Pearson ≈0.94, p<0.001). Multi-evaluator agreement is high (pairwise rho 0.68–0.85). Robustness to meaning-preserving paraphrases is strong (≈76.7–80.6%); semantic adversarial changes degrade scores clearly. Prompt-quality tracks downstream accuracy during iterative rewrites. Using PEEM scores and rationales as the sole feedback, zero-shot prompt rewriting boosts accuracy by up to 11.7 points, outperforming supervised and RL-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: PEEM offers a reproducible, criterion-driven, and evaluator-agnostic protocol that links prompt formulation to response behavior. It enables interpretable diagnosis of failures and practical optimization of prompts, complementing traditional accuracy-centric evaluations.

title: RandMark: On Random Watermarking of Visual Foundation Models
summary: Being trained on large and diverse datasets, visual foundation models (VFMs) can be fine-tuned to achieve remarkable performance and efficiency in various downstream computer vision tasks. The high computational cost of data collection and training makes these models valuable assets, which motivates some VFM owners to distribute them alongside a license to protect their intellectual property rights. In this paper, we propose an approach to ownership verification of visual foundation models that leverages a small encoder-decoder network to embed digital watermarks into an internal representation of a hold-out set of input images. The method is based on random watermark embedding, which makes the watermark statistics detectable in functional copies of the watermarked model. Both theoretically and experimentally, we demonstrate that the proposed method yields a low probability of false detection for non-watermarked models and a low probability of false misdetection for watermarked models.

AI.tldr: They propose watermarking visual foundation models by embedding random statistical signals into internal feature representations (on a secret hold‑out image set) using a small encoder–decoder, enabling ownership verification with provably low false positive/negative rates and detectability even in functional copies of the model.
AI.motivation: VFMs are costly to build and are distributed under licenses; owners need a reliable way to prove model ownership—especially after model stealing, distillation, or unauthorized redistribution—without harming downstream performance.
AI.method: Train a lightweight encoder–decoder to inject a random digital watermark into an internal representation of the model for a private hold‑out set of images. The watermark is designed so its statistics become detectable in any functional copy of the watermarked model. Verification reduces to a statistical test on model responses to the secret inputs, with theoretical bounds on error rates.
AI.result: Theory and experiments show low false detection on non‑watermarked models and low false misdetection on watermarked ones, indicating both specificity and sensitivity of the verifier while maintaining efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Feature‑space random watermarking offers a practical, low‑overhead approach to VFM ownership verification that remains detectable after functional copying, providing a viable tool for IP protection with controllable error rates.

title: Human-AI Co-reasoning for Clinical Diagnosis with Evidence-Integrated Language Agent
summary: We present PULSE, a medical reasoning agent that combines a domain-tuned large language model with scientific literature retrieval to support diagnostic decision-making in complex real-world cases. To evaluate its capabilities, we curated a benchmark of 82 authentic endocrinology case reports encompassing a broad spectrum of disease types and incidence levels. In controlled experiments, we compared PULSE's performance against physicians with varying levels of expertise-from residents to senior specialists-and examined how AI assistance influenced human diagnostic reasoning. PULSE attained expert-competitive accuracy, outperforming residents and junior specialists while matching senior specialist performance at both Top@1 and Top@4 thresholds. Unlike physicians, whose accuracy declined with disease rarity, PULSE maintained stable performance across incidence tiers. The agent also exhibited adaptive reasoning, increasing output length with case difficulty in a manner analogous to the longer deliberation observed among expert clinicians. When used collaboratively, PULSE enabled physicians to correct initial errors and broaden diagnostic hypotheses, but also introduced risks of automation bias. The study explores both serial and concurrent collaboration workflows, revealing that PULSE offers robust support across common and rare presentations. These findings underscore both the promise and the limitations of language model-based agents in clinical diagnosis, and offer a framework for evaluating their role in real-world decision-making.

AI.tldr: PULSE is a domain-tuned LLM agent with literature retrieval that achieves expert-competitive diagnostic accuracy on 82 real endocrinology cases, maintains performance even for rare diseases, adapts its reasoning length to case difficulty, and beneficially augments clinicians—though it can induce automation bias—offering a structured framework to assess AI-clinician collaboration.
AI.motivation: Clinical diagnosis in complex, often rare, endocrinology cases is challenging and time-constrained. There is a need to test whether LLM-based agents can match specialist performance, handle disease rarity, and effectively collaborate with humans without harming decision quality. The authors also aim to provide a realistic evaluation setting and framework for assessing AI’s role in clinical workflows.
AI.method: Build PULSE: a domain-tuned large language model augmented with scientific literature retrieval. Curate a benchmark of 82 authentic endocrinology case reports spanning common-to-rare diseases. Run controlled experiments comparing PULSE to physicians (residents, junior and senior specialists) using Top@1 and Top@4 metrics. Analyze performance across disease incidence tiers and examine adaptive reasoning length vs. case difficulty. Study human-AI collaboration in serial and concurrent workflows, measuring error correction, hypothesis expansion, and automation bias.
AI.result: PULSE outperforms residents and junior specialists and matches senior specialists at Top@1 and Top@4. Unlike physicians, its accuracy does not degrade with disease rarity. It produces longer outputs for more difficult cases, mirroring expert deliberation. In collaborative settings, PULSE helps clinicians correct initial errors and widen differential diagnoses but can also trigger automation bias. It performs robustly across both common and rare presentations.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based agents with retrieval can reach specialist-level diagnostic performance and usefully support clinicians, particularly on rare cases, but they introduce risks (e.g., automation bias) that must be managed. The work provides an empirical framework for evaluating such systems and highlights both their promise and limitations in real-world diagnostic decision-making.

title: UniCom: Unified Multimodal Modeling via Compressed Continuous Semantic Representations
summary: Current unified multimodal models typically rely on discrete visual tokenizers to bridge the modality gap. However, discretization inevitably discards fine-grained semantic information, leading to suboptimal performance in visual understanding tasks. Conversely, directly modeling continuous semantic representations (e.g., CLIP, SigLIP) poses significant challenges in high-dimensional generative modeling, resulting in slow convergence and training instability. To resolve this dilemma, we introduce UniCom, a unified framework that harmonizes multimodal understanding and generation via compressed continuous representation. We empirically demonstrate that reducing channel dimension is significantly more effective than spatial downsampling for both reconstruction and generation. Accordingly, we design an attention-based semantic compressor to distill dense features into a compact unified representation. Furthermore, we validate that the transfusion architecture surpasses query-based designs in convergence and consistency. Experiments demonstrate that UniCom achieves state-of-the-art generation performance among unified models. Notably, by preserving rich semantic priors, it delivers exceptional controllability in image editing and maintains image consistency even without relying on VAE.

AI.tldr: UniCom proposes a unified multimodal framework that uses a compressed continuous representation—achieved by reducing channel dimensions via an attention-based semantic compressor and a transfusion architecture—to harmonize understanding and generation, yielding state-of-the-art unified image generation, faster convergence, strong controllability, and image consistency without a VAE.
AI.motivation: Discrete visual tokenizers bridge modalities but discard fine-grained semantics; directly modeling high-dimensional continuous features (e.g., CLIP/SigLIP) causes slow, unstable generative training. The goal is to keep rich semantic priors while making training efficient and stable for unified understanding and generation.
AI.method: Compress continuous visual features primarily by reducing channel dimensions (rather than spatial downsampling) using an attention-based semantic compressor to distill dense features into a compact, unified latent. Employ a transfusion architecture (instead of query-based designs) to improve convergence and consistency for both understanding and generation tasks.
AI.result: Empirical evidence shows channel-dimension reduction outperforms spatial downsampling for reconstruction and generation. The transfusion design converges better and is more consistent than query-based approaches. UniCom achieves state-of-the-art generation among unified models, offers strong controllability for image editing, and maintains image consistency without relying on a VAE.
AI.conclusion: Compressed continuous representations can reconcile multimodal understanding and generation, preserving semantic detail while stabilizing and accelerating training. UniCom demonstrates that attention-based channel compression and transfusion architecture outperform prior discrete or query-based methods, enabling SOTA unified generation and practical benefits like VAE-free consistency and superior editing control.

title: VERI-DPO: Evidence-Aware Alignment for Clinical Summarization via Claim Verification and Direct Preference Optimization
comment: Paper submitted to AMIA 2026 Annual Symposium
summary: Brief Hospital Course (BHC) narratives must be clinically useful yet faithful to fragmented EHR evidence. LLM-based clinical summarizers still introduce unsupported statements, and alignment can encourage omissions ("say-less" degeneration). We introduce VERI-DPO, which uses claim verification to mine preferences and distill them into the summarizer with Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). On MIMIC-III-Ext-VeriFact-BHC (100 ICU patients; patient-level splits), we train a retrieval-augmented verifier to label claim-evidence pairs as Supported, Not Supported, or Not Addressed via a single-token format. The verifier scores sentence-level claims from sampled BHC candidates and aggregates margins into a coverage-aware utility to mine length-controlled, contradiction-anchored preference pairs. On held-out patients, verifier-mined preferences separate candidates by contradiction density, and VERI-DPO reduces Not Supported claim rates from 10.7% to 1.9% (local verifier judge) and from 11.6% to 6.4% (GPT-4o judge), while improving validity from 76.7% to 82.5% and maintaining informative length.

AI.tldr: VERI-DPO uses an automated claim verifier to mine preference signals and train a summarizer via Direct Preference Optimization, substantially reducing unsupported statements in brief hospital course (BHC) narratives while preserving informativeness.
AI.motivation: Clinical BHC summaries from LLMs often contain unsupported claims, and standard alignment can cause harmful omissions (“say-less” degeneration). The goal is to improve factuality without sacrificing coverage/length in fragmented EHR settings.
AI.method: Train a retrieval-augmented verifier to classify claim–evidence pairs as Supported/Not Supported/Not Addressed using a single-token format. Sample candidate BHCs, score sentence-level claims with the verifier, aggregate margins into a coverage-aware utility, and mine length-controlled, contradiction-anchored preference pairs. Distill these preferences into the summarizer with DPO.
AI.result: On MIMIC-III-Ext-VeriFact-BHC (100 ICU patients; patient-level splits), verifier-mined preferences separate candidates by contradiction density. VERI-DPO reduces Not Supported claim rates from 10.7%→1.9% (local verifier) and 11.6%→6.4% (GPT-4o), increases validity from 76.7%→82.5%, and maintains informative length.
AI.conclusion: Verification-guided preference mining integrated with DPO improves BHC faithfulness and validity without inducing “say-less” degeneration, indicating a practical path for safer clinical summarization.

title: WalkGPT: Grounded Vision-Language Conversation with Depth-Aware Segmentation for Pedestrian Navigation
summary: Ensuring accessible pedestrian navigation requires reasoning about both semantic and spatial aspects of complex urban scenes, a challenge that existing Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) struggle to meet. Although these models can describe visual content, their lack of explicit grounding leads to object hallucinations and unreliable depth reasoning, limiting their usefulness for accessibility guidance. We introduce WalkGPT, a pixel-grounded LVLM for the new task of Grounded Navigation Guide, unifying language reasoning and segmentation within a single architecture for depth-aware accessibility guidance. Given a pedestrian-view image and a navigation query, WalkGPT generates a conversational response with segmentation masks that delineate accessible and harmful features, along with relative depth estimation. The model incorporates a Multi-Scale Query Projector (MSQP) that shapes the final image tokens by aggregating them along text tokens across spatial hierarchies, and a Calibrated Text Projector (CTP), guided by a proposed Region Alignment Loss, that maps language embeddings into segmentation-aware representations. These components enable fine-grained grounding and depth inference without user-provided cues or anchor points, allowing the model to generate complete and realistic navigation guidance. We also introduce PAVE, a large-scale benchmark of 41k pedestrian-view images paired with accessibility-aware questions and depth-grounded answers. Experiments show that WalkGPT achieves strong grounded reasoning and segmentation performance. The source code and dataset are available on the \href{https://sites.google.com/view/walkgpt-26/home}{project website}.

AI.tldr: WalkGPT is a pixel-grounded vision-language model for accessible pedestrian navigation that jointly performs language reasoning, segmentation, and relative depth inference, enabled by new projection modules and losses, and evaluated on a new 41k-image benchmark (PAVE).
AI.motivation: Current LVLMs can describe scenes but often hallucinate objects and fail at reliable depth reasoning because they lack explicit pixel-level grounding, limiting their utility for safety-critical accessibility guidance in complex urban environments.
AI.method: Define a new task, Grounded Navigation Guide. Propose WalkGPT, a single architecture that, given a pedestrian-view image and a navigation query, outputs a conversational answer plus segmentation masks for accessible vs. harmful regions and relative depth. Key components: (1) Multi-Scale Query Projector (MSQP) that aggregates multi-scale image features aligned along text tokens to shape the final image tokens for better grounding; (2) Calibrated Text Projector (CTP) that maps language embeddings into segmentation-aware representations, supervised by a Region Alignment Loss to tie text to pixel regions. The system requires no user-specified anchors or cues.
AI.result: On the new PAVE benchmark of 41k pedestrian-view images with accessibility-aware questions and depth-grounded answers, WalkGPT demonstrates strong grounded reasoning, segmentation quality, and depth-aware guidance compared to prior LVLMs (qualitative and reported quantitative gains, details not provided in the abstract). Code and data are released.
AI.conclusion: Pixel-level grounding and calibrated text projections enable fine-grained, depth-aware navigation guidance without external cues, reducing hallucinations and improving reliability for accessibility; the introduced dataset and code support further research.

title: Safe and Scalable Web Agent Learning via Recreated Websites
summary: Training autonomous web agents is fundamentally limited by the environments they learn from: real-world websites are unsafe to explore, hard to reset, and rarely provide verifiable feedback. We propose VeriEnv, a framework that treats language models as environment creators, automatically cloning real-world websites into fully executable, verifiable synthetic environments. By exposing controlled internal access via a Python SDK, VeriEnv enables agents to self-generate tasks with deterministic, programmatically verifiable rewards, eliminating reliance on heuristic or LLM-based judges. This design decouples agent learning from unsafe real-world interaction while enabling scalable self-evolution through environment expansion. Through experiments on web agent benchmarks, we show that agents trained with VeriEnv generalize to unseen websites, achieve site-specific mastery through self-evolving training, and benefit from scaling the number of training environments. Code and resources will be released at https://github.com/kyle8581/VeriEnv upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: VeriEnv uses language models to clone real websites into safe, executable, and verifiable synthetic web environments so agents can train with deterministic, program-checked rewards, leading to better generalization and site-specific mastery.
AI.motivation: Real websites are unsafe to explore, hard to reset, and lack reliable, verifiable feedback, which hampers training and evaluation of autonomous web agents.
AI.method: Treat LMs as environment creators to auto-clone real sites into synthetic counterparts; expose controlled internal access via a Python SDK so agents can self-generate tasks with deterministic, programmatically verifiable rewards; enable self-evolution by expanding and refining environments over time, decoupling training from live web interaction.
AI.result: On standard web-agent benchmarks, agents trained in VeriEnv generalize to unseen websites, achieve strong site-specific performance through self-evolving training, and improve further as the number of training environments scales.
AI.conclusion: VeriEnv provides a scalable, safe, and verifiable training paradigm that removes dependence on heuristic/LLM judges and live web interaction, enabling robust skill acquisition and transfer for web agents; resources will be released upon acceptance.

title: UAV traffic scene understanding: A cross-spectral guided approach and a unified benchmark
summary: Traffic scene understanding from unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) platforms is crucial for intelligent transportation systems due to its flexible deployment and wide-area monitoring capabilities. However, existing methods face significant challenges in real-world surveillance, as their heavy reliance on optical imagery leads to severe performance degradation under adverse illumination conditions like nighttime and fog. Furthermore, current Visual Question Answering (VQA) models are restricted to elementary perception tasks, lacking the domain-specific regulatory knowledge required to assess complex traffic behaviors. To address these limitations, we propose a novel Cross-spectral Traffic Cognition Network (CTCNet) for robust UAV traffic scene understanding. Specifically, we design a Prototype-Guided Knowledge Embedding (PGKE) module that leverages high-level semantic prototypes from an external Traffic Regulation Memory (TRM) to anchor domain-specific knowledge into visual representations, enabling the model to comprehend complex behaviors and distinguish fine-grained traffic violations. Moreover, we develop a Quality-Aware Spectral Compensation (QASC) module that exploits the complementary characteristics of optical and thermal modalities to perform bidirectional context exchange, effectively compensating for degraded features to ensure robust representation in complex environments. In addition, we construct Traffic-VQA, the first large-scale optical-thermal infrared benchmark for cognitive UAV traffic understanding, comprising 8,180 aligned image pairs and 1.3 million question-answer pairs across 31 diverse types. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CTCNet significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both cognition and perception scenarios. The dataset is available at https://github.com/YuZhang-2004/UAV-traffic-scene-understanding.

AI.tldr: CTCNet integrates optical–thermal fusion with regulation-aware knowledge embeddings to enable robust UAV traffic VQA and violation cognition, and introduces a large optical–thermal Traffic-VQA dataset; it outperforms prior methods, especially under adverse illumination.
AI.motivation: UAV traffic monitoring suffers when relying on optical images in nighttime/fog, and existing VQA lacks embedded traffic-regulation knowledge to reason about complex behaviors and fine-grained violations.
AI.method: Propose Cross-spectral Traffic Cognition Network (CTCNet) with: (1) Prototype-Guided Knowledge Embedding (PGKE) that uses high-level semantic prototypes from a Traffic Regulation Memory (TRM) to infuse domain knowledge into visual features; (2) Quality-Aware Spectral Compensation (QASC) for bidirectional context exchange between optical and thermal streams, compensating degraded modality features; plus a new optical–thermal Traffic-VQA benchmark (8,180 aligned pairs; 1.3M QA; 31 types).
AI.result: CTCNet achieves state-of-the-art results on both cognition (VQA/violation reasoning) and perception tasks, showing strong robustness under challenging conditions; the new dataset supports comprehensive evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Knowledge-anchored, cross-spectral fusion effectively enhances UAV traffic understanding and fine-grained violation detection; the released dataset and approach provide a solid foundation for future research and real-world deployment in adverse environments.

title: AILS-NTUA at SemEval-2026 Task 8: Evaluating Multi-Turn RAG Conversations
summary: We present the AILS-NTUA system for SemEval-2026 Task 8 (MTRAGEval), addressing all three subtasks of multi-turn retrieval-augmented generation: passage retrieval (A), reference-grounded response generation (B), and end-to-end RAG (C). Our unified architecture is built on two principles: (i) a query-diversity-over-retriever-diversity strategy, where five complementary LLM-based query reformulations are issued to a single corpus-aligned sparse retriever and fused via variance-aware nested Reciprocal Rank Fusion; and (ii) a multistage generation pipeline that decomposes grounded generation into evidence span extraction, dual-candidate drafting, and calibrated multi-judge selection. Our system ranks 1st in Task A (nDCG@5: 0.5776, +20.5% over the strongest baseline) and 2nd in Task B (HM: 0.7698). Empirical analysis shows that query diversity over a well-aligned retriever outperforms heterogeneous retriever ensembling, and that answerability calibration-rather than retrieval coverage-is the primary bottleneck in end-to-end performance.

AI.tldr: A unified multi-turn RAG system that favors query diversity over retriever diversity and uses a multistage, calibrated generation pipeline achieves state-of-the-art retrieval and near–state-of-the-art grounded generation at MTRAGEval.
AI.motivation: Improve multi-turn RAG by reducing reliance on heterogeneous retriever ensembles, strengthening grounding fidelity, and tackling the key failure modes in end-to-end RAG (especially answerability/calibration).
AI.method: (1) Retrieval: generate five complementary LLM-based query rewrites for each turn; send them to a single corpus-aligned sparse retriever; fuse results via variance-aware nested Reciprocal Rank Fusion. (2) Generation: decompose grounded response into evidence span extraction, dual-candidate drafting, and a calibrated multi-judge selection step that decides what to answer and how confidently. Unified architecture handles tasks A–C.
AI.result: Task A: 1st place (nDCG@5 = 0.5776, +20.5% over strongest baseline). Task B: 2nd place (HM = 0.7698). Analysis: query diversity over a well-aligned single retriever outperforms heterogeneous retriever ensembling; in end-to-end RAG, answerability calibration, not retrieval coverage, is the main bottleneck.
AI.conclusion: Emphasizing diverse query reformulations with a single aligned retriever and a calibrated, staged generator yields strong retrieval and grounded generation. Future end-to-end gains hinge more on answerability calibration than on further retrieval coverage improvements.

title: eLasmobranc Dataset: An Image Dataset for Elasmobranch Species Recognition and Biodiversity Monitoring
comment: 9 pages, 6 figures, 5 tables. A future extended version of this work will be submitted to Scientific Data
summary:
Elasmobranch populations are experiencing significant global declines, and several species are currently classified as threatened. Reliable monitoring and species-level identification are essential to support conservation and spatial planning initiatives such as Important Shark and Ray Areas (ISRAs). However, existing visual datasets are predominantly detection-oriented, underwater-acquired, or limited to coarse-grained categories, restricting their applicability to fine-grained morphological classification.
  We present the eLasmobranc Dataset, a curated and publicly available image collection from seven ecologically relevant elasmobranch species inhabiting the eastern Spanish Mediterranean coast, a region where two ISRAs have been identified. Images were obtained through dedicated data collection, including field campaigns and collaborations with local fish markets and projects, as well as from open-access public sources. The dataset was constructed predominantly from images acquired outside the aquatic environment under standardized protocols to ensure clear visualization of diagnostic morphological traits. It integrates expert-validated species annotations, structured spatial and temporal metadata, and complementary species-level information.
  The eLasmobranc Dataset is specifically designed to support supervised species-level classification, population studies, and the development of artificial intelligence systems for biodiversity monitoring. By combining morphological clarity, taxonomic reliability, and public accessibility, the dataset addresses a critical gap in fine-grained elasmobranch identification and promotes reproducible research in conservation-oriented computer vision. The dataset is publicly available at https://zenodo.org/records/18549737.

AI.tldr: Introduces eLasmobranc, a publicly available, expert-validated image dataset of seven elasmobranch species from the eastern Spanish Mediterranean, designed for fine-grained, species-level classification and conservation-focused AI.
AI.motivation: Elasmobranchs (sharks and rays) are declining globally, necessitating reliable, species-level monitoring to inform conservation and spatial planning (e.g., ISRAs). Existing visual datasets are mostly detection-focused, underwater, or coarse-grained, limiting fine-grained morphological identification.
AI.method: Curated images primarily taken outside the water under standardized protocols to maximize visibility of diagnostic traits; collected via field campaigns, collaborations with local fish markets and projects, plus open-access sources. All images include expert-validated species labels, structured spatial/temporal metadata, and species-level information; publicly released on Zenodo.
AI.result: A cleaned, morphology-focused dataset covering seven ecologically relevant species from a region with two ISRAs, with taxonomically reliable annotations and rich metadata to support supervised classification and population studies.
AI.conclusion: The dataset fills a key gap for fine-grained elasmobranch identification, enabling reproducible, conservation-oriented computer vision research and improved monitoring to support ISRAs and related management efforts.

title: Automatic End-to-End Data Integration using Large Language Models
comment: 8 pages, 9 tables. Accepted at the Beyond SQL Workshop at ICDE 2026
summary: Designing data integration pipelines typically requires substantial manual effort from data engineers to configure pipeline components and label training data. While LLMs have shown promise in handling individual steps of the integration process, their potential to replace all human input across end-to-end data integration pipelines has not been investigated. As a step toward exploring this potential, we present an automatic data integration pipeline that uses GPT-5.2 to generate all artifacts required to adapt the pipeline to specific use cases. These artifacts are schema mappings, value mappings for data normalization, training data for entity matching, and validation data for selecting conflict resolution heuristics in data fusion. We compare the performance of this LLM-based pipeline to the performance of human-designed pipelines along three case studies requiring the integration of video game, music, and company related data. Our experiments show that the LLM-based pipeline is able to produce similar results, for some tasks even better results, as the human-designed pipelines. End-to-end, the human and the LLM pipelines produce integrated datasets of comparable size and density.
