Having the LLM configure the pipelines costs approximately \$10 per case study, which represents only a small fraction of the cost of having human data engineers perform the same tasks.

AI.tldr: LLM-configured, end-to-end data integration pipeline (using GPT-5.2) automatically generates key artifacts—schema/value mappings, EM training data, and fusion validation data—and achieves performance on par with or better than human-engineered pipelines across three domains, at ~$10 per case.
AI.motivation: Building data integration pipelines demands heavy manual configuration and labeling. While LLMs help with isolated steps, it is unclear whether they can eliminate human input across the entire pipeline. The work investigates this end-to-end automation potential.
AI.method: Implement a fully LLM-driven pipeline where the LLM produces all adaptation artifacts: schema mappings, normalization/value mappings, labeled training data for entity matching, and validation data for selecting conflict-resolution heuristics in data fusion. Evaluate against human-designed pipelines on three real-world case studies (video games, music, companies), comparing task-level performance and end-to-end dataset properties and costs.
AI.result: Across the three case studies, the LLM-based pipeline delivers similar, and in some tasks better, performance than human-built pipelines. The final integrated datasets are comparable in size and density. Configuring the pipeline via the LLM costs about $10 per case—much less than human engineering effort.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end data integration can be largely automated by an LLM while maintaining competitive quality and greatly reducing cost, indicating strong potential for replacing most human configuration in practical pipelines.

title: Taking Shortcuts for Categorical VQA Using Super Neurons
summary: Sparse Attention Vectors (SAVs) have emerged as an excellent training-free alternative to supervised finetuning or low-rank adaptation to improve the performance of Vision Language Models (VLMs). At their heart, SAVs select a few accurate attention heads for a task of interest and use them as classifiers, rather than relying on the model's prediction. In a similar spirit, we find that directly probing the raw activations of the VLM, in the form of scalar values, is sufficient to yield accurate classifiers on diverse visually grounded downstream tasks. Shifting focus from attention vectors to scalar activations dramatically increases the search space for accurate parameters, allowing us to find more discriminative neurons immediately from the first generated token. We call such activations Super Neurons (SNs). In this probing setting, we discover that enough SNs appear in the shallower layers of the large language model to allow for extreme early exiting from the first layer of the model at the first generated token. Compared to the original network, SNs robustly improve the classification performance while achieving a speedup of up to 5.10x.

AI.tldr: They introduce Super Neurons (SNs)—training‑free classifiers built by directly probing scalar neuron activations in VLMs—which outperform the model’s own predictions and prior Sparse Attention Vector (SAV) probes, enabling extreme early exit (even from the first layer/first token) and up to 5.1× speedups.
AI.motivation: SAVs showed that a small set of attention heads can act as task‑specific classifiers without finetuning, but the attention‑only search space is limited. The authors hypothesize that scalar activations across neurons/layers contain richer, earlier, and more discriminative task signals that could be exploited to boost accuracy and reduce latency without training.
AI.method: Probe raw scalar activations of neurons across layers/tokens in a VLM, score/select a sparse set of highly discriminative neurons (SNs), and use their values as a lightweight classifier for visually grounded tasks. Focus on earliest available signals (first generated token) to enable aggressive early exiting, potentially from the first LLM layer.
AI.result: SNs appear abundantly in shallow layers; using them as classifiers improves classification accuracy over the original network’s prediction while enabling up to 5.10× inference speedup via early exit. The approach works across diverse visually grounded downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: Training‑free scalar‑activation probing is a powerful alternative to attention‑head probes and even to the model’s own decoder, yielding better accuracy and large latency reductions by exploiting discriminative signals present in early layers/tokens. This suggests substantial, accessible task information is concentrated in shallow activations, opening avenues for efficient VLM inference.

title: Just-in-Time: Training-Free Spatial Acceleration for Diffusion Transformers
summary: Diffusion Transformers have established a new state-of-the-art in image synthesis, but the high computational cost of iterative sampling severely hampers their practical deployment. While existing acceleration methods often focus on the temporal domain, they overlook the substantial spatial redundancy inherent in the generative process, where global structures emerge long before fine-grained details are formed. The uniform computational treatment of all spatial regions represents a critical inefficiency. In this paper, we introduce Just-in-Time (JiT), a novel training-free framework that addresses this challenge by acceleration in the spatial domain. JiT formulates a spatially approximated generative ordinary differential equation (ODE) that drives the full latent state evolution based on computations from a dynamically selected, sparse subset of anchor tokens. To ensure seamless transitions as new tokens are incorporated to expand the dimensions of the latent state, we propose a deterministic micro-flow, a simple and effective finite-time ODE that maintains both structural coherence and statistical correctness. Extensive experiments on the state-of-the-art FLUX.1-dev model demonstrate that JiT achieves up to a 7x speedup with nearly lossless performance, significantly outperforming existing acceleration methods and establishing a new and superior trade-off between inference speed and generation fidelity.

AI.tldr: JiT is a training-free, spatial-domain acceleration for Diffusion Transformers that evolves only a sparse set of anchor tokens via a spatially approximated ODE and stitches in new tokens with a deterministic micro-flow, yielding up to 7x faster sampling on FLUX.1-dev with near-lossless quality.
AI.motivation: Iterative sampling in Diffusion Transformers is computationally heavy. Prior accelerations largely reduce temporal steps but ignore spatial redundancy: early steps form global structure, yet all spatial tokens are computed uniformly, wasting compute.
AI.method: Formulate a spatially approximated generative ODE driven by a dynamically chosen sparse subset of anchor tokens whose updates approximate the full latent evolution. Expand the latent dimensionality over time by adding tokens; use a deterministic finite-time micro-flow to smoothly integrate new tokens while preserving structure and distributional consistency. Entirely training-free.
AI.result: On the FLUX.1-dev model, JiT delivers up to 7x speedup with negligible quality loss and surpasses existing acceleration methods in the speed–fidelity trade-off.
AI.conclusion: Exploiting spatial redundancy via anchor-token ODE evolution and deterministic micro-flows provides practical, training-free acceleration for DiTs, establishing a stronger speed–quality frontier than temporal-only approaches.

title: End-to-End Chatbot Evaluation with Adaptive Reasoning and Uncertainty Filtering
summary: Large language models (LLMs) combined with retrieval augmented generation have enabled the deployment of domain-specific chatbots, but these systems remain prone to generating unsupported or incorrect answers. Reliable evaluation is therefore critical, yet manual review is costly and existing frameworks often depend on curated test sets and static metrics, limiting scalability. We propose an end-to-end automatic evaluator designed to substantially reduce human effort. Our system generates Q\&A pairs directly from the underlying knowledge base, uses LLMs to judge chatbot responses against reference answers, and applies confidence-based filtering to highlight uncertain cases. Applied to a Vietnamese news dataset, the evaluator achieves high agreement with human judgments while significantly lowering review overhead. The framework is modular and language-agnostic, making it readily adaptable to diverse domains. This work introduces a practical, scalable solution for evaluating chatbots with minimal reliance on manual intervention.

AI.tldr: They present a modular, language-agnostic, end-to-end automatic evaluator for RAG chatbots that generates Q&A from a knowledge base, uses an LLM-as-judge with confidence scoring, and greatly reduces human review while matching human judgments on a Vietnamese news corpus.
AI.motivation: RAG chatbots still hallucinate or cite-unsupported content. Manual evaluation is expensive, and existing methods rely on curated test sets and static metrics that don’t scale or generalize. A more scalable, low-overhead, automatic evaluation pipeline is needed.
AI.method: 1) Automatically synthesize Q&A pairs from the target knowledge base; 2) run the chatbot to produce answers; 3) use an LLM to compare chatbot answers to reference answers; 4) apply confidence-based filtering to surface uncertain cases for human review. The framework is modular and intended to be language-agnostic.
AI.result: On a Vietnamese news dataset, the evaluator shows high agreement with human judgments and substantially reduces the human review workload.
AI.conclusion: An effective, scalable, and adaptable evaluation framework that minimizes manual intervention while maintaining reliability, suitable for diverse domains and languages.

title: BALD-SAM: Disagreement-based Active Prompting in Interactive Segmentation
summary: The Segment Anything Model (SAM) has revolutionized interactive segmentation through spatial prompting. While existing work primarily focuses on automating prompts in various settings, real-world annotation workflows involve iterative refinement where annotators observe model outputs and strategically place prompts to resolve ambiguities. Current pipelines typically rely on the annotator's visual assessment of the predicted mask quality. We postulate that a principled approach for automated interactive prompting is to use a model-derived criterion to identify the most informative region for the next prompt. In this work, we establish active prompting: a spatial active learning approach where locations within images constitute an unlabeled pool and prompts serve as queries to prioritize information-rich regions, increasing the utility of each interaction. We further present BALD-SAM: a principled framework adapting Bayesian Active Learning by Disagreement (BALD) to spatial prompt selection by quantifying epistemic uncertainty. To do so, we freeze the entire model and apply Bayesian uncertainty modeling only to a small learned prediction head, making intractable uncertainty estimation practical for large multi-million parameter foundation models. Across 16 datasets spanning natural, medical, underwater, and seismic domains, BALD-SAM demonstrates strong cross-domain performance, ranking first or second on 14 of 16 benchmarks. We validate these gains through a comprehensive ablation suite covering 3 SAM backbones and 35 Laplace posterior configurations, amounting to 38 distinct ablation settings. Beyond strong average performance, BALD-SAM surpasses human prompting and, in several categories, even oracle prompting, while consistently outperforming one-shot baselines in final segmentation quality, particularly on thin and structurally complex objects.

AI.tldr: They introduce active prompting for SAM: a Bayesian, uncertainty-driven way to place the next interactive prompt, implemented as BALD-SAM, which adds a small Bayesian head (with a Laplace posterior) on top of a frozen SAM to make epistemic uncertainty estimation tractable. It delivers top-tier cross-domain performance and can even beat human and oracle prompting, especially on thin/complex structures.
AI.motivation: Interactive segmentation workflows require iterative, informative prompts, but current automation largely mimics ad‑hoc human visual assessment of masks. A principled criterion is needed to select the most informative next prompt and to make uncertainty estimation feasible for large foundation models like SAM.
AI.method: Define active prompting as spatial active learning where image locations are the unlabeled pool and prompts are queries. Adapt BALD (Bayesian Active Learning by Disagreement) to select spatial prompts by quantifying epistemic uncertainty. Freeze the full SAM, add a small learned prediction head, and use a Laplace-approximated posterior over that head to compute BALD scores and pick the next prompt location. Evaluate across multiple SAM backbones and many Laplace configurations.
AI.result: Across 16 datasets (natural, medical, underwater, seismic), BALD-SAM ranks 1st or 2nd on 14/16 benchmarks, consistently surpasses one-shot baselines, outperforms human prompting, and in some cases even oracle prompting, with pronounced gains on thin and structurally complex objects. A large ablation suite (3 backbones, 35 Laplace settings; 38 total) validates robustness.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-guided active prompting is an effective, practical strategy for interactive segmentation with foundation models. By restricting Bayesian modeling to a small head, they achieve tractable epistemic uncertainty that generalizes across domains and increases the utility of each interaction, often exceeding human and oracle prompt selection.

title: Event-based Photometric Stereo via Rotating Illumination and Per-Pixel Learning
summary: Photometric stereo is a technique for estimating surface normals using images captured under varying illumination. However, conventional frame-based photometric stereo methods are limited in real-world applications due to their reliance on controlled lighting, and susceptibility to ambient illumination. To address these limitations, we propose an event-based photometric stereo system that leverages an event camera, which is effective in scenarios with continuously varying scene radiance and high dynamic range conditions. Our setup employs a single light source moving along a predefined circular trajectory, eliminating the need for multiple synchronized light sources and enabling a more compact and scalable design. We further introduce a lightweight per-pixel multi-layer neural network that directly predicts surface normals from event signals generated by intensity changes as the light source rotates, without system calibration. Experimental results on benchmark datasets and real-world data collected with our data acquisition system demonstrate the effectiveness of our method, achieving a 7.12\% reduction in mean angular error compared to existing event-based photometric stereo methods. In addition, our method demonstrates robustness in regions with sparse event activity, strong ambient illumination, and scenes affected by specularities.

AI.tldr: Event-based photometric stereo using a single moving light and a lightweight per-pixel neural network estimates surface normals without calibration, outperforming prior event-based methods and remaining robust to ambient light, sparse events, and specularities.
AI.motivation: Conventional frame-based photometric stereo needs controlled, synchronized lighting and struggles with ambient illumination and HDR, limiting real-world use. Event cameras naturally handle high dynamics and continuous radiance changes, motivating an event-driven alternative.
AI.method: Use an event camera with a single light source moving on a circular trajectory. Convert the stream of events induced by the rotating light into per-pixel signals. Feed these to a small multi-layer neural network that directly regresses surface normals without explicit system calibration or multiple lights.
AI.result: On benchmarks and newly captured real data, the approach reduces mean angular error by 7.12% versus existing event-based photometric stereo baselines and shows improved performance in challenging regions (sparse events, strong ambient light, specularities).
AI.conclusion: A compact, calibration-free event-based photometric stereo system with a single moving light and a lightweight per-pixel network achieves better accuracy and robustness than prior event-based methods, making photometric stereo more practical for real-world, high-dynamic-range settings.

title: MUNIChus: Multilingual News Image Captioning Benchmark
comment: Accepted to LREC 2026 (The Fifteenth biennial Language Resources and Evaluation Conference)
summary: The goal of news image captioning is to generate captions by integrating news article content with corresponding images, highlighting the relationship between textual context and visual elements. The majority of research on news image captioning focuses on English, primarily because datasets in other languages are scarce. To address this limitation, we create the first multilingual news image captioning benchmark, MUNIChus, comprising 9 languages, including several low-resource languages such as Sinhala and Urdu. We evaluate various state-of-the-art neural news image captioning models on MUNIChus and find that news image captioning remains challenging. We also make MUNIChus publicly available with over 20 models already benchmarked. MUNIChus opens new avenues for further advancements in developing and evaluating multilingual news image captioning models.

AI.tldr: Introduces MUNIChus, the first multilingual news image captioning benchmark covering 9 languages (incl. low‑resource ones), benchmarks 20+ models, and shows the task remains difficult, aiming to spur progress in multilingual settings.
AI.motivation: Most news image captioning work is English‑centric due to scarce non‑English datasets. There is a need to evaluate models that align visual content with article text across multiple languages, including low‑resource ones.
AI.method: Construct a multilingual benchmark (MUNIChus) spanning 9 languages with paired news images and article context; evaluate a broad set of state‑of‑the‑art neural news image captioning models on this dataset; publicly release the benchmark and results.
AI.result: Across the 9 languages, even state‑of‑the‑art models perform suboptimally, indicating that multilingual news image captioning is challenging; over 20 models are benchmarked to establish baselines.
AI.conclusion: MUNIChus provides the first comprehensive multilingual testbed for news image captioning, highlighting current limitations and enabling future development and fair evaluation of multilingual captioning models.

title: On the Reliability of Cue Conflict and Beyond
summary: Understanding how neural networks rely on visual cues offers a human-interpretable view of their internal decision processes. The cue-conflict benchmark has been influential in probing shape-texture preference and in motivating the insight that stronger, human-like shape bias is often associated with improved in-domain performance. However, we find that the current stylization-based instantiation can yield unstable and ambiguous bias estimates. Specifically, stylization may not reliably instantiate perceptually valid and separable cues nor control their relative informativeness, ratio-based bias can obscure absolute cue sensitivity, and restricting evaluation to preselected classes can distort model predictions by ignoring the full decision space. Together, these factors can confound preference with cue validity, cue balance, and recognizability artifacts. We introduce REFINED-BIAS, an integrated dataset and evaluation framework for reliable and interpretable shape-texture bias diagnosis. REFINED-BIAS constructs balanced, human- and model- recognizable cue pairs using explicit definitions of shape and texture, and measures cue-specific sensitivity over the full label space via a ranking-based metric, enabling fairer cross-model comparisons. Across diverse training regimes and architectures, REFINED-BIAS enables fairer cross-model comparison, more faithful diagnosis of shape and texture biases, and clearer empirical conclusions, resolving inconsistencies that prior cue-conflict evaluations could not reliably disambiguate.

AI.tldr: Stylization-based cue-conflict tests can produce misleading estimates of shape vs. texture bias. The paper introduces REFINED-BIAS, a dataset and evaluation framework that builds balanced, perceptually valid shape/texture cues and uses a ranking-based, full-label-space metric to measure cue-specific sensitivity, yielding more reliable, comparable bias diagnoses across models.
AI.motivation: Shape–texture preference is widely used to interpret vision models, with stronger shape bias often linked to better in-domain accuracy. But current stylization-based benchmarks confound preference with cue validity, balance, and recognizability: stylization may not yield separable cues or controlled informativeness; ratio-based scores hide absolute sensitivity; and limiting evaluation to preselected classes ignores the full decision space, distorting predictions.
AI.method: Create an integrated dataset and evaluation protocol (REFINED-BIAS): (1) construct balanced, human- and model-recognizable shape/texture cue pairs using explicit operational definitions; (2) ensure perceptual validity and controlled cue informativeness; (3) evaluate cue-specific sensitivity over the entire label space with a ranking-based metric rather than simple ratios; (4) apply across diverse architectures and training regimes for fair cross-model comparison.
AI.result: REFINED-BIAS yields fairer cross-model comparisons and more faithful diagnoses of shape/texture reliance, resolving inconsistencies that the prior cue-conflict stylization approach could not disambiguate. It clarifies models’ true cue sensitivities across architectures and training setups.
AI.conclusion: Stylization-based cue-conflict can be unstable and ambiguous. REFINED-BIAS offers a reliable, interpretable alternative by using balanced, perceptually valid cues and a full-label-space ranking metric, enabling clearer conclusions about models’ shape and texture biases.

title: CodePercept: Code-Grounded Visual STEM Perception for MLLMs
summary: When MLLMs fail at Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics (STEM) visual reasoning, a fundamental question arises: is it due to perceptual deficiencies or reasoning limitations? Through systematic scaling analysis that independently scales perception and reasoning components, we uncover a critical insight: scaling perception consistently outperforms scaling reasoning. This reveals perception as the true lever limiting current STEM visual reasoning. Motivated by this insight, our work focuses on systematically enhancing the perception capabilities of MLLMs by establishing code as a powerful perceptual medium--executable code provides precise semantics that naturally align with the structured nature of STEM visuals. Specifically, we construct ICC-1M, a large-scale dataset comprising 1M Image-Caption-Code triplets that materializes this code-as-perception paradigm through two complementary approaches: (1) Code-Grounded Caption Generation treats executable code as ground truth for image captions, eliminating the hallucinations inherent in existing knowledge distillation methods; (2) STEM Image-to-Code Translation prompts models to generate reconstruction code, mitigating the ambiguity of natural language for perception enhancement. To validate this paradigm, we further introduce STEM2Code-Eval, a novel benchmark that directly evaluates visual perception in STEM domains. Unlike existing work relying on problem-solving accuracy as a proxy that only measures problem-relevant understanding, our benchmark requires comprehensive visual comprehension through executable code generation for image reconstruction, providing deterministic and verifiable assessment. Code is available at https://github.com/TongkunGuan/Qwen-CodePercept.

AI.tldr: Perception—not reasoning—is the main bottleneck in MLLM STEM visual reasoning; using executable code as a precise perceptual representation and supervision signal (ICC-1M) and a code-based evaluation (STEM2Code-Eval) addresses this gap.
AI.motivation: Disentangle whether MLLM failures on STEM visual tasks are due to weak perception or insufficient reasoning, and replace noisy, proxy supervision/evaluation (e.g., problem-solving accuracy, distillation-induced hallucinations) with precise, verifiable signals.
AI.method: Conduct scaling studies that independently scale perception and reasoning components, revealing perception as the limiting factor. Propose code-as-perception: (1) Code-Grounded Caption Generation that treats executable code as ground-truth semantics for captions; (2) STEM Image-to-Code Translation that trains models to reconstruct visuals via code. Build ICC-1M (1M Image–Caption–Code triplets) and introduce STEM2Code-Eval, a benchmark requiring executable code generation for image reconstruction to deterministically assess perception.
AI.result: Scaling perception consistently outperforms scaling reasoning on STEM visual reasoning tasks, identifying perception as the key lever. ICC-1M operationalizes code-as-perception and, together with STEM2Code-Eval, enables direct, verifiable measurement and improvement of visual perception. (Abstract does not report specific metrics.)
AI.conclusion: Improving perception—via executable code as a structured, unambiguous medium—is the most effective path to advance STEM visual reasoning in MLLMs. The released dataset and benchmark provide principled training and evaluation tools to realize this paradigm.

title: Disentangling Similarity and Relatedness in Topic Models
summary: The recent advancement of large language models has spurred a growing trend of integrating pre-trained language model (PLM) embeddings into topic models, fundamentally reshaping how topics capture semantic structure. Classical models such as Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) derive topics from word co-occurrence statistics, whereas PLM-augmented models anchor these statistics to pre-trained embedding spaces, imposing a prior that also favours clustering of semantically similar words. This structural difference can be captured by the psycholinguistic dimensions of thematic relatedness and taxonomic similarity of the topic words. To disentangle these dimensions in topic models, we construct a large synthetic benchmark of word pairs using LLM-based annotation to train a neural scoring function. We apply this scorer to a comprehensive evaluation across multiple corpora and topic model families, revealing that different model families capture distinct semantic structure in their topics. We further demonstrate that similarity and relatedness scores successfully predict downstream task performance depending on task requirements. This paper establishes similarity and relatedness as essential axes for topic model evaluation and provides a reliable pipeline for characterising these across model families and corpora.

AI.tldr: They build an LLM-annotated benchmark and a neural scorer to disentangle taxonomic similarity vs thematic relatedness in topic words, showing different topic-model families emphasize different axes and that these axes predict downstream performance—proposing them as core evaluation dimensions with a practical pipeline.
AI.motivation: Classical topic models (e.g., LDA) rely on word co-occurrence, while PLM-augmented models anchor topics in pre-trained embedding spaces that bias toward clustering semantically similar words. The field lacks a clear, quantitative way to separate and evaluate two key semantic dimensions—taxonomic similarity and thematic relatedness—within topics, which hampers understanding model behavior and selecting models for tasks.
AI.method: Create a large synthetic benchmark of word pairs annotated via LLMs for similarity vs relatedness; train a neural scoring function on this benchmark; apply the scorer to topics produced by multiple topic-model families across diverse corpora; quantify how much each model captures similarity vs relatedness; test whether these scores correlate with downstream task performance under different task requirements.
AI.result: The evaluation shows that model families differ systematically in the semantic structure their topics encode (e.g., PLM-anchored models skew toward taxonomic similarity, classic co-occurrence models capture more thematic relatedness). Moreover, the derived similarity/relatedness scores reliably predict downstream performance in line with task needs.
AI.conclusion: Similarity and relatedness are essential, complementary axes for evaluating topic models. The paper delivers an effective, reproducible pipeline—LLM-based annotation plus a neural scorer—to characterize these axes across model families and corpora, and demonstrates their practical value through predictive links to downstream outcomes.

title: An Extreme Multi-label Text Classification (XMTC) Library Dataset: What if we took "Use of Practical AI in Digital Libraries" seriously?
comment: 9 pages, 5 figures. Accepted to appear in the Proceedings of the Fifteenth Language Resources and Evaluation Conference (LREC 2026)
summary: Subject indexing is vital for discovery but hard to sustain at scale and across languages. We release a large bilingual (English/German) corpus of catalog records annotated with the Integrated Authority File (GND), plus a machine-actionable GND taxonomy. The resource enables ontology-aware multi-label classification, mapping text to authority terms, and agent-assisted cataloging with reproducible, authority-grounded evaluation. We provide a brief statistical profile and qualitative error analyses of three systems. We invite the community to assess not only accuracy but usefulness and transparency, toward authority-anchored AI co-pilots that amplify catalogers' work.

AI.tldr: Releases a bilingual (English/German) corpus of catalog records annotated with GND plus a machine-actionable GND taxonomy, enabling ontology-aware multi-label classification and authority-grounded evaluation; includes dataset profile and qualitative error analyses of three systems, aiming for transparent, useful AI copilots for catalogers.
AI.motivation: Subject indexing underpins discovery but is difficult to scale and sustain across languages; current systems lack standardized, authority-linked resources and evaluations to build reliable, multilingual indexing support.
AI.method: Construct and release a large EN/DE corpus labeled with GND authority terms and a computable GND taxonomy; define tasks for ontology-aware multi-label text-to-authority mapping; provide a statistical overview and conduct qualitative error analyses on three systems to illuminate strengths/weaknesses.
AI.result: A public resource (corpus + taxonomy) for authority-anchored classification is made available, along with baseline qualitative insights from three systems and descriptive statistics of the dataset.
AI.conclusion: Community is encouraged to evaluate not only accuracy but also usefulness and transparency, moving toward authority-anchored AI copilots that augment catalogers’ work with reproducible, trustworthy methods.

title: Guiding Diffusion Models with Semantically Degraded Conditions
summary: Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) is a cornerstone of modern text-to-image models, yet its reliance on a semantically vacuous null prompt ($\varnothing$) generates a guidance signal prone to geometric entanglement. This is a key factor limiting its precision, leading to well-documented failures in complex compositional tasks. We propose Condition-Degradation Guidance (CDG), a novel paradigm that replaces the null prompt with a strategically degraded condition, $\boldsymbol{c}_{\text{deg}}$. This reframes guidance from a coarse "good vs. null" contrast to a more refined "good vs. almost good" discrimination, thereby compelling the model to capture fine-grained semantic distinctions. We find that tokens in transformer text encoders split into two functional roles: content tokens encoding object semantics, and context-aggregating tokens capturing global context. By selectively degrading only the former, CDG constructs $\boldsymbol{c}_{\text{deg}}$ without external models or training. Validated across diverse architectures including Stable Diffusion 3, FLUX, and Qwen-Image, CDG markedly improves compositional accuracy and text-image alignment. As a lightweight, plug-and-play module, it achieves this with negligible computational overhead. Our work challenges the reliance on static, information-sparse negative samples and establishes a new principle for diffusion guidance: the construction of adaptive, semantically-aware negative samples is critical to achieving precise semantic control. Code is available at https://github.com/Ming-321/Classifier-Degradation-Guidance.

AI.tldr: CDG replaces the null prompt in classifier-free guidance with a deliberately degraded version of the original condition, yielding a stronger, semantically aware guidance signal that improves compositional fidelity in text-to-image diffusion models with negligible overhead.
AI.motivation: CFG’s negative branch uses a null prompt that lacks semantics, causing geometric entanglement and limiting precision, particularly in complex compositional prompts. The authors aim to provide a richer, task-relevant negative signal to enhance fine-grained control without retraining or external models.
AI.method: Identify two functional token roles in transformer text encoders: content tokens (object semantics) and context-aggregating tokens (global context). Construct a degraded condition c_deg by selectively degrading only content tokens, keeping context tokens intact. Use c_deg in place of the null prompt during guidance, reframing the contrast from “good vs. null” to “good vs. almost good.” Implemented as a lightweight, plug-and-play module compatible with common architectures and requiring no additional training.
AI.result: Across Stable Diffusion 3, FLUX, and Qwen-Image, CDG reportedly boosts compositional accuracy and text–image alignment. Improvements are achieved with negligible computational overhead and without auxiliary models. The approach generalizes across architectures.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, semantically aware negative samples are more effective than static, information-sparse null prompts for diffusion guidance. CDG offers a practical, architecture-agnostic way to increase semantic control and compositional precision in text-to-image generation.

title: Making Bielik LLM Reason (Better): A Field Report
summary: This paper presents a research program dedicated to evaluating and advancing the reasoning capabilities of Bielik, a Polish large language model. The study describes a number of stages of work: initial benchmarking and creation of evaluation methodology, analyzing of comparative results with other LLMs and outlining of future prospects that take into account the limitations of the analyses conducted so far and aims to keep Bielik in the race give the ever-changing -- and competitive -- AI landscape.

AI.tldr: A research agenda to rigorously evaluate and improve the reasoning abilities of Bielik, a Polish LLM, via benchmarking, a dedicated evaluation methodology, comparative analysis against other models, and a roadmap addressing current limitations to keep Bielik competitive.
AI.motivation: Reasoning quality is a key differentiator among LLMs. For a Polish-language model, there is a need for tailored evaluation to understand strengths/weaknesses, guide targeted improvements, and maintain competitiveness in a rapidly evolving AI landscape.
AI.method: Multi-stage plan: (1) initial benchmarking of Bielik; (2) design of an evaluation methodology for reasoning; (3) comparative analysis versus other LLMs; (4) identification of limitations in current analyses; (5) articulation of future work to close gaps and sustain progress.
AI.result: Establishment of a benchmarking/evaluation framework and reporting of comparative findings positioning Bielik relative to peers; recognition of analysis limitations and areas needing further work. No concrete quantitative results are specified in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Systematic evaluation plus a forward-looking roadmap can drive improvements to Bielik’s reasoning and help it remain competitive; further, more comprehensive analyses are planned to address current limitations.

title: GroundCount: Grounding Vision-Language Models with Object Detection for Mitigating Counting Hallucinations
summary: Vision Language Models (VLMs) exhibit persistent hallucinations in counting tasks, with accuracy substantially lower than other visual reasoning tasks (excluding sentiment). This phenomenon persists even in state-of-the-art reasoning-capable VLMs. Conversely, CNN-based object detection models (ODMs) such as YOLO excel at spatial localization and instance counting with minimal computational overhead. We propose GroundCount, a framework that augments VLMs with explicit spatial grounding from ODMs to mitigate counting hallucinations. In the best case, our prompt-based augmentation strategy achieves 81.3% counting accuracy on the best-performing model (Ovis2.5-2B) - a 6.6pp improvement - while reducing inference time by 22% through elimination of hallucination-driven reasoning loops for stronger models. We conduct comprehensive ablation studies demonstrating that positional encoding is a critical component, being beneficial for stronger models but detrimental for weaker ones. Confidence scores, by contrast, introduce noise for most architectures and their removal improves performance in four of five evaluated models. We further evaluate feature-level fusion architectures, finding that explicit symbolic grounding via structured prompts outperforms implicit feature fusion despite sophisticated cross-attention mechanisms. Our approach yields consistent improvements across four of five evaluated VLM architectures (6.2--7.5pp), with one architecture exhibiting degraded performance due to incompatibility between its iterative reflection mechanisms and structured prompts. These results suggest that counting failures stem from fundamental spatial-semantic integration limitations rather than architecture-specific deficiencies, while highlighting the importance of architectural compatibility in augmentation strategies.

AI.tldr: GroundCount augments vision–language models (VLMs) with detections from object detection models (ODMs) via structured, spatially grounded prompts to curb counting hallucinations, yielding up to 81.3% accuracy (+6.6pp) and 22% faster inference; explicit symbolic grounding outperforms feature-level fusion, with positional encoding helping stronger models but hurting weaker ones, and confidence scores mostly adding noise.
AI.motivation: VLMs struggle with accurate counting due to weak spatial–semantic integration, causing hallucinations and inefficient reasoning loops. In contrast, ODMs (e.g., YOLO) are strong at localization and instance counting with low compute. The work aims to leverage ODM strengths to remedy VLM counting failures.
AI.method: Introduce GroundCount: obtain object detections from an ODM and feed them to a VLM using structured prompts that explicitly encode spatial grounding (e.g., bounding boxes/positions). Evaluate prompt-based augmentation versus feature-level fusion with cross-attention. Perform ablations on positional encoding and confidence scores, and measure effects on accuracy and inference time across five VLM architectures, including Ovis2.5-2B.
AI.result: Prompt-based augmentation achieves 81.3% counting accuracy on Ovis2.5-2B (+6.6 percentage points) and reduces inference time by 22% on stronger models by avoiding hallucination-driven reasoning loops. Improvements of 6.2–7.5pp are observed on four of five VLMs; one model degrades due to incompatibility with its iterative reflection mechanism. Positional encoding benefits stronger VLMs but harms weaker ones; removing detector confidence scores improves four of five models. Structured prompts outperform feature-level fusion despite sophisticated cross-attention.
AI.conclusion: Counting errors in VLMs appear rooted in fundamental spatial–semantic integration limitations rather than idiosyncratic architectures. Explicit, symbolic spatial grounding is an effective, compute-efficient augmentation, but compatibility with a model’s internal reasoning (e.g., reflection loops) is crucial. Confidence scores can introduce noise; positional encodings should be tuned to model strength.

title: Prism-$Δ$: Differential Subspace Steering for Prompt Highlighting in Large Language Models
summary: Prompt highlighting steers a large language model to prioritize user-specified text spans during generation. A key challenge is extracting steering directions that capture the difference between relevant and irrelevant contexts, rather than shared structural patterns common to both. We propose PRISM-$Δ$ (Projection-based Relevance-Informed Steering Method), which decomposes the difference between positive and negative cross-covariance matrices to maximize discriminative energy while eliminating shared directions. Each attention head receives a continuous softplus importance weight, letting weak-but-useful heads contribute at reduced strength. The framework extends naturally to Value representations, capturing content-channel signal that Key-only methods leave unused. Across four benchmarks and five models, PRISM-$Δ$ matches or exceeds the best existing method on 19 of 20 configurations, with relative gains up to +10.6%, while halving the fluency cost of steering. PRISM-$Δ$ also scales to long-context retrieval, outperforming the best existing method by up to +4.8% relative gain. PRISM-$Δ$ is compatible with FlashAttention and adds negligible memory overhead.

AI.tldr: PRISM-Δ is a projection-based steering method that extracts discriminative directions between relevant and irrelevant contexts for prompt highlighting, weights attention heads softly, leverages Value as well as Key representations, and delivers state-of-the-art steering accuracy with lower fluency cost and minimal overhead, scaling to long contexts.
AI.motivation: Prompt highlighting often conflates features shared by relevant and irrelevant spans, leading to weak or noisy steering. The authors aim to isolate truly discriminative signals (not shared structure), exploit Value-side information ignored by Key-only methods, preserve fluency, and maintain efficiency and scalability (e.g., with FlashAttention, long contexts).
AI.method: Compute positive vs. negative cross-covariance between representations tied to relevant and irrelevant spans, then decompose their difference to maximize discriminative energy while canceling shared directions. Apply continuous softplus importance weights per attention head so even weak-but-helpful heads contribute. Extend the projection to Value representations to capture content-channel signal. Integrates cleanly with FlashAttention and adds negligible memory overhead.
AI.result: Across 4 benchmarks and 5 models, PRISM-Δ matches or beats the best prior method in 19/20 settings, yields up to +10.6% relative gains, cuts the fluency penalty roughly in half, and improves long-context retrieval with up to +4.8% relative gains.
AI.conclusion: Discriminative, head-weighted, Key+Value steering via PRISM-Δ provides stronger, more fluent prompt highlighting than prior approaches while remaining efficient and scalable. It offers a practical upgrade path for real-world LLM steering, especially in long-context scenarios.

title: Does AI See like Art Historians? Interpreting How Vision Language Models Recognize Artistic Style
summary: VLMs have become increasingly proficient at a range of computer vision tasks, such as visual question answering and object detection. This includes increasingly strong capabilities in the domain of art, from analyzing artwork to generation of art. In an interdisciplinary collaboration between computer scientists and art historians, we characterize the mechanisms underlying VLMs' ability to predict artistic style and assess the extent to which they align with the criteria art historians use to reason about artistic style. We employ a latent-space decomposition approach to identify concepts that drive art style prediction and conduct quantitative evaluations, causal analysis and assessment by art historians. Our findings indicate that 73% of the extracted concepts are judged by art historians to exhibit a coherent and semantically meaningful visual feature and 90% of concepts used to predict style of a given artwork were judged relevant. In cases where an irrelevant concept was used to successfully predict style, art historians identified possible reasons for its success; for example, the model might "understand" a concept in more formal terms, such as dark/light contrasts.

AI.tldr: They probe how vision-language models (VLMs) predict artistic style using a latent-space decomposition, then compare the discovered concepts with art-historical criteria via quant/causal tests and expert review; most concepts are coherent and relevant, and even some seemingly irrelevant ones succeed due to formal visual cues (e.g., light–dark contrast).
AI.motivation: VLMs show strong performance on art-related tasks, but it is unclear what internal visual concepts they rely on and whether these align with how art historians reason about style. Understanding this improves interpretability, trust, and interdisciplinary dialogue.
AI.method: Decompose VLM latent space to extract style-predictive concepts; evaluate them with quantitative metrics, conduct causal analyses to verify influence on predictions, and obtain judgments from art historians about coherence and relevance.
AI.result: Art historians judged 73% of extracted concepts as coherent and semantically meaningful; 90% of the concepts used to predict the style of a given artwork were deemed relevant. When an irrelevant concept still predicted style correctly, experts could often explain success via formal attributes (e.g., tonal contrast).
AI.conclusion: VLMs’ style-predictive representations substantially overlap with human-understood art-historical features, indicating meaningful internal concepts and partly human-aligned reasoning. The approach enhances interpretability and suggests VLMs often rely on formal visual dimensions, though not all concepts are semantically aligned.

title: Phase-Interface Instance Segmentation as a Visual Sensor for Laboratory Process Monitoring
summary: Reliable visual monitoring of chemical experiments remains challenging in transparent glassware, where weak phase boundaries and optical artifacts degrade conventional segmentation. We formulate laboratory phenomena as the time evolution of phase interfaces and introduce the Chemical Transparent Glasses dataset 2.0 (CTG 2.0), a vessel-aware benchmark with 3,668 images, 23 glassware categories, and five multiphase interface types for phase-interface instance segmentation. Building on YOLO11m-seg, we propose LGA-RCM-YOLO, which combines Local-Global Attention (LGA) for robust semantic representation and a Rectangular Self-Calibration Module (RCM) for boundary refinement of thin, elongated interfaces. On CTG 2.0, the proposed model achieves 84.4% AP@0.5 and 58.43% AP@0.5-0.95, improving over the YOLO11m baseline by 6.42 and 8.75 AP points, respectively, while maintaining near real-time inference (13.67 FPS, RTX 3060). An auxiliary color-attribute head further labels liquid instances as colored or colorless with 98.71% precision and 98.32% recall. Finally, we demonstrate continuous process monitoring in separatory-funnel phase separation and crystallization, showing that phase-interface instance segmentation can serve as a practical visual sensor for laboratory automation.

AI.tldr: They introduce a vessel-aware dataset (CTG 2.0) and a YOLO11m-seg–based model with Local-Global Attention and a Rectangular Self-Calibration Module to robustly segment thin, multiphase interfaces in transparent glassware, achieving strong accuracy (84.4 AP@0.5, 58.43 AP@0.5–0.95) at near real-time speed and demonstrating utility for automated lab monitoring.
AI.motivation: Visual monitoring in transparent glassware is unreliable because phase boundaries are faint, thin/elongated, and confounded by optical artifacts, undermining conventional segmentation. Robust, real-time detection of phase interfaces is needed to automate chemical workflows.
AI.method: Formulate experiments as time-evolving phase interfaces; release CTG 2.0 (3,668 images, 23 vessel categories, five interface types) as a vessel-aware benchmark. Propose LGA-RCM-YOLO, augmenting YOLO11m-seg with Local-Global Attention for stronger semantics and a Rectangular Self-Calibration Module for thin boundary refinement; add an auxiliary head to classify liquid color (colored vs colorless). Demonstrate continuous monitoring in separatory-funnel phase separation and crystallization.
AI.result: On CTG 2.0, the model attains 84.4% AP@0.5 and 58.43% AP@0.5–0.95, improving over YOLO11m by 6.42 and 8.75 AP points. Inference runs at 13.67 FPS on an RTX 3060. The color-attribute head reaches 98.71% precision and 98.32% recall. Process demos show successful continuous tracking of interfaces.
AI.conclusion: Phase-interface instance segmentation with the proposed architecture is accurate and fast enough to act as a practical visual sensor for laboratory automation; the dataset and modules specifically address thin-interface and vessel-awareness challenges, enabling reliable monitoring in transparent glassware.

title: HeartAgent: An Autonomous Agent System for Explainable Differential Diagnosis in Cardiology
summary: Heart diseases remain a leading cause of morbidity and mortality worldwide, necessitating accurate and trustworthy differential diagnosis. However, existing artificial intelligence-based diagnostic methods are often limited by insufficient cardiology knowledge, inadequate support for complex reasoning, and poor interpretability. Here we present HeartAgent, a cardiology-specific agent system designed to support a reliable and explainable differential diagnosis. HeartAgent integrates customized tools and curated data resources and orchestrates multiple specialized sub-agents to perform complex reasoning while generating transparent reasoning trajectories and verifiable supporting references. Evaluated on the MIMIC dataset and a private electronic health records cohort, HeartAgent achieved over 36% and 20% improvements over established comparative methods, in top-3 diagnostic accuracy, respectively. Additionally, clinicians assisted by HeartAgent demonstrated gains of 26.9% in diagnostic accuracy and 22.7% in explanatory quality compared with unaided experts. These results demonstrate that HeartAgent provides reliable, explainable, and clinically actionable decision support for cardiovascular care.

AI.tldr: HeartAgent is a cardiology-focused multi-agent system that integrates tools and curated knowledge to deliver transparent, reference-backed differential diagnoses, markedly improving top-3 diagnostic accuracy and clinician performance over existing methods.
AI.motivation: Heart disease demands accurate, trustworthy differential diagnosis, yet current AI systems lack sufficient cardiology knowledge, struggle with complex clinical reasoning, and offer poor interpretability for clinical adoption.
AI.method: Design a cardiology-specific agent framework that orchestrates multiple specialized sub-agents, integrates customized tools and curated data resources, and produces transparent reasoning trajectories with verifiable supporting references; evaluate on MIMIC and a private EHR cohort.
AI.result: Compared with established baselines, HeartAgent achieved >36% and 20% gains in top-3 diagnostic accuracy on MIMIC and a private cohort, respectively; clinicians aided by HeartAgent improved diagnostic accuracy by 26.9% and explanatory quality by 22.7% versus unaided experts.
AI.conclusion: HeartAgent provides reliable, explainable, and clinically actionable decision support for cardiovascular care, demonstrating substantial performance and interpretability benefits over existing AI approaches.

title: Instruction set for the representation of graphs
summary: We present IsalGraph, a method for representing the structure of any finite, simple graph as a compact string over a nine-character instruction alphabet. The encoding is executed by a small virtual machine comprising a sparse graph, a circular doubly-linked list (CDLL) of graph-node references, and two traversal pointers. Instructions either move a pointer through the CDLL or insert a node or edge into the graph. A key design property is that every string over the alphabet decodes to a valid graph, with no invalid states reachable. A greedy \emph{GraphToString} algorithm encodes any connected graph into a string in time polynomial in the number of nodes; an exhaustive-backtracking variant produces a canonical string by selecting the lexicographically smallest shortest string across all starting nodes and all valid traversal orders. We evaluate the representation on five real-world graph benchmark datasets (IAM Letter LOW/MED/HIGH, LINUX, and AIDS) and show that the Levenshtein distance between IsalGraph strings correlates strongly with graph edit distance (GED). Together, these properties make IsalGraph strings a compact, isomorphism-invariant, and language-model-compatible sequential encoding of graph structure, with direct applications in graph similarity search, graph generation, and graph-conditioned language modelling

AI.tldr: IsalGraph encodes any finite simple graph as a compact string over a 9-symbol instruction alphabet, with every string mapping to a valid graph. A greedy polynomial-time encoder and a backtracking canonicalizer produce strings whose Levenshtein distances correlate strongly with graph edit distance, enabling isomorphism-invariant, LM-compatible graph representations.
AI.motivation: Provide a compact, sequential, isomorphism-invariant representation of graphs that (1) guarantees validity for any generated string, (2) is compatible with language models, and (3) offers a practical proxy for graph similarity to support search, generation, and conditioning tasks where GED is costly.
AI.method: Define a small virtual machine with a sparse graph, a circular doubly linked list of node references, and two traversal pointers. Use a 9-instruction alphabet whose operations either move pointers on the CDLL or insert nodes/edges, ensuring all instruction strings decode to valid graphs. Introduce GraphToString: (a) a greedy polynomial-time encoder for connected graphs; (b) an exhaustive backtracking variant that selects the lexicographically smallest shortest string across all starts/traversals to yield a canonical, isomorphism-invariant encoding.
AI.result: On five benchmark datasets (IAM Letter LOW/MED/HIGH, LINUX, AIDS), Levenshtein distance between IsalGraph strings correlates strongly with graph edit distance. The strings are compact and suitable for sequence models.
AI.conclusion: IsalGraph offers a compact, canonical, and language-model-friendly graph encoding where every string is valid, enabling robust graph similarity search, graph generation, and graph-conditioned language modeling via simple sequence operations like Levenshtein distance.

title: The Quadratic Geometry of Flow Matching: Semantic Granularity Alignment for Text-to-Image Synthesis
summary: In this work, we analyze the optimization dynamics of generative fine-tuning. We observe that under the Flow Matching framework, the standard MSE objective can be formulated as a Quadratic Form governed by a dynamically evolving Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK). This geometric perspective reveals a latent Data Interaction Matrix, where diagonal terms represent independent sample learning and off-diagonal terms encode residual correlation between heterogeneous features. Although standard training implicitly optimizes these cross-term interferences, it does so without explicit control; moreover, the prevailing data-homogeneity assumption may constrain the model's effective capacity. Motivated by this insight, we propose Semantic Granularity Alignment (SGA), using Text-to-Image synthesis as a testbed. SGA engineers targeted interventions in the vector residual field to mitigate gradient conflicts. Evaluations across DiT and U-Net architectures confirm that SGA advances the efficiency-quality trade-off by accelerating convergence and improving structural integrity.

AI.tldr: Recasts flow-matching fine-tuning as a quadratic-form optimization governed by a dynamic NTK, exposing a data interaction matrix whose off-diagonal terms cause cross-sample/feature interference; introduces Semantic Granularity Alignment to reduce gradient conflicts in text-to-image models, yielding faster convergence and better structure on DiT and U-Net.
AI.motivation: Standard generative fine-tuning with an MSE loss implicitly assumes data homogeneity and provides no explicit control over cross-term interactions among heterogeneous samples/features, potentially wasting capacity and slowing/derailing learning. A principled view could identify and mitigate these interferences.
AI.method: Provide a geometric/NTK-based reformulation of the flow-matching objective as a quadratic form, revealing a latent data interaction matrix (diagonal = independent learning, off-diagonal = residual correlations). Using this lens, design Semantic Granularity Alignment (SGA): targeted manipulations in the vector residual field to align semantic scales and reduce gradient conflicts during text-to-image fine-tuning.
AI.result: Across DiT and U-Net backbones, SGA accelerates convergence and improves structural integrity, shifting the efficiency–quality frontier favorably compared to standard training.
AI.conclusion: Controlling off-diagonal interactions in the dynamic NTK can improve generative fine-tuning. SGA operationalizes this by aligning semantic granularity in residuals, demonstrating consistent gains in speed and structure; this suggests broader utility of NTK-informed, interference-aware training strategies.

title: mAceReason-Math: A Dataset of High-Quality Multilingual Math Problems Ready For RLVR
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has been successfully applied to significantly boost the capabilities of pretrained large language models, especially in the math and logic problem domains. However, current research and available training datasets remain English-centric. While mul- tilingual training data and benchmarks have been created in the past, they were not created with RLVR and current model capability in mind, and their level of difficulty is often too low to provide appropriate training signals for current models. To address this gap, we provide mAceReason-Math, a dataset of high-quality translations of challenging math problems sourced from a corpus specifically curated for RLVR (AceReason-Math). We further take specific care to clean and improve our translations, resulting in a coverage of 14 languages with more than 10,000 samples per language. We release the dataset to facilitate multilingual RLVR research and benchmarking in the research community.

AI.tldr: They release mAceReason-Math, a large, high-quality multilingual dataset (14 languages, >10k samples each) of challenging math problems translated from an RLVR-curated corpus to enable multilingual RL with verifiable rewards training and benchmarking.
AI.motivation: RLVR has boosted LLM reasoning in math/logic but remains largely English-centric. Existing multilingual datasets aren’t tailored for RLVR and are too easy for current models, offering weak training signals.
AI.method: Translate the RLVR-focused AceReason-Math corpus into 14 languages; perform careful cleaning and quality improvements to ensure fidelity, difficulty, and suitability for RLVR; package for public release.
AI.result: A multilingual dataset covering 14 languages with over 10,000 high-quality, challenging math problems per language, aligned with RLVR needs, is released to the community.
AI.conclusion: mAceReason-Math fills a key gap by providing difficult, RLVR-appropriate multilingual math problems, supporting stronger multilingual RLVR training and standardized benchmarking.

title: PolGS++: Physically-Guided Polarimetric Gaussian Splatting for Fast Reflective Surface Reconstruction
comment: arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2509.19726
summary: Accurate reconstruction of reflective surfaces remains a fundamental challenge in computer vision, with broad applications in real-time virtual reality and digital content creation. Although 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) enables efficient novel-view rendering with explicit representations, its performance on reflective surfaces still lags behind implicit neural methods, especially in recovering fine geometry and surface normals. To address this gap, we propose PolGS++, a physically-guided polarimetric Gaussian Splatting framework for fast reflective surface reconstruction. Specifically, we integrate a polarized BRDF (pBRDF) model into 3DGS to explicitly decouple diffuse and specular components, providing physically grounded reflectance modeling and stronger geometric cues for reflective surface recovery. Furthermore, we introduce a depth-guided visibility mask acquisition mechanism that enables angle-of-polarization (AoP)-based tangent-space consistency constraints in Gaussian Splatting without costly ray-tracing intersections. This physically guided design improves reconstruction quality and efficiency, requiring only about 10 minutes of training. Extensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets validate the effectiveness of our method.

AI.tldr: PolGS++ integrates a polarized BRDF into 3D Gaussian Splatting and introduces an AoP-guided, depth-based visibility scheme to explicitly separate diffuse/specular components, strengthening geometric cues for reflective surfaces. It achieves fast, accurate reconstruction (≈10 minutes training) on synthetic and real data.
AI.motivation: Explicit 3DGS methods render efficiently but struggle on reflective surfaces—failing to recover fine geometry and normals as well as implicit neural methods. A physics-grounded approach leveraging polarization cues can better disentangle reflectance and improve reconstruction of specular objects.
AI.method: Embed a polarized BRDF (pBRDF) into 3DGS to explicitly decouple diffuse and specular terms, yielding physically consistent reflectance and stronger normal/geometry supervision. Introduce a depth-guided visibility mask to enable angle-of-polarization (AoP)–based tangent-space consistency constraints in Gaussian Splatting without expensive ray–surface intersections.
AI.result: The design improves both quality and efficiency of reflective-surface reconstruction, requiring about 10 minutes of training. Experiments on synthetic and real datasets validate the method’s effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Physically guided, polarization-aware Gaussian Splatting (PolGS++) closes the performance gap of 3DGS on reflective scenes by leveraging pBRDF and efficient AoP constraints, delivering fast, accurate geometry and normal recovery suitable for VR and content creation workflows.

title: Word Recovery in Large Language Models Enables Character-Level Tokenization Robustness
summary: Large language models (LLMs) trained with canonical tokenization exhibit surprising robustness to non-canonical inputs such as character-level tokenization, yet the mechanisms underlying this robustness remain unclear. We study this phenomenon through mechanistic interpretability and identify a core process we term word recovery. We first introduce a decoding-based method to detect word recovery, showing that hidden states reconstruct canonical word-level token identities from character-level inputs. We then provide causal evidence by removing the corresponding subspace from hidden states, which consistently degrades downstream task performance. Finally, we conduct a fine-grained attention analysis and show that in-group attention among characters belonging to the same canonical token is critical for word recovery: masking such attention in early layers substantially reduces both recovery scores and task performance. Together, our findings provide a mechanistic explanation for tokenization robustness and identify word recovery as a key mechanism enabling LLMs to process character-level inputs.

AI.tldr: LLMs trained with standard subword tokenization internally reconstruct canonical word-level tokens from character-level inputs via a mechanism the authors call word recovery, which explains robustness to non-canonical tokenization.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs are trained on canonical subword tokens, they perform surprisingly well when given non-canonical (e.g., character-level) inputs. The paper seeks a mechanistic explanation for this robustness.
AI.method: 1) Introduce a decoding-based probe that reads canonical token identities from hidden states given character-level inputs. 2) Provide causal tests by removing the identified word-recovery subspace from hidden states and measuring downstream performance. 3) Analyze attention, masking in-group attention among characters of the same canonical token—especially in early layers—to assess its role in recovery and task performance.
AI.result: Hidden states reliably reconstruct canonical token identities from character-level inputs. Eliminating the corresponding subspace consistently degrades downstream task performance. Masking in-group character attention in early layers substantially lowers both recovery scores and task accuracy, indicating this attention pattern is critical for word recovery.
AI.conclusion: Tokenization robustness arises from an internal word recovery mechanism: early-layer in-group character attention builds representations aligned to canonical token identities, enabling LLMs to process character-level inputs. Disrupting this mechanism harms both recovery and task performance, offering a mechanistic explanation of robustness.

title: LiTo: Surface Light Field Tokenization
summary: We propose a 3D latent representation that jointly models object geometry and view-dependent appearance. Most prior works focus on either reconstructing 3D geometry or predicting view-independent diffuse appearance, and thus struggle to capture realistic view-dependent effects. Our approach leverages that RGB-depth images provide samples of a surface light field. By encoding random subsamples of this surface light field into a compact set of latent vectors, our model learns to represent both geometry and appearance within a unified 3D latent space. This representation reproduces view-dependent effects such as specular highlights and Fresnel reflections under complex lighting. We further train a latent flow matching model on this representation to learn its distribution conditioned on a single input image, enabling the generation of 3D objects with appearances consistent with the lighting and materials in the input. Experiments show that our approach achieves higher visual quality and better input fidelity than existing methods.

AI.tldr: A unified 3D latent representation learned from RGB‑D surface light field samples captures both geometry and view‑dependent appearance; coupled with a conditional latent flow‑matching prior, it enables single‑image‑conditioned generation of photorealistic 3D objects with realistic specular/Fresnel effects, outperforming prior methods in visual quality and input fidelity.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D methods typically reconstruct geometry or model only diffuse, view‑independent appearance, failing to reproduce realistic view‑dependent effects (specular highlights, Fresnel) under complex lighting. There is a need for a compact representation that jointly encodes geometry and view‑dependent appearance and can be inferred from limited observations.
AI.method: Treat RGB‑D captures as samples of a surface light field. Randomly subsample these observations and encode them into a compact set of latent vectors that constitute a unified 3D latent space for geometry and appearance. Train a latent flow‑matching model on this space to learn a conditional distribution from a single input image, enabling generation of 3D objects consistent with the input’s lighting and materials.
AI.result: The representation reproduces realistic view‑dependent effects (specular, Fresnel) under complex illumination. The conditional flow prior enables single‑image 3D generation whose appearance aligns with input lighting/materials. Experiments report higher visual quality and better fidelity to the input than competing approaches.
AI.conclusion: Jointly encoding geometry and view‑dependent appearance as a 3D latent space, and learning a conditional generative prior with flow matching, yields more faithful and photorealistic 3D reconstructions from minimal input, addressing limitations of diffuse‑only or geometry‑only methods.

title: Backdoor Directions in Vision Transformers
summary: This paper investigates how Backdoor Attacks are represented within Vision Transformers (ViTs). By assuming knowledge of the trigger, we identify a specific ``trigger direction'' in the model's activations that corresponds to the internal representation of the trigger. We confirm the causal role of this linear direction by showing that interventions in both activation and parameter space consistently modulate the model's backdoor behavior across multiple datasets and attack types. Using this direction as a diagnostic tool, we trace how backdoor features are processed across layers. Our analysis reveals distinct qualitative differences: static-patch triggers follow a different internal logic than stealthy, distributed triggers. We further examine the link between backdoors and adversarial attacks, specifically testing whether PGD-based perturbations (de-)activate the identified trigger mechanism. Finally, we propose a data-free, weight-based detection scheme for stealthy-trigger attacks. Our findings show that mechanistic interpretability offers a robust framework for diagnosing and addressing security vulnerabilities in computer vision.

AI.tldr: The authors find a linear “trigger direction” in Vision Transformer activations that causally controls backdoor behavior. By intervening along this direction in activations and parameters, they can turn backdoor effects on/off, trace how triggers propagate across layers, relate them to PGD adversarial perturbations, and propose a data-free, weight-based detector for stealthy triggers.
AI.motivation: Backdoor attacks threaten the reliability of ViTs, yet we lack mechanistic understanding of how trigger features are internally represented and how to detect stealthy triggers without clean data. The paper aims to causally identify backdoor mechanisms and leverage that understanding for diagnosis and detection.
AI.method: Assuming the trigger pattern is known, the authors extract a linear direction in the model’s activation space that corresponds to the trigger’s internal representation. They validate causality by intervening both in activation space (projecting/adding/removing the direction) and parameter space (weight edits) and observing consistent modulation of backdoor behavior across datasets and attack types. They track the direction’s evolution across layers to contrast static-patch versus distributed, stealthy triggers, probe interactions with PGD perturbations, and design a data-free, weight-based detection heuristic for stealthy-trigger attacks.
AI.result: A single linear direction captures the trigger representation, and targeted interventions along it reliably toggle or attenuate the backdoor effect. Static-patch triggers exhibit distinct processing dynamics from distributed stealthy triggers. PGD perturbations can interact with (activate/deactivate) the identified mechanism, linking adversarial and backdoor behaviors. The paper introduces a practical, data-free detection approach operating on weights for stealthy-trigger attacks.
AI.conclusion: Mechanistic interpretability provides a causal handle on backdoor representations in ViTs, enabling diagnostics, comparisons across trigger types, insights into adversarial interactions, and a promising data-free detection path for stealthy backdoors.

title: Large Language Models as Annotators for Machine Translation Quality Estimation
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated excellent performance on Machine Translation Quality Estimation (MTQE), yet their high inference costs make them impractical for direct application. In this work, we propose applying LLMs to generate MQM-style annotations for training a COMET model: following Fernandes et al. (2023), we reckon that segment-level annotations provide a strong rationale for LLMs and are key to good segment-level QE. We propose a simplified MQM scheme, mostly restricted to top-level categories, to guide LLM selection. We present a systematic approach for the development of a GPT-4o-based prompt, called PPbMQM (Prompt-Pattern-based-MQM). We show that the resulting annotations correlate well with human annotations and that training COMET on them leads to competitive performance on segment-level QE for Chinese-English and English-German.

AI.tldr: Use GPT-4o with a simplified MQM scheme to generate segment-level annotations that train a COMET QE model, yielding human-correlated labels and competitive QE for Zh–En and En–De at far lower runtime cost than deploying LLMs directly.
AI.motivation: LLMs excel at machine translation quality estimation but are too costly to run at scale. Segment-level rationales improve QE quality. The goal is to distill LLM judgment into a lightweight, deployable QE model while preserving accuracy.
AI.method: Design a simplified, mostly top-level MQM taxonomy and a structured GPT-4o prompting strategy (PPbMQM) to produce segment-level MQM-style annotations. Use these annotations to train a COMET model and evaluate correlation with human MQM and segment-level QE performance on Chinese–English and English–German.
AI.result: LLM-generated MQM annotations correlate well with human annotations. A COMET model trained on these labels achieves competitive segment-level QE on Zh–En and En–De compared to strong baselines, despite lower inference costs.
AI.conclusion: Prompted LLMs can bootstrap reliable MQM annotations that effectively train smaller QE models, offering a practical, cost-efficient alternative to direct LLM-based QE. Simplified segment-level MQM is sufficient to guide LLMs and transfer quality to COMET; extending to more languages and error granularity is a natural next step.

title: COMIC: Agentic Sketch Comedy Generation
summary: We propose a fully automated AI system that produces short comedic videos similar to sketch shows such as Saturday Night Live. Starting with character references, the system employs a population of agents loosely based on real production studio roles, structured to optimize the quality and diversity of ideas and outputs through iterative competition, evaluation, and improvement. A key contribution is the introduction of LLM critics aligned with real viewer preferences through the analysis of a corpus of comedy videos on YouTube to automatically evaluate humor. Our experiments show that our framework produces results approaching the quality of professionally produced sketches while demonstrating state-of-the-art performance in video generation.

AI.tldr: An automated, studio-style multi‑agent AI system generates short sketch‑comedy videos, using LLM “critics” aligned to real viewer preferences from YouTube data to iteratively select and refine ideas, yielding near–professional quality and state‑of‑the‑art video generation results.
AI.motivation: Automate and scale the creation of high‑quality comedy sketches while tackling two challenges: coordinating diverse creative roles and objectively evaluating humor in a way that reflects actual audience preferences.
AI.method: Deploy a population of specialized agents modeled on production studio roles that generate, compete, and refine ideas in iterative cycles. Introduce LLM critics calibrated to audience tastes via analysis of a large YouTube comedy corpus to score humor and guide selection/improvement. Final outputs are synthesized with advanced video generation models.
AI.result: Experiments indicate the system’s sketches approach professional studio quality and achieve state‑of‑the‑art performance on video generation metrics/benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: A studio‑inspired multi‑agent pipeline combined with audience‑aligned LLM humor critics can automatically produce diverse, high‑quality comedic sketches, suggesting a viable path toward automated entertainment production and robust humor evaluation.

title: HanMoVLM: Large Vision-Language Models for Professional Artistic Painting Evaluation
summary: While Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) demonstrate impressive general visual capabilities, they remain artistically blind and unable to offer professional evaluation of artworks within specific artistic domains like human experts. To bridge this gap, we transform VLMs into experts capable of professional-grade painting evaluation in the Chinese Artistic Domain, which is more abstract and demands extensive artistic training for evaluation. We introduce HanMo-Bench, a new dataset that features authentic auction-grade masterpieces and AI-generated works, grounded in real-world market valuations. To realize the rigorous judgment, we propose the HanMoVLM and construct a Chain-of-Thought (CoT) validated by experts. This CoT guides the model to perform expert-level reasoning: from content identification and Region of Interest (RoI) localization to professional evaluation, guided by both theme-specific evaluation and typical three-tier evaluation in Chinese paintings. Furthermore, we design a reward function to refine the reasoning process of the HanMoVLM to improve the accuracy. We demonstrate that HanMoVLM can serve as a critical backbone for Test-time Scaling in image generation. By acting as a high-quality verifier, HanMoVLM enables generative models to select the most artistically superior outputs from multiple candidates. Experimental results and human studies confirm that the proposed HanMoVLM effectively bridges the gap, achieving a high consistency with professional experts and significantly improving the quality of Chinese Painting generation.

AI.tldr: HanMoVLM is a VLM adapted for expert-level evaluation of Chinese paintings using a new auction-grade dataset (HanMo-Bench), expert-validated chain-of-thought reasoning, and reward-guided refinement; it also serves as a verifier to pick higher-quality generations at test time, aligning closely with professional judgments.
